> **Engineering interpretation:** The block diagrams above map the **functional flow** of the analysis workflow, the **comparison of geometries**, and the **physical velocity progression** that a fluid undergoes when crossing a tube bank. They serve as KTU-board-ready schematic aids.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Differentiate between in-line and staggered arrangement of tubes in a tube bank.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | In-line | Staggered |
|---|---|---|
| Tube alignment | Tubes in successive rows are *directly behind* each other | Tubes in successive rows are *offset by $S_T/2$* |
| Flow path | Straight through the bank | Zigzag through the bank |
| Heat transfer | Lower (wake from upstream tube hits next tube directly) | Higher (flow is forced to accelerate through offset gaps) |
| Pressure drop | Lower | Higher |
| Typical use | Where pumping power is critical | Where thermal performance is critical |

*(1 mark for each correct distinguishing feature, max 3 marks)*

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Define the term "maximum velocity" in a tube bank. Why is it preferred over approach velocity for defining the Reynolds number?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** *Maximum velocity* $V_{max}$ is the highest fluid velocity attained by the fluid as it passes through the narrowest contraction within the tube bank (transverse or diagonal gap, depending on geometry).

**Why preferred (2 marks):**
1. The local convective heat-transfer coefficient depends on the **local flow conditions** at the tube surface, which are governed by $V_{max}$, not the far-field approach velocity $V$.
2. Correlations such as Zhukauskas are derived **experimentally** using $V_{max}$ as the reference velocity; using $V$ directly would yield non-conservative (underestimated) Nusselt numbers.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU Rule:** Module-Internal Choice — Answer **either** Q-A **or** Q-B in full.

---

### Q-A. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**(14 Marks)**

A shell-and-tube heat exchanger has **$N_T = 12$ tubes per row** and **$N_L = 10$ rows** in a **staggered** arrangement. Outer tube diameter $D_o = 30 \text{ mm}$, transverse pitch $S_T = 60 \text{ mm}$, longitudinal pitch $S_L = 45 \text{ mm}$. Hot exhaust gases flow across the bank at approach velocity $V = 6 \text{ m/s}$. Gas properties at mean film temperature $T_f = 480 \text{ K}$: $\rho = 0.745 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $c_p = 1025 \text{ J/kg·K}$, $\mu = 2.59 \times 10^{-5} \text{ kg/m·s}$, $k = 0.0395 \text{ W/m·K}$, $Pr = 0.672$.

Take tube length $L = 1.5 \text{ m}$ and neglect radiation. Calculate:

**(a)** The mean convective heat-transfer coefficient $\overline{h}$ using Zhukauskas correlation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The pressure drop across the tube bank. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q-A

**Step 1: Geometric setup (1 mark)**

$$
S_D = \sqrt{S_L^2 + S_T^2} = \sqrt{(0.045)^2 + (0.060)^2} = \sqrt{0.002025 + 0.0036} = \sqrt{0.005625} = 0.075 \text{ m}
$$

---

**(a) Heat Transfer Coefficient**

**Step 2: Identify governing gap (1 mark)**

Compute the two candidates:

$$
\frac{2(S_D - D_o)}{D_o} = \frac{2(0.075 - 0.030)}{0.030} = \frac{0.090}{0.030} = 3.00
$$

$$
\frac{S_T}{S_T - D_o} = \frac{0.060}{0.060 - 0.030} = \frac{0.060}{0.030} = 2.00
$$

Since $3.00 > 2.00$, the **transverse gap governs → Case 1**.

**Step 3: $V_{max}$ (1 mark)**

$$
V_{max} = \frac{S_T}{S_T - D_o}\, V = \frac{0.060}{0.030} \times 6 = 12.0 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 4: Reynolds number (1 mark)**

$$
Re_D = \frac{\rho V_{max} D_o}{\mu} = \frac{0.745 \times 12.0 \times 0.030}{2.59 \times 10^{-5}} = \frac{0.2682}{2.59 \times 10^{-5}} = 10{,}356
$$

**Step 5: Zhukauskas constants (1 mark)**

For staggered, $1000 < Re_D < 2\times 10^5$:
- $C_1 = 0.33\,(S_T/S_L)^{0.20} = 0.33 \times (60/45)^{0.20} = 0.33 \times (1.333)^{0.20} = 0.33 \times 1.0594 = 0.3496$
- $m = 0.6$
- For $N_L = 10$ → $C_2 = 1.00$

**Step 6: Nusselt number (1 mark)**

$$
\overline{Nu}_D = 0.3496 \times 1.00 \times (10356)^{0.6} \times (0.672)^{0.36}
$$

- $(10356)^{0.6} = 257.1$
- $(0.672)^{0.36} = 0.876$
- Neglecting Pr/Pr_w correction (≈1.0 for gases):

$$
\overline{Nu}_D = 0.3496 \times 257.1 \times 0.876 = 78.7
$$

**Step 7: Heat-transfer coefficient (1 mark)**

$$
\boxed{\overline{h} = \frac{78.7 \times 0.0395}{0.030} = 103.6 \text{ W/m}^2\text{·K}}
$$

---

**(b) Pressure Drop**

**Step 8: Friction factor for staggered (1 mark)**

$$
f = 0.25 + \frac{0.1175}{(S_T/D_o - 1)^{1.08}} = 0.25 + \frac{0.1175}{(60/30 - 1)^{1.08}} = 0.25 + \frac{0.1175}{1.0^{1.08}}
$$

$$
f = 0.25 + 0.1175 = 0.3675
$$

**Step 9: Pressure drop (1 mark)**

$$
\Delta P = N_L \cdot f \cdot \frac{\rho V_{max}^2}{2} = 10 \times 0.3675 \times \frac{0.745 \times (12.0)^2}{2}
$$

$$
\Delta P = 10 \times 0.3675 \times \frac{0.745 \times 144}{2} = 10 \times 0.3675 \times 53.64
$$

$$
\boxed{\Delta P = 197.1 \text{ Pa}}
$$

**Step 10: Final answer summary (5 marks for clarity across all steps)**

---

### Q-B. `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]` — Alternative Choice

**(14 Marks)**

A cross-flow air heater uses an **in-line** tube bank with **$N_L = 6$ rows** and **$N_T = 14$ tubes/row**. Tube data: $D_o = 40 \text{ mm}$, $S_T = 80 \text{ mm}$, $S_L = 80 \text{ mm}$, tube length $L = 2 \text{ m}$. Air at $1\text{ atm}$ approaches the bank at $V = 4 \text{ m/s}$ with mean bulk temperature $300 \text{ K}$. Tube wall at $T_w = 420 \text{ K}$. Air properties at film temperature $T_f = 360 \text{ K}$: $\rho = 0.972 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $c_p = 1009 \text{ J/kg·K}$, $\mu = 2.13 \times 10^{-5} \text{ kg/m·s}$, $k = 0.0310 \text{ W/m·K}$, $Pr = 0.693$.

**(a)** Compute $\overline{h}$ and the total heat transfer rate $Q$ from the bank. Assume the air leaves at $380 \text{ K}$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Calculate the pressure drop across the bank. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q-B

**(a) Heat Transfer Coefficient**

**Step 1: $V_{max}$ for in-line (1 mark)**

$$
V_{max} = \frac{S_T}{S_T - D_o}\,V = \frac{0.080}{0.080 - 0.040} \times 4 = 2.0 \times 4 = 8.0 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 2: Reynolds number (1 mark)**

$$
Re_D = \frac{0.972 \times 8.0 \times 0.040}{2.13 \times 10^{-5}} = \frac{0.3110}{2.13 \times 10^{-5}} = 14{,}605
$$

**Step 3: Zhukauskas constants for in-line, $1000 < Re_D < 2\times 10^5$ (1 mark)**

- $C_1 = 0.31\,(S_T/S_L)^{0.20} = 0.31 \times (1.0)^{0.20} = 0.31$
- $m = 0.6$
- For $N_L = 6$ → $C_2 = 0.95$ (in-line)

**Step 4: Nusselt number (1 mark)**

$$
\overline{Nu}_D = 0.31 \times 0.95 \times (14605)^{0.6} \times (0.693)^{0.36}
$$

- $(14605)^{0.6} = 326.5$
- $(0.693)^{0.36} = 0.884$

$$
\overline{Nu}_D = 0.2945 \times 326.5 \times 0.884 = 85.0
$$

**Step 5: Heat-transfer coefficient (1 mark)**

$$
\boxed{\overline{h} = \frac{85.0 \times 0.0310}{0.040} = 65.9 \text{ W/m}^2\text{·K}}
$$

**Step 6: Heat transfer area and LMTD (1 mark)**

Total external area:

$$
A_o = \pi D_o L N_T N_L = \pi \times 0.040 \times 2 \times 14 \times 6 = 21.11 \text{ m}^2
$$

LMTD (gas side, tube wall approximately isothermal for cross-flow over entire bank):

$$
\Delta T_{lm} = \frac{(T_w - T_{in}) - (T_w - T_{out})}{\ln[(T_w - T_{in})/(T_w - T_{out})]} = \frac{(420-300) - (420-380)}{\ln[(420-300)/(420-380)]}
$$

$$
\Delta T_{lm} = \frac{120 - 40}{\ln(3.0)} = \frac{80}{1.0986} = 72.82 \text{ K}
$$

**Step 7: Heat transfer rate (1 mark)**

$$
\boxed{Q = \overline{h}\, A_o\, \Delta T_{lm} = 65.9 \times 21.11 \times 72.82 \approx 101{,}300 \text{ W} \approx 101.3 \text{ kW}}
$$

---

**(b) Pressure Drop**

**Step 8: Friction factor for in-line (1 mark)**

$$
f = 0.25 + \frac{0.1175}{(S_L/D_o - 1)^{1.08}} = 0.25 + \frac{0.1175}{(80/40 - 1)^{1.08}} = 0.25 + \frac{0.1175}{1.0^{1.08}}
$$

$$
f = 0.3675
$$

**Step 9: Pressure drop (1 mark)**

$$
\Delta P = N_L \cdot f \cdot \frac{\rho V_{max}^2}{2} = 6 \times 0.3675 \times \frac{0.972 \times 64}{2}
$$

$$
\boxed{\Delta P = 6 \times 0.3675 \times 31.10 = 68.6 \text{ Pa}}
$$

**Step 10: Final summary and conclusion (5 marks for presentation and final values)**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark-Loss Points**
>
> 1. **Using $V$ instead of $V_{max}$ in $Re_D$ calculation:** This is the **#1 mistake** in KTU board papers. It causes $Re_D$ to be off by a factor of 1.5–3× and the entire Nusselt number chain collapses. *Loss: 3–4 marks.*
> 2. **Wrong Case selection for staggered bank:** If the case-selection inequality is misapplied, $V_{max}$ is wrong, and **all downstream quantities** are wrong. *Loss: 5–7 marks.*
> 3. **Forgetting the $(Pr/Pr_w)^{1/4}$ correction:** For gases, this factor is very close to 1.0 and may be **omitted** — but for liquids it matters. State your assumption explicitly.
> 4. **Not applying the $C_2$ correction** for fewer than 10 rows: This costs 1–2 marks. Always state the value of $C_2$ used.
> 5. **Pressure drop formula without $N_L$ multiplier:** Some students write $\Delta P = f \rho V_{max}^2 / 2$ forgetting to multiply by $N_L$. *Loss: 1 mark.*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Things to Remember — Bank of Tubes**

- ✅ **Tube bank** = a group of parallel cylindrical tubes arranged for cross-flow heat exchange.
- ✅ Two arrangements: **In-line** (aligned) and **Staggered** (offset by $S_T/2$).
- ✅ Always use **maximum velocity** $V_{max}$, **never** the approach velocity, in $Re_D$.
- ✅ For **staggered**, check whether transverse gap or diagonal gap is narrower using the inequality $\dfrac{2(S_D - D_o)}{D_o}$ **vs** $\dfrac{S_T}{S_T - D_o}$.
- ✅ For **in-line**, $V_{max} = \dfrac{S_T}{S_T - D_o}\,V$ always.
- ✅ **Zhukauskas correlations** are tabulated in 4 $Re$ ranges; pick the right row.
- ✅ **Staggered > Inline** in heat transfer (typically 10–30%) but also has higher pressure drop.
- ✅ **$C_2$ correction factor** applies for $N_L < 10$ rows.
- ✅ **Pressure drop** $\Delta P = N_L \cdot f \cdot \rho V_{max}^2 / 2$ — single multiplier, not squared.
- ✅ **Friction factor** uses $S_T$ for staggered, $S_L$ for in-line (Zhukauskas formulation).
- ✅ **Typical $Re_D$ regime** for engineering tube banks: $10^3$–$10^5$ (turbulent).
- ✅ **LMTD** applies on the gas/fluid side for total heat duty: $Q = \overline{h}\, A_o\, \Delta T_{lm}$.
- ✅ **Trade-off principle:** Improving $\overline{h}$ via tighter pitch raises both $Q$ *and* $\Delta P$ — design optimisation balances this.
- ✅ Units must be **SI throughout**: $D_o$ in m, $V$ in m/s, $\rho$ in kg/m³, $\mu$ in kg/m·s.

---

**END OF NOTES — Bank of Tubes (Module 2, PEPET754)**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Internal Flow
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPET754 - HEAT AND MASS TRANSFER > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Internal Flow ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Internal Flow** (also known as **Convective Internal Flow** or **Flow Inside Closed Conduits**) is defined as the forced or natural convection heat transfer that occurs when a fluid flows through a completely bounded geometry such as a circular pipe, rectangular duct, annulus, or any other confined channel. The flow is bounded by solid walls on all sides, and the hydraulic diameter $D_h$ is used as the characteristic length scale to define the **Reynolds number** and **Nusselt number** for the system. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2 – Internal Flow):** > The student must master the concept of hydrodynamic and thermal entry lengths, fully developed velocity and temperature profiles, mean bulk temperature, and the application of the empirical correlations of Sieder-Tate, Dittus-Boelter, and Sieder-Tate for turbulent and laminar internal flow regimes. The governing regime is determined by the **Reynolds number**: $$\text{Re} = \frac{\rho \cdot u_m \cdot D_h}{\mu} = \frac{4 \dot{m}}{\pi D \mu}$$ where $u_m$ is the mean fluid velocity and $D_h$ is the hydraulic diameter. For a circular pipe of internal diameter $D$, $D_h = D$. > [!NOTE] > **Regime Classification for Internal Flow in Pipes:** > - **Laminar Flow:** $\text{Re} < 2300$ > - **Transitional Flow:** $2300 \leq \text{Re} \leq 4000$ > - **Turbulent Flow:** $\text{Re} > 4000$ ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy – The "Garden Hose vs. Fire Hose" Intuition Imagine you are watering a garden using a thin rubber tube. The water flows slowly, the stream is smooth, and you can predict the trajectory. Now switch to a fire hose used by firefighters—the water gushes out violently with chaotic swirls and eddies. Both are examples of internal flow (the fluid is enclosed by the pipe walls), but their heat transfer behavior is dramatically different. **Why does this matter for Heat Transfer?** - In the **garden hose (laminar)**, the fluid moves in neat parallel layers, and heat is transported primarily by **molecular conduction** across streamlines. The thermal resistance is high. - In the **fire hose (turbulent)**, chaotic eddies constantly mix hot and cold fluid parcels, sweeping heat rapidly across the pipe cross-section. The thermal resistance is dramatically reduced. > [!TIP] > **The Universal Engineering Trade-off:** For an engineer designing a heat exchanger, the goal is often to push the flow into the turbulent regime to maximize heat transfer coefficients ($h$), accepting the penalty of higher pumping power. ### 1.3 Why Internal Flow is the "Backbone" of Engineering Internal flow problems form the engineering basis of: - **Power plant condensers** (steam flowing inside tubes cooled by water) - **Refrigeration and air-conditioning evaporators/condensers** - **Chemical process heat exchangers** (shell-and-tube, plate heat exchangers) - **Nuclear reactor coolant channels** - **Microfluidic chips in biomedical devices** All of these systems rely on the precise mathematical description of how heat enters or leaves a fluid as it travels through a confined channel. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Hydrodynamic and Thermal Boundary Layers in Internal Flow When a fluid enters a pipe with a **uniform velocity profile**, the no-slip condition at the wall forces the fluid in contact with the wall to zero velocity. This creates a **velocity boundary layer** that grows downstream. Simultaneously, if the wall temperature differs from the inlet fluid temperature, a **thermal boundary layer** develops and grows. The two boundary layers are distinct but related: - The **hydrodynamic (velocity) boundary layer** thickness $\delta_v$ reaches the pipe centerline at a certain downstream distance called the **hydrodynamic entry length**, $L_h$. - The **thermal boundary layer** thickness $\delta_t$ reaches the centerline at the **thermal entry length**, $L_t$. ### 2.2 Entry Lengths – The "Mixing Zone" Concept > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Examination Favorite:** The entry length formulas are direct "1-mark or 3-mark" questions. Memorize them. **Laminar Flow:** $$L_h = 0.05 \cdot \text{Re} \cdot D \quad \text{(Hydrodynamic Entry Length)}$$ $$L_t = 0.05 \cdot \text{Re} \cdot D \cdot \text{Pr} \quad \text{(Thermal Entry Length)}$$ **Turbulent Flow:** $$L_h = 10 \cdot D$$ $$L_t = 10 \cdot D$$ > [!NOTE] > For laminar flow, if $\text{Pr} \gg 1$ (e.g., oils), $L_t \gg L_h$. If $\text{Pr} \ll 1$ (e.g., liquid metals), $L_t \ll L_h$. For gases, $\text{Pr} \approx 0.7$, so the two are similar in magnitude. ### 2.3 Mean Bulk Temperature – The "Cup-Mixing" Concept The **mean bulk temperature** $T_m$ (also called the **mixing-cup temperature**) is the temperature a fluid parcel would have if it were perfectly mixed at a given cross-section. It is defined as: $$T_m = \frac{\int_A u \cdot \rho \cdot c_p \cdot T \, dA}{\int_A u \cdot \rho \cdot c_p \, T \, dA} = \frac{\sum \dot{m}_i T_i}{\sum \dot{m}_i}$$ For a constant-property fluid with uniform inlet conditions: $$T_{m,o} = T_{m,i} + \frac{q}{\dot{m} c_p}$$ where $q$ is the total heat transferred to the fluid (positive for heating, negative for cooling). ### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Boundary Condition** | **Units** | |---|---|---|---| | Reynolds Number (Pipe) | $\text{Re} = \dfrac{4 \dot{m}}{\pi D \mu}$ | — | dimensionless | | Hydraulic Diameter | $D_h = \dfrac{4 A_c}{P}$ | Non-circular ducts | m | | Prandtl Number | $\text{Pr} = \dfrac{\mu c_p}{k}$ | Fluid property | dimensionless | | Nusselt Number | $\text{Nu} = \dfrac{h D}{k}$ | — | dimensionless | | Hydrodynamic Entry Length (Laminar) | $L_h = 0.05 \, \text{Re} \, D$ | $\text{Re} < 2300$ | m | | Thermal Entry Length (Laminar) | $L_t = 0.05 \, \text{Re} \, D \, \text{Pr}$ | $\text{Re} < 2300$ | m | | Entry Length (Turbulent) | $L_h = L_t = 10D$ | $\text{Re} > 4000$ | m | | Mean Nusselt Number – Fully Developed Laminar, Const $T_s$ | $\text{Nu}_D = 3.66$ | $L \gg L_t$, $L \gg L_h$ | — | | Mean Nusselt Number – Fully Developed Laminar, Const $q''$ | $\text{Nu}_D = 4.36$ | $L \gg L_t$, $L \gg L_h$ | — | | Dittus-Boelter Correlation (Turbulent) | $\text{Nu}_D = 0.023 \, \text{Re}_D^{0.8} \, \text{Pr}^n$ | $n = 0.4$ (heating), $n = 0.3$ (cooling) | — | | Sieder-Tate Correlation (Turbulent) | $\text{Nu}_D = 0.027 \, \text{Re}_D^{0.8} \, \text{Pr}^{1/3} \left(\dfrac{\mu}{\mu_s}\right)^{0.14}$ | Includes viscosity correction | — | | Heat Transfer Rate | $q = \dot{m} c_p (T_{m,o} - T_{m,i})$ | First Law of Thermodynamics | W | | Log Mean Temperature Difference | $\Delta T_{lm} = \dfrac{\Delta T_o - \Delta T_i}{\ln(\Delta T_o / \Delta T_i)}$ | Counter/Co-current flow | K or °C | ### 2.5 Why These Correlations Matter – Engineering Utility The **Dittus-Boelter** equation is the workhorse of the chemical and power industries for **designing shell-and-tube heat exchangers**. The viscosity correction exponent of **0.14** in the Sieder-Tate equation accounts for the large viscosity variation near the wall, which is critical when heating viscous oils. The constant values of **3.66** and **4.36** for fully developed laminar flow are the result of the exact solution of the **Navier-Stokes and energy equations** (parabolic velocity profile assumption). ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of the Energy Balance for Internal Flow Consider a control volume of length $dx$ on a pipe. The fluid enters at $x$ with bulk mean temperature $T_m(x)$ and leaves at $x+dx$ with $T_m(x) + dT_m$. **Step 1:** Apply the First Law of Thermodynamics to the differential control volume. $$dE_{cv}/dt = \dot{Q}_{in} - \dot{W}_{out} + \sum \dot{m}_{in} h_{in} - \sum \dot{m}_{out} h_{out}$$ **Step 2:** For steady flow with no shaft work, neglecting kinetic and potential energy changes: $$0 = dq - \dot{m} \, d h$$ **Step 3:** Substitute $dh = c_p \, dT_m$ (assuming constant specific heat): $$0 = dq - \dot{m} c_p \, dT_m$$ **Step 4:** The heat transferred to the fluid through the pipe wall over the elemental length $dx$ is: $$dq = h \cdot (\pi D) \cdot (T_s - T_m) \, dx$$ **Step 5:** Substituting and rearranging: $$\dot{m} c_p \, dT_m = h \pi D (T_s - T_m) \, dx$$ **Step 6:** Separate variables to obtain the classic ODE: $$\frac{dT_m}{T_s - T_m} = \frac{h \pi D}{\dot{m} c_p} \, dx$$ **Step 7:** Integrating from $x=0$ to $x=L$: $$\ln \frac{T_s - T_{m,o}}{T_s - T_{m,i}} = -\frac{h \pi D L}{\dot{m} c_p} = -\frac{h A_s}{\dot{m} c_p}$$ **Step 8:** Exponentiate to obtain the temperature distribution along the pipe: $$\frac{T_s - T_{m,o}}{T_s - T_{m,i}} = \exp\left(-\frac{h A_s}{\dot{m} c_p}\right)$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > This exponential decay is the **thermal signature of internal flow with constant surface temperature** and is the foundation of the **LMTD method** for heat exchanger analysis. ### 3.2 Analytical Derivation: LMTD for Constant Wall Temperature **Step 1:** Start from the energy balance for a finite length: $$q = \dot{m} c_p (T_{m,o} - T_{m,i})$$ **Step 2:** Newton's law of cooling for the entire pipe: $$q = h A_s \Delta T_{lm}$$ **Step 3:** For constant $T_s$, the temperature difference varies exponentially. The local temperature difference is: $$\Delta T(x) = T_s - T_m(x)$$ **Step 4:** The bulk mean temperature varies as: $$T_m(x) = T_s - (T_s - T_{m,i}) \exp\left(-\frac{h \pi D x}{\dot{m} c_p}\right)$$ **Step 5:** Therefore the local temperature difference is: $$\Delta T(x) = (T_s - T_{m,i}) \exp\left(-\frac{h \pi D x}{\dot{m} c_p}\right)$$ **Step 6:** The mean (logarithmic mean) temperature difference is: $$\Delta T_{lm} = \frac{\Delta T_i - \Delta T_o}{\ln(\Delta T_i / \Delta T_o)}$$ where $\Delta T_i = T_s - T_{m,i}$ and $\Delta T_o = T_s - T_{m,o}$. > [!NOTE] > The LMTD is used only when the **overall heat transfer coefficient $U$** is approximately constant along the length. For phase-change processes (boiling/condensation), the LMTD method may be replaced by the **Effectiveness-NTU method**. ### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example – A KTU-Style Problem **Problem:** Water flows through a pipe of inner diameter $D = 25 \text{ mm}$ with mean velocity $u_m = 2 \text{ m/s}$. The water enters at $T_{m,i} = 20°\text{C}$ and is heated by a constant wall temperature $T_s = 80°\text{C}$. The pipe length is $L = 3 \text{ m}$. Determine: 1. The flow regime. 2. The mean outlet temperature. 3. The total heat transfer rate. **Given Properties of Water (mean film temperature ~50°C):** $\rho = 988 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $\mu = 5.47 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kg/(m·s)}$, $k = 0.643 \text{ W/(m·K)}$, $c_p = 4181 \text{ J/(kg·K)}$, $\text{Pr} = 3.56$. **Step 1 – Reynolds Number:** $$\text{Re} = \frac{\rho u_m D}{\mu} = \frac{988 \times 2 \times 0.025}{5.47 \times 10^{-4}} = 90{,}310$$ Since $\text{Re} > 4000$, the flow is **turbulent**. **Step 2 – Use Dittus-Boelter Correlation (heating, so n = 0.4):** $$\text{Nu}_D = 0.023 \, \text{Re}_D^{0.8} \, \text{Pr}^{0.4}$$ $$\text{Nu}_D = 0.023 \times (90{,}310)^{0.8} \times (3.56)^{0.4}$$ $$\text{Nu}_D = 0.023 \times 9000 \times 1.636 = 338.6$$ **Step 3 – Heat Transfer Coefficient:** $$h = \frac{\text{Nu}_D \cdot k}{D} = \frac{338.6 \times 0.643}{0.025} = 8707 \text{ W/(m}^2\text{·K)}$$ **Step 4 – Mass Flow Rate:** $$\dot{m} = \rho u_m A_c = 988 \times 2 \times \frac{\pi (0.025)^2}{4} = 0.969 \text{ kg/s}$$ **Step 5 – LMTD Calculation:** We need the outlet temperature first to find LMTD. Use the exponential temperature relation: $$\frac{T_s - T_{m,o}}{T_s - T_{m,i}} = \exp\left(-\frac{h \pi D L}{\dot{m} c_p}\right)$$ $$\frac{h \pi D L}{\dot{m} c_p} = \frac{8707 \times \pi \times 0.025 \times 3}{0.969 \times 4181} = 0.505$$ $$T_{m,o} = 80 - (80 - 20) e^{-0.505} = 80 - 60 \times 0.6036 = 43.78°\text{C}$$ **Step 6 – Total Heat Transfer Rate:** $$q = \dot{m} c_p (T_{m,o} - T_{m,i}) = 0.969 \times 4181 \times (43.78 - 20)$$ $$q = 0.969 \times 4181 \times 23.78 = 96{,}348 \text{ W} \approx 96.35 \text{ kW}$$ > [!TIP] > **Self-Check Method:** Calculate $q$ using LMTD: > $\Delta T_i = 60°\text{C}$, $\Delta T_o = 36.22°\text{C}$, $\Delta T_{lm} = (60 - 36.22)/\ln(60/36.22) = 47.27°\text{C}$, $A_s = \pi D L = 0.2356 \text{ m}^2$, $q = h A_s \Delta T_{lm} = 8707 \times 0.2356 \times 47.27 = 96{,}960 \text{ W}$. Close to the value from energy balance (small difference due to rounding). **Both methods must agree.** ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix – Choosing the Right Correlation | **Flow Regime** | **Geometry** | **Boundary Condition** | **Recommended Correlation** | **Validity Range** | |---|---|---|---|---| | Laminar, Fully Developed | Circular Pipe | Const $T_s$ | $\text{Nu}_D = 3.66$ | $\text{Re} < 2300$ | | Laminar, Fully Developed | Circular Pipe | Const $q''$ | $\text{Nu}_D = 4.36$ | $\text{Re} < 2300$ | | Laminar, Thermal Entry | Circular Pipe | Const $T_s$ | $\text{Nu}_D = 3.66 + \dfrac{0.0668 (D/L) \text{Re}_D \text{Pr}}{1 + 0.04 [(D/L) \text{Re}_D \text{Pr}]^{2/3}}$ | $\text{Re} < 2300$ | | Turbulent, Fully Developed | Circular Pipe | Heating/Cooling | Dittus-Boelter | $\text{Re} > 10{,}000$, $0.7 < \text{Pr} < 160$ | | Turbulent, with $\Delta \mu$ | Circular Pipe | Viscous fluids | Sieder-Tate | $0.7 < \text{Pr} < 16{,}700$ | | Turbulent, Liquid Metals | Circular Pipe | Const $T_s$ | $\text{Nu}_D = 4.82 + 0.0185 (\text{Re}_D \text{Pr})^{0.827}$ | $3.6 \times 10^3 < \text{Re}_D \text{Pr} < 9.05 \times 10^6$ | ## 4. The block diagrams correctly map the **physics, governing equations, and decision logic** as required by the KTU 2024 Scheme learning outcomes. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A: 3-Mark Short Answer Questions **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set A]** *Define hydrodynamic entry length and thermal entry length for internal flow in a pipe. How are they related to the Reynolds number and Prandtl number for laminar flow?* **Model Answer:** The **hydrodynamic entry length** $L_h$ is the axial distance from the pipe inlet at which the velocity boundary layer merges at the pipe centerline and the flow becomes fully developed (velocity profile no longer changes with $x$). The **thermal entry length** $L_t$ is the corresponding distance for the thermal boundary layer to reach the centerline, after which the dimensionless temperature profile becomes invariant with $x$. For laminar flow: $$L_h = 0.05 \, \text{Re}_D \cdot D \quad \text{and} \quad L_t = 0.05 \, \text{Re}_D \cdot D \cdot \text{Pr}$$ The thermal entry length is the hydrodynamic entry length multiplied by the Prandtl number. For gases ($\text{Pr} \approx 0.7$), $L_t \approx L_h$. For oils ($\text{Pr} \gg 1$), $L_t \gg L_h$. For liquid metals ($\text{Pr} \ll 1$), $L_t \ll L_h$. **(3 marks: 1 mark for each definition + 1 mark for the relationship)** --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Set B]** *Distinguish between constant heat flux and constant surface temperature boundary conditions for fully developed laminar flow in a pipe. State the corresponding Nusselt numbers.* **Model Answer:** In the **constant surface temperature** condition, the wall temperature $T_s$ is uniform along the pipe length, but the local wall heat flux $q''(x)$ varies with position. In the **constant heat flux** condition, the wall heat flux $q''$ is uniform along the pipe, but the wall temperature $T_s(x)$ varies with $x$. For fully developed laminar flow in a circular pipe: - Constant surface temperature: $\text{Nu}_D = 3.66$ - Constant heat flux: $\text{Nu}_D = 4.36$ The 19% higher Nusselt number in the constant flux case arises because the developing thermal boundary layer is "stretched" in a manner that yields a steeper temperature gradient at the wall. **(3 marks: 1 mark distinction + 1 mark each for the two Nusselt numbers)** --- ### Part B: 14-Mark Module Internal Choice Questions > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** > The most common mistakes students make in this topic are: (1) confusing the **mean bulk temperature** $T_m$ with the **centerline temperature** $T_c$ or the **wall temperature** $T_s$; (2) using the **Dittus-Boelter** correlation in the laminar regime ($\text{Re} < 2300$) and losing full marks; (3) using the wrong value of $n$ (heating 0.4 vs cooling 0.3) in Dittus-Boelter; (4) forgetting to convert **mm to m** in the diameter. Always begin by stating the regime based on $\text{Re}$. --- #### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **(a) [7 Marks]** Air at $1 \text{ atm}$ and $T_{m,i} = 27°\text{C}$ enters a 5-cm-diameter, 8-m-long circular tube with an average velocity of $5 \text{ m/s}$. The tube wall is maintained at a uniform temperature of $T_s = 127°\text{C}$. Estimate: 1. The total heat transfer rate to the air. 2. The mean outlet temperature of the air. 3. The pressure drop across the tube. *Use the following air properties at the mean film temperature $T_f = 77°\text{C}$:* $\rho = 0.9994 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $c_p = 1009 \text{ J/kg·K}$, $\mu = 2.108 \times 10^{-5} \text{ kg/m·s}$, $k = 0.03024 \text{ W/m·K}$, $\text{Pr} = 0.7034$. **Model Solution:** **Step 1 – Reynolds Number:** $$\text{Re}_D = \frac{\rho u_m D}{\mu} = \frac{0.9994 \times 5 \times 0.05}{2.108 \times 10^{-5}} = 11{,}853$$ Since $\text{Re}_D > 10{,}000$, the flow is **turbulent** and Dittus-Boelter is applicable. **Step 2 – Entry Length Check:** $$L_h = 10D = 0.5 \text{ m} \quad \text{and} \quad L = 8 \text{ m} \gg L_h$$ Therefore, the flow is fully developed. **Step 3 – Dittus-Boelter Correlation (heating, $n = 0.4$):** $$\text{Nu}_D = 0.023 \, \text{Re}_D^{0.8} \, \text{Pr}^{0.4}$$ $$\text{Nu}_D = 0.023 \times (11{,}853)^{0.8} \times (0.7034)^{0.4}$$ $$\text{Nu}_D = 0.023 \times 1660.5 \times 0.8806 = 33.62$$ **Step 4 – Heat Transfer Coefficient:** $$h = \frac{\text{Nu}_D \cdot k}{D} = \frac{33.62 \times 0.03024}{0.05} = 20.33 \text{ W/m}^2\text{·K}$$ **Step 5 – Surface Area and Mass Flow Rate:** $$A_s = \pi D L = \pi \times 0.05 \times 8 = 1.2566 \text{ m}^2$$ $$\dot{m} = \rho u_m A_c = 0.9994 \times 5 \times \frac{\pi (0.05)^2}{4} = 0.00981 \text{ kg/s}$$ **Step 6 – LMTD Calculation (assuming unknown $T_{m,o}$ initially):** We use the LMTD method with the help of an iterative/closed form: $$\frac{T_s - T_{m,o}}{T_s - T_{m,i}} = \exp\left(-\frac{h A_s}{\dot{m} c_p}\right)$$ $$\frac{h A_s}{\dot{m} c_p} = \frac{20.33 \times 1.2566}{0.00981 \times 1009} = 2.585$$ $$T_{m,o} = 127 - (127 - 27) e^{-2.585} = 127 - 100 \times 0.0755 = 119.45°\text{C}$$ **Step 7 – Total Heat Transfer Rate:** $$q = \dot{m} c_p (T_{m,o} - T_{m,i}) = 0.00981 \times 1009 \times (119.45 - 27) = 914 \text{ W}$$ **Step 8 – Pressure Drop (using Darcy friction factor):** For turbulent flow, the **Haaland equation** or **Petukhov** correlation gives: $$f = (0.790 \ln \text{Re} - 1.64)^{-2} = (0.790 \times \ln 11{,}853 - 1.64)^{-2} = (7.40 - 1.64)^{-2} = 0.0364$$ $$\Delta P = f \cdot \frac{L}{D} \cdot \frac{\rho u_m^2}{2} = 0.0364 \times \frac{8}{0.05} \times \frac{0.9994 \times 25}{2} = 72.7 \text{ Pa}$$ **Final Answer:** $q = 914 \text{ W}$, $T_{m,o} = 119.45°\text{C}$, $\Delta P = 72.7 \text{ Pa}$. --- **(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the differences between hydrodynamic and thermal boundary layers in internal flow. Using neat sketches (or block schematics), show the variation of local Nusselt number along the pipe length in (i) constant wall temperature and (ii) constant wall heat flux conditions. **Model Answer:** A **hydrodynamic boundary layer** is the region near the wall where viscous effects retard the fluid velocity from the free-stream value down to zero at the wall (no-slip). A **thermal boundary layer** is the region where the fluid temperature deviates from the inlet bulk mean temperature to the wall temperature. Both grow with $x$ and merge at the centerline at the corresponding entry lengths. For **constant surface temperature** $T_s$, the local heat flux $q''_s(x)$ is highest at the inlet and decays exponentially as the bulk mean temperature $T_m$ approaches $T_s$. Therefore, the local Nusselt number $\text{Nu}_x$ is high at the inlet and decreases monotonically to an asymptotic value of 3.66 in fully developed laminar flow. For **constant heat flux** $q''_s$, the wall temperature $T_s(x)$ increases linearly with $x$ as the bulk temperature rises. The local Nusselt number decreases from a high entry value to a constant value of 4.36 in fully developed laminar flow. In **turbulent flow**, the local Nusselt number decreases sharply in the entry region (typically over the first 10 diameters) and then becomes nearly constant. ```mermaid flowchart LR A["A Constant T_s condition
Wall temperature uniform"] --> B["B Nu_x versus x
Starts very high at inlet
Decays to 3.66 asymptotically
Exponential decay shape"] C["C Constant q double prime condition
Heat flux uniform along length"] --> D["D Nu_x versus x
Starts very high at inlet
Decays to 4.36 asymptotically
Steeper decay shape"] B --> E["E Physical reason
Inlet delta T = T_s minus T_m is maximum
Heat transfer driving force is large initially"] D --> F["F Physical reason
Thermal resistance builds up as boundary layer thickens
But fixed q double prime forces linear T_s rise"] ``` **(7 marks: 2 marks for boundary layer explanation, 2 marks for constant $T_s$ profile, 2 marks for constant $q''$ profile, 1 mark for diagram/schematic)** --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Alternative Set]** **(a) [7 Marks]** Engine oil flows through a 50-mm-diameter pipe at an average temperature of $60°\text{C}$ with a velocity of $2 \text{ m/s}$. The pipe is 10 m long. The pipe wall is maintained at $100°\text{C}$. Determine the total heat transfer and the outlet mean temperature using the Sieder-Tate correlation. *Engine oil properties at $60°\text{C}$:* $\rho = 864 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $c_p = 2131 \text{ J/kg·K}$, $\mu = 0.0038 \text{ kg/m·s}$, $k = 0.140 \text{ W/m·K}$, $\text{Pr} = 57.9$. *At wall temperature $100°\text{C}$:* $\mu_s = 0.00304 \text{ kg/m·s}$. **Model Solution:** **Step 1 – Reynolds Number:** $$\text{Re}_D = \frac{\rho u_m D}{\mu} = \frac{864 \times 2 \times 0.05}{0.0038} = 2274$$ Since $\text{Re}_D < 2300$, the flow is **laminar** but very close to transition. We will use the **Sieder-Tate** correlation as specified. **Step 2 – Entry Length Check:** $$L_h = 0.05 \times 2274 \times 0.05 = 5.685 \text{ m}$$ $$L_t = 0.05 \times 2274 \times 0.05 \times 57.9 = 329.2 \text{ m} \quad \text{(>> L)}$$ Since $L_t \gg L$, the flow is in the **thermal entry region**, not fully developed. Sieder-Tate still applies but we treat the correlation value as an average Nusselt number. **Step 3 – Sieder-Tate Correlation:** $$\text{Nu}_D = 0.027 \, \text{Re}_D^{0.8} \, \text{Pr}^{1/3} \left(\frac{\mu}{\mu_s}\right)^{0.14}$$ $$\text{Nu}_D = 0.027 \times (2274)^{0.8} \times (57.9)^{1/3} \times \left(\frac{0.0038}{0.00304}\right)^{0.14}$$ $$\text{Nu}_D = 0.027 \times 487.5 \times 3.853 \times 1.0318 = 52.36$$ **Step 4 – Heat Transfer Coefficient:** $$h = \frac{\text{Nu}_D \cdot k}{D} = \frac{52.36 \times 0.140}{0.05} = 146.6 \text{ W/m}^2\text{·K}$$ **Step 5 – Mass Flow Rate and Surface Area:** $$\dot{m} = 864 \times 2 \times \frac{\pi (0.05)^2}{4} = 3.392 \text{ kg/s}$$ $$A_s = \pi \times 0.05 \times 10 = 1.571 \text{ m}^2$$ **Step 6 – Outlet Temperature:** $$\frac{h A_s}{\dot{m} c_p} = \frac{146.6 \times 1.571}{3.392 \times 2131} = 0.03187$$ $$T_{m,o} = 100 - (100 - 60) e^{-0.03187} = 100 - 40 \times 0.9686 = 61.26°\text{C}$$ **Step 7 – Total Heat Transfer:** $$q = 3.392 \times 2131 \times (61.26 - 60) = 9{,}113 \text{ W} \approx 9.11 \text{ kW}$$ **Final Answer:** $T_{m,o} = 61.26°\text{C}$ and $q = 9.11 \text{ kW}$. --- **(b) [7 Marks]** A concentric tube heat exchanger is to be designed to cool engine oil from $90°\text{C}$ to $60°\text{C}$ using cooling water that enters at $25°\text{C}$ and exits at $40°\text{C}$. The oil flows through the inner tube (25 mm ID) at a rate of $0.1 \text{ kg/s}$, and water flows in the annulus. Using the LMTD method and assuming an overall heat transfer coefficient $U = 500 \text{ W/m}^2\text{·K}$: 1. Identify whether the flow is counter-flow or parallel-flow for maximum LMTD. 2. Calculate the required heat exchanger area. 3. Calculate the LMTD. *Use $c_{p,\text{oil}} = 2131 \text{ J/kg·K}$.* **Model Solution:** **Step 1 – Heat Duty:** $$q = \dot{m}_{\text{oil}} c_{p,\text{oil}} (T_{i,\text{oil}} - T_{o,\text{oil}}) = 0.1 \times 2131 \times (90 - 60) = 6393 \text{ W}$$ **Step 2 – Choice of Flow Arrangement:** For maximum LMTD (and thus minimum area), the **counter-flow** arrangement is chosen because it maintains a more uniform temperature difference along the length. **Step 3 – LMTD Calculation for Counter-Flow:** Inlet oil at $90°\text{C}$ meets outlet water at $40°\text{C}$ → $\Delta T_1 = 50°\text{C}$ Outlet oil at $60°\text{C}$ meets inlet water at $25°\text{C}$ → $\Delta T_2 = 35°\text{C}$ $$\Delta T_{lm} = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)} = \frac{50 - 35}{\ln(50/35)} = \frac{15}{0.3567} = 42.06°\text{C}$$ **Step 4 – Required Heat Exchanger Area:** $$A = \frac{q}{U \cdot \Delta T_{lm}} = \frac{6393}{500 \times 42.06} = 0.304 \text{ m}^2$$ **Step 5 – Verification Using Parallel-Flow (for comparison):** Inlet oil at $90°\text{C}$ meets inlet water at $25°\text{C}$ → $\Delta T_1 = 65°\text{C}$ Outlet oil at $60°\text{C}$ meets outlet water at $40°\text{C}$ → $\Delta T_2 = 20°\text{C}$ $$\Delta T_{lm, \text{parallel}} = \frac{65 - 20}{\ln(65/20)} = \frac{45}{1.178} = 38.20°\text{C}$$ $$A_{\text{parallel}} = \frac{6393}{500 \times 38.20} = 0.335 \text{ m}^2$$ **Final Answer:** Counter-flow gives $A = 0.304 \text{ m}^2$ and $\Delta T_{lm} = 42.06°\text{C}$. This is approximately 10% more compact than the parallel-flow design. --- ### 5.2 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist – Internal Flow** - **Flow regime** is decided by $\text{Re}_D = \rho u_m D / \mu$. Laminar $< 2300$, Turbulent $> 4000$. - **Entry length** for laminar flow: $L_h = 0.05 \, \text{Re} \, D$ and $L_t = 0.05 \, \text{Re} \, D \, \text{Pr}$. For turbulent flow, both are approximately $10D$. - **Fully developed laminar** Nusselt numbers: $\text{Nu}_D = 3.66$ for constant $T_s$, $\text{Nu}_D = 4.36$ for constant $q''$. These are **constants**, not functions of $\text{Re}$ or $\text{Pr}$. - **Dittus-Boelter** is for turbulent flow with $n = 0.4$ (heating) and $n = 0.3$ (cooling). Valid range: $\text{Re} > 10{,}000$, $0.7 < \text{Pr} < 160$. - **Sieder-Tate** is preferred for liquids with significant viscosity variation between bulk and wall. The $\left(\mu/\mu_s\right)^{0.14}$ term is the **viscosity correction**. - **Mean bulk temperature** $T_m$ is a mass-flow-weighted average. The exponential form $\dfrac{T_s - T_{m,o}}{T_s - T_{m,i}} = \exp\left(-\dfrac{h A_s}{\dot{m} c_p}\right)$ is for constant $T_s$. For constant $q''$, the relation becomes $T_{m,o} - T_{m,i} = \dfrac{q'' \pi D L}{\dot{m} c_p}$ (linear in $x$). - **LMTD formula** is $\Delta T_{lm} = \dfrac{\Delta T_o - \Delta T_i}{\ln(\Delta T_o/\Delta T_i)}$; applicable only when $U$ and $c_p$ are constant along the length. - **Energy balance check:** $q$ from $\dot{m} c_p \Delta T_m$ **must equal** $h A_s \Delta T_{lm}$. Use this as a verification step. - **Counter-flow heat exchangers** always yield a higher LMTD (and thus smaller area) than parallel-flow for the same terminal temperatures. - **Property evaluation** is always at the **bulk mean temperature** for $c_p$, $k$, $\rho$, $\mu$, and at the **wall temperature** for $\mu_s$ in Sieder-Tate. - **Always convert units** consistently: mm to m for length, °C or K for temperature (differences are the same, but absolute values differ). > [!TIP] > **Final Exam Strategy:** In every 14-mark question on internal flow, present the solution in this order: (1) Calculate $\text{Re}_D$ and state the flow regime. (2) State the applicable correlation. (3) Show all property values with units. (4) Compute the heat transfer coefficient. (5) Apply the energy balance. (6) Verify with the LMTD method. Examiners reward **systematic approach** and **unit consistency** more than the final numerical answer.

topics.title: Free Convection: Flow Over Vertical Plate
Inclined Plate
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET754 - HEAT AND MASS TRANSFER
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Inclined Plate

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Inclined Plate (Natural Convection on an Inclined Surface):**
> An inclined plate is a flat, planar surface oriented at an angle **φ (phi)** measured from the **vertical** (some texts measure from the horizontal — KTU 2024 standard uses **vertical reference, φ = 0° → vertical, φ = 90° → horizontal facing upward**). When the plate is heated (or cooled) relative to the surrounding quiescent fluid, buoyancy-driven **free (natural) convection** sets in along the plate. The resulting boundary layer flow is governed by a **modified Grashof number** in which gravity is replaced by its component normal to the plate, **g·cos(φ)**.

The two canonical configurations are:
- **Hot plate facing upward (φ near 90°):** Buoyancy lifts the fluid off the surface — a **destabilizing** configuration.
- **Hot plate facing downward (φ near -90° or 180°):** Buoyancy presses the fluid against the surface — a **stabilizing** configuration.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Sliding Heated Skillet"

Imagine a hot tawa (skillet) tilted at an angle from the vertical:
- The air right next to the hot tawa heats up and becomes lighter (less dense).
- The rest of the cooler, heavier air tries to push the warm air **upward, sliding along the tilted surface**.
- The **steeper the tilt away from vertical (larger φ)**, the weaker the "pull" along the surface, because only the component of gravity along the surface does the sliding work. That component is **g·cos(φ)**.
- If the tawa is **nearly vertical (φ → 0°)**, the slide is strong — flow is vigorous.
- If the tawa is **nearly horizontal facing up (φ → 90°)**, the buoyancy acts perpendicular to the surface, and a different (plume-like) regime takes over.

This physical picture is the **intuitive reason** why every Grashof number in inclined-plate convection is modified by a factor of **cos(φ)**.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> - **Characteristic length L** for a vertical plate = plate height; for an inclined plate, KTU convention retains **plate length along the slope L** as the characteristic length.
> - **Angle φ** is measured from the **vertical** (φ = 0° = vertical plate; φ = 90° = horizontal-upward plate; φ = 180° = horizontal-downward plate).
> - **g = 9.81 m/s²** (acceleration due to gravity).
> - **β** = volumetric thermal expansion coefficient [**K⁻¹**].
> - **ν** = kinematic viscosity [**m²/s**]; **α** = thermal diffusivity [**m²/s**]; **Pr = ν/α** (dimensionless).

## 1.4 Why Inclined Plates Matter in Engineering

- **Solar thermal collectors** — flat plate collectors are tilted at the latitude angle.
- **Heat exchangers** on ships, aircraft, and submarines (non-vertical surfaces due to space constraints).
- **Building facades** and window glazing — wind-driven + buoyancy convection on tilted walls.
- **Electronic cooling** — tilted fin arrays.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Physical Setup and Coordinate System

Consider a flat plate of length **L** (measured along the plate from the leading edge), inclined at angle **φ** to the vertical. The wall temperature is **T_s** and the ambient fluid temperature is **T_∞**. The fluid is Newtonian with constant properties.

- **x-coordinate:** along the plate, measured from the lower leading edge.
- **y-coordinate:** normal to the plate, pointing into the fluid.
- **Effective body force along the plate:** $g_x = g \cos\phi$
- **Body force normal to the plate:** $g_y = -g \sin\phi$ (does not drive the along-plate flow)

The **continuity, x-momentum, and energy equations** for the laminar boundary layer on the inclined plate (assuming the same form as vertical plate) are:

$$\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} = 0$$

$$u \frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + v \frac{\partial u}{\partial y} = g \cos\phi \cdot \beta (T_s - T) + \nu \frac{\partial^{2} u}{\partial y^{2}}$$

$$u \frac{\partial T}{\partial x} + v \frac{\partial T}{\partial y} = \alpha \frac{\partial^{2} T}{\partial y^{2}}$$

The only change from the vertical-plate formulation is the appearance of **g·cos(φ)** in place of **g** in the buoyancy term.

## 2.2 Modified Dimensionless Groups

Because only the **g·cos(φ)** component drives the along-plate flow, the relevant Grashof and Rayleigh numbers are:

$$\boxed{Gr_x^{*} = \frac{g \cos\phi \cdot \beta (T_s - T_\infty) \, x^{3}}{\nu^{2}}}$$

$$\boxed{Ra_x^{*} = Gr_x^{*} \cdot Pr = \frac{g \cos\phi \cdot \beta (T_s - T_\infty) \, x^{3}}{\nu \alpha}}$$

$$\boxed{Gr_L^{*} = \frac{g \cos\phi \cdot \beta (T_s - T_\infty) \, L^{3}}{\nu^{2}}, \quad Ra_L^{*} = Gr_L^{*} \cdot Pr}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Convention:** In the 2024 scheme, the asterisk (⋆) or prime symbol on **Gr** and **Ra** denotes the **inclination-corrected (effective)** form. Always replace **g** by **g·cos(φ)** when applying vertical-plate correlations to inclined plates.

## 2.3 Regime Map (Critical for KTU Problems)

| Regime | Range of Ra⋆L | Flow Character | Valid Correlation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Laminar** | $10^{4} < Ra_L^{*} < 10^{9}$ | Smooth, steady boundary layer | **Churchill–Chu (modified)** |
| **Transition** | $10^{9} < Ra_L^{*} < 10^{13}$ | Intermittent turbulence | **Vliet (interpolated)** or Fujii–Imura |
| **Turbulent** | $Ra_L^{*} > 10^{13}$ | Fully turbulent | **Vliet turbulent correlation** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Laminar–Turbulent transition Ra⋆_L ≈ 10⁹** is the **standard KTU cut-off** for an inclined plate. Below this, use laminar correlations; above, switch to turbulent.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Correlation | Regime / Condition | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Gr_L^{*} = \dfrac{g \cos\phi \cdot \beta (T_s - T_\infty) L^{3}}{\nu^{2}}$ | All regimes | Effective Grashof number on inclined plate |
| 2 | $Ra_L^{*} = Gr_L^{*} \cdot Pr$ | All regimes | Effective Rayleigh number |
| 3 | $\bar{Nu}_L = \left\{ 0.825 + \dfrac{0.387\, Ra_L^{1/6}}{\left[1 + (0.492/Pr)^{9/16}\right]^{8/27}} \right\}^{2}$ (Churchill–Chu, **vertical**) | $10^{-1} < Ra < 10^{12}$, laminar-turbulent combined | Use Ra⋆ for inclined plate |
| 4 | $\bar{Nu}_L = 0.68 + \dfrac{0.670 \, (Ra_L^{*})^{1/4}}{\left[1 + (0.492/Pr)^{9/16}\right]^{4/9}}$ (laminar) | $10^{4} < Ra_L^{*} < 10^{9}$ | Simplified Churchill–Chu (laminar) |
| 5 | $\bar{Nu}_L = 0.13 \, (Ra_L^{*})^{1/3}$ (**Vliet turbulent**) | $10^{5} < Ra_L^{*} < 10^{11}$, turbulent | Empirical |
| 6 | $\bar{h} = \dfrac{\bar{Nu}_L \cdot k}{L}$ | All | Mean convection coefficient |
| 7 | $q = \bar{h} A (T_s - T_\infty)$ | All | Heat transfer rate |
| 8 | $\phi$ measured from vertical; $\cos\phi = 1 \Rightarrow$ vertical plate; $\cos\phi = 0 \Rightarrow$ horizontal | Geometry | Standard KTU convention |
| 9 | **Property evaluation:** $T_f = (T_s + T_\infty)/2$ | All | Film temperature for constant-property assumption |
| 10 | Combined: $Nu_L^{n} = Nu_{L,\text{lam}}^{n} + Nu_{L,\text{turb}}^{n}$ with $n = 6$ or $n = 3$ | Transition regime | For interpolation |

> [!WARNING]
> When $\phi = 0$ (vertical plate), $g \cos\phi = g$ and the inclined-plate correlation **reduces exactly to the vertical-plate form**. This is a quick sanity check for KTU numerical problems.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Flat-plate solar collectors:** tilt angle ≈ latitude; correlation gives hourly collector efficiency.
- **Curtain wall façades:** determines U-value of building envelope.
- **Condenser fins** in HVAC: tilted for condensate drainage.
- **Heat sink fins** in compact electronics: orientation matters at low Re, free convection dominates.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Origin of the g·cos(φ) Modification

**Step 1 — Decompose the gravity vector.**
The gravitational acceleration vector **g** acts vertically downward. The plate is tilted at angle **φ** from the vertical. Decompose **g** into components:
- **Parallel to plate surface (along x-axis):** $g_\parallel = g \cos\phi$
- **Perpendicular to plate surface (along y-axis):** $g_\perp = g \sin\phi$

**Step 2 — Identify the driving force.**
Natural convection arises from the buoyancy force per unit volume:
$$\rho g_\parallel \beta (T - T_\infty)$$
Only the **along-plate component** drives the boundary-layer flow because the cross-plate component merely produces a hydrostatic pressure gradient, already absorbed by the pressure term in the boundary-layer momentum equation.

**Step 3 — Write the modified x-momentum equation.**
The classical laminar boundary-layer equation on a vertical plate (Schmidt–Mickelsen) is:
$$u \frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + v \frac{\partial u}{\partial y} = g \beta (T_s - T) + \nu \frac{\partial^{2} u}{\partial y^{2}}$$
For the inclined plate, replace **g** by **g·cos(φ)**:
$$u \frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + v \frac{\partial u}{\partial y} = g \cos\phi \cdot \beta (T_s - T) + \nu \frac{\partial^{2} u}{\partial y^{2}}$$

**Step 4 — Non-dimensionalise.**
Define:
$$X = \frac{x}{L}, \quad Y = \frac{y}{L}\left(\frac{Ra_L}{1}\right)^{1/4}, \quad U = \frac{uL}{\alpha}\left(\frac{1}{Ra_L}\right)^{1/2}, \quad \theta = \frac{T - T_\infty}{T_s - T_\infty}$$
The non-dimensional buoyancy term becomes $\cos\phi$ in the rescaled momentum equation. Thus **all solutions of the vertical-plate problem apply to the inclined plate** provided we replace **g by g·cos(φ)** in **Gr** and **Ra**.

## 3.2 Derivation: Churchill–Chu Correlation for Laminar Flow on Inclined Plate

**Step 1 — Start from the vertical-plate local Nusselt number (Churchill–Chu):**
$$Nu_x = \frac{0.508 \, Ra_x^{1/4}}{\left[1 + (0.492/Pr)^{9/16}\right]^{4/9}}$$

**Step 2 — Integrate from 0 to L** (using $Ra_x = Ra_L (x/L)^3$, so $Ra_x^{1/4} = Ra_L^{1/4}(x/L)^{3/4}$):
$$\bar{Nu}_L = \frac{1}{L}\int_0^L Nu_x \, dx = \frac{0.508 \, Ra_L^{1/4}}{\left[1 + (0.492/Pr)^{9/16}\right]^{4/9}} \int_0^1 (X)^{3/4} dX$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the integral:**
$$\int_0^1 X^{3/4} \, dX = \left[\frac{X^{7/4}}{7/4}\right]_0^1 = \frac{4}{7}$$

**Step 4 — Multiply:**
$$\bar{Nu}_L = \frac{0.508 \times 4}{7} \cdot \frac{Ra_L^{1/4}}{\left[1 + (0.492/Pr)^{9/16}\right]^{4/9}} = \frac{0.290 \, Ra_L^{1/4}}{\left[1 + (0.492/Pr)^{9/16}\right]^{4/9}}$$

**Step 5 — The constant 0.290 is often rounded to 0.67 with the corresponding formula in the alternate integral form** that some textbooks use, and the rigorous KTU board-accepted form is:
$$\bar{Nu}_L = 0.68 + \frac{0.670 \, Ra_L^{1/4}}{\left[1 + (0.492/Pr)^{9/16}\right]^{4/9}}$$
The **0.68** term accounts for the leading-edge conduction contribution which becomes significant at very low Ra.

**Step 6 — Adapt to inclined plate by replacing g with g·cos(φ):**
$$\boxed{\bar{Nu}_L = 0.68 + \frac{0.670 \left(Ra_L^{*}\right)^{1/4}}{\left[1 + (0.492/Pr)^{9/16}\right]^{4/9}}, \quad Ra_L^{*} = Ra_L \cos\phi, \quad 10^4 \le Ra_L^{*} \le 10^9}$$

## 3.3 Derivation: Local Nusselt Number for Inclined Plate

**Step 1 — Start from vertical plate local Nusselt (Churchill–Chu):**
$$Nu_x = \frac{0.508 \, (Ra_x)^{1/4}}{\left[1 + (0.492/Pr)^{9/16}\right]^{4/9}}$$

**Step 2 — Apply inclined-plate correction:**
$$Nu_x = \frac{0.508 \, (Ra_x^{*})^{1/4}}{\left[1 + (0.492/Pr)^{9/16}\right]^{4/9}} = \frac{0.508 \left( \dfrac{g \cos\phi \cdot \beta (T_s - T_\infty) x^{3}}{\nu \alpha} \right)^{1/4}}{\left[1 + (0.492/Pr)^{9/16}\right]^{4/9}}$$

**Step 3 — Local heat transfer coefficient:**
$$h_x = \frac{Nu_x \cdot k}{x}$$

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (Model Solution)

**Problem:** A vertical plate of height 0.5 m at 100 °C is suspended in air at 20 °C. Calculate the convection heat transfer rate per unit width. Properties of air at film temperature 60 °C: k = 0.028 W/m·K, ν = 1.896 × 10⁻⁵ m²/s, Pr = 0.72, β = 1/T_f = 1/333 K⁻¹.

**Step 1 — Compute Ra_L:**
$$Ra_L = \frac{9.81 \times (1/333) \times (100-20) \times (0.5)^{3}}{1.896 \times 10^{-5} \times 2.21 \times 10^{-5}}$$

Numerator:
$$9.81 \times 0.003003 \times 80 \times 0.125 = 0.2946$$

Denominator:
$$1.896 \times 10^{-5} \times 2.21 \times 10^{-5} = 4.190 \times 10^{-10}$$

$$Ra_L = \frac{0.2946}{4.190 \times 10^{-10}} = 7.03 \times 10^{8}$$

**Step 2 — Laminar regime** (Ra < 10⁹), apply Churchill–Chu:
$$A = \left[1 + (0.492/0.72)^{9/16}\right]^{4/9}$$
$$\left(\frac{0.492}{0.72}\right) = 0.6833$$
$$0.6833^{9/16} = 0.6833^{0.5625} \approx 0.7984$$
$$1 + 0.7984 = 1.7984$$
$$1.7984^{4/9} = 1.7984^{0.4444} \approx 1.2974$$

$$Ra_L^{1/4} = (7.03 \times 10^8)^{0.25} = 163.0$$

$$\bar{Nu}_L = 0.68 + \frac{0.670 \times 163.0}{1.2974} = 0.68 + 84.18 = 84.86$$

**Step 3 — Mean convection coefficient:**
$$\bar{h} = \frac{84.86 \times 0.028}{0.5} = 4.752 \ \text{W/m}^2\text{K}$$

**Step 4 — Heat transfer per unit width:**
$$q/w = \bar{h} \cdot L \cdot (T_s - T_\infty) = 4.752 \times 0.5 \times 80 = 190.1 \ \text{W/m}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **If the plate were inclined at φ = 60° from vertical, replace Ra_L with Ra_L·cos(60°) = 7.03 × 10⁸ × 0.5 = 3.515 × 10⁸.** The heat transfer would drop to about 84% of the vertical value.

## 3.5 Transition Regime — Interpolation (Combined Correlation)

For $10^9 < Ra_L^{*} < 10^{13}$, KTU examiners often accept the **Churchill–Chu full-range correlation** (valid up to Ra = 10¹²):
$$\bar{Nu}_L = \left\{ 0.825 + \frac{0.387\, (Ra_L^{*})^{1/6}}{\left[1 + (0.492/Pr)^{9/16}\right]^{8/27}} \right\}^{2}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Inclined Plate Geometry (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["Vertical Reference Axis"] --> B["Inclined Plate Surface"]
    B --> C["Angle phi from Vertical"]
    C --> D{"Orientation of Hot Surface"}
    D -- "phi less than 60 degrees" --> E["Hot Surface facing sideways or upward mild tilt"]
    D -- "phi greater than 60 degrees" --> F["Hot Surface facing upward dominant plume regime"]
    G["Gravity vector g"] --> H["Component along plate g cos phi"]
    G --> I["Component normal to plate g sin phi"]
    H --> J["Drives buoyancy flow along plate"]
    I --> K["Hydrostatic pressure effect only"]
    J --> L["Modified Grashof Gr star equals g cos phi"]
    I --> M["Negligible effect on convection"]
    L --> N["Apply Vertical Plate Correlation"]
    N --> O["Output Nu bar and h bar"]
    P["Plate length L along slope"] --> N
    Q["Plate width W into page"] --> O
    O --> R["Heat Transfer Rate q equals h A delta T"]
```

## 4.2 Inclined-Plate Convection Flowchart (Decision Logic)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    S0["Start: Inclined plate problem with phi, L, Ts, Tinf, fluid"] --> S1["Compute film temperature Tf and fluid properties"]
    S1 --> S2["Compute Gr L star = g cos phi beta delta T L cubed over nu squared"]
    S2 --> S3["Compute Ra L star = Gr L star times Pr"]
    S3 --> S4{"Ra L star in range 10 to power 4 to 10 to power 9"}
    S4 -- "Yes Laminar" --> S5["Use Churchill Chu laminar with Ra L star"]
    S4 -- "No" --> S6{"Ra L star in range 10 to power 9 to 10 to power 13"}
    S6 -- "Yes Transition" --> S7["Use Churchill Chu full range or interpolation"]
    S6 -- "No Turbulent" --> S8["Use Vliet Nu = 0.13 Ra L star to the 1 over 3"]
    S5 --> S9["Compute Nu bar and h bar equals Nu k over L"]
    S7 --> S9
    S8 --> S9
    S9 --> S10["Compute q equals h A delta T"]
```

## 4.3 Component / Variable Interaction Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1["phi"] --> P2["cos phi factor"]
    P2 --> P3["Ra L star"]
    P3 --> P4["Nu bar"]
    P4 --> P5["h bar"]
    P5 --> P6["q"]
    P7["L"] --> P3
    P8["delta T"] --> P3
    P9["fluid properties k, nu, Pr, alpha"] --> P3
    P9 --> P4
    P9 --> P5
    P10["Area A"] --> P6
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define the modified Grashof number for natural convection on an inclined plate. Why is the gravitational acceleration replaced by g·cos(φ)?**

**Model Answer:**

The **modified Grashof number** for an inclined plate is defined as:
$$Gr_L^{*} = \frac{g \cos\phi \cdot \beta (T_s - T_\infty) L^{3}}{\nu^{2}}$$

**Reason for g·cos(φ):** The buoyancy force driving the boundary-layer flow along the plate is only the component of gravity acting **parallel to the plate surface**, which is $g \cos\phi$. The perpendicular component $g \sin\phi$ produces only a hydrostatic pressure variation and does not contribute to the convective motion along the plate. Since the entire mathematical similarity between vertical and inclined plate problems is preserved by replacing g with g·cos(φ), all vertical-plate correlations can be applied to the inclined plate by using this modified Grashof number. **(3 marks: 1 for formula, 2 for explanation)**

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**State the Churchill–Chu correlation for average Nusselt number on a vertical plate. How is it adapted for an inclined plate?**

**Model Answer:**

The Churchill–Chu correlation for **laminar natural convection on a vertical plate** is:
$$\bar{Nu}_L = 0.68 + \frac{0.670 \, Ra_L^{1/4}}{\left[1 + (0.492/Pr)^{9/16}\right]^{4/9}}, \quad 10^{4} \le Ra_L \le 10^{9}$$

**Adaptation for inclined plate:** Replace $Ra_L$ by the modified Rayleigh number $Ra_L^{*} = Ra_L \cos\phi$ (where φ is measured from the vertical). The correlation then reads:
$$\bar{Nu}_L = 0.68 + \frac{0.670 \, (Ra_L^{*})^{1/4}}{\left[1 + (0.492/Pr)^{9/16}\right]^{4/9}}$$
The factor cos(φ) accounts for the reduced effective gravity driving the flow. **(3 marks: 1 for formula, 1 for range, 1 for adaptation)**

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

A flat plate of dimensions 1.0 m (length along slope) × 1.5 m (width) is inclined at **φ = 30° from the vertical**. The plate surface is maintained at **T_s = 80 °C** and exposed to quiescent air at **T_∞ = 25 °C**.

**(a)** Determine whether the flow regime is **laminar, transition, or turbulent**.
**(b)** Calculate the **rate of heat transfer** from the plate to the air.

*Properties of air at film temperature T_f = (80 + 25)/2 = 52.5 °C ≈ 325.5 K:*
- k = 0.02735 W/m·K
- ν = 1.798 × 10⁻⁵ m²/s
- α = 2.141 × 10⁻⁵ m²/s
- Pr = 0.72
- β = 1/T_f = 1/325.5 = 3.073 × 10⁻³ K⁻¹

#### Model Solution

**(a) Regime Identification (7 marks)**

**Step 1:** Compute the modified Rayleigh number $Ra_L^{*}$ using:
$$Ra_L^{*} = \frac{g \cos\phi \cdot \beta (T_s - T_\infty) L^{3}}{\nu \alpha}$$

**Step 2:** Substituting values:
$$\cos(30°) = 0.8660$$
$$g \cos\phi = 9.81 \times 0.8660 = 8.495 \ \text{m/s}^2$$
$$\beta \cdot \Delta T = 3.073 \times 10^{-3} \times (80 - 25) = 0.1690 \ \text{m/s}^2 \cdot \text{K/K}$$
$$L^3 = (1.0)^3 = 1.0 \ \text{m}^3$$
$$\nu \alpha = 1.798 \times 10^{-5} \times 2.141 \times 10^{-5} = 3.850 \times 10^{-10} \ \text{m}^4/\text{s}^2$$

**Step 3:**
$$Ra_L^{*} = \frac{8.495 \times 0.1690 \times 1.0}{3.850 \times 10^{-10}} = \frac{1.4357}{3.850 \times 10^{-10}} = 3.73 \times 10^{9}$$

**Step 4 — Regime identification:** Since $10^9 < Ra_L^{*} = 3.73 \times 10^9 < 10^{13}$, the flow is in the **transitional regime**. KTU board accepts the **Churchill–Chu full-range correlation** (valid up to $Ra = 10^{12}$) for this range.

**(b) Heat Transfer Rate (7 marks)**

**Step 5 — Apply Churchill–Chu full-range correlation:**
$$\bar{Nu}_L = \left\{ 0.825 + \frac{0.387\, (Ra_L^{*})^{1/6}}{\left[1 + (0.492/Pr)^{9/16}\right]^{8/27}} \right\}^{2}$$

**Step 6 — Compute intermediate terms:**
$$(Ra_L^{*})^{1/6} = (3.73 \times 10^9)^{1/6} = 12.51$$
$$(0.492/0.72) = 0.6833$$
$$0.6833^{9/16} = 0.7984$$
$$1 + 0.7984 = 1.7984$$
$$1.7984^{8/27} = 1.7984^{0.2963} \approx 1.2114$$

**Step 7 — Bracket term:**
$$0.825 + \frac{0.387 \times 12.51}{1.2114} = 0.825 + 3.996 = 4.821$$

**Step 8 — Nusselt number:**
$$\bar{Nu}_L = (4.821)^2 = 23.24$$

**Step 9 — Mean heat transfer coefficient:**
$$\bar{h} = \frac{\bar{Nu}_L \cdot k}{L} = \frac{23.24 \times 0.02735}{1.0} = 0.6356 \ \text{W/m}^2\text{K}$$

**Step 10 — Heat transfer rate:**
$$A = L \times W = 1.0 \times 1.5 = 1.5 \ \text{m}^2$$
$$q = \bar{h} \cdot A \cdot (T_s - T_\infty) = 0.6356 \times 1.5 \times (80 - 25) = 52.44 \ \text{W}$$

**Final Answer:** **q ≈ 52.4 W**, regime = **transitional**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

A vertical plate of height **L = 0.6 m** is at **T_s = 60 °C**, placed in air at **T_∞ = 20 °C**. The plate is then tilted to an angle of **φ = 45° from the vertical**.

**(a)** Calculate the **average Nusselt number** and the **mean convection coefficient** for both the vertical and inclined positions.
**(b)** Comment on the **percentage change in heat transfer** between the two orientations.

*Properties of air at film temperature T_f = 40 °C = 313 K:*
- k = 0.0263 W/m·K
- ν = 1.702 × 10⁻⁵ m²/s
- α = 2.306 × 10⁻⁵ m²/s
- Pr = 0.71
- β = 1/313 = 3.195 × 10⁻³ K⁻¹

#### Model Solution

**(a) Nusselt Number and Heat Transfer Coefficient (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Compute Rayleigh numbers:**
For vertical plate (φ = 0°, cos φ = 1):
$$Ra_L = \frac{9.81 \times 3.195 \times 10^{-3} \times 40 \times (0.6)^3}{1.702 \times 10^{-5} \times 2.306 \times 10^{-5}}$$

Numerator: $9.81 \times 3.195 \times 10^{-3} \times 40 \times 0.216 = 0.2707$

Denominator: $1.702 \times 10^{-5} \times 2.306 \times 10^{-5} = 3.925 \times 10^{-10}$

$$Ra_L = \frac{0.2707}{3.925 \times 10^{-10}} = 6.90 \times 10^{8}$$

**Laminar** (since $Ra_L < 10^9$).

**Step 2 — Vertical plate Nu:**
$$A_v = \left[1 + (0.492/0.71)^{9/16}\right]^{4/9}$$
$$(0.492/0.71) = 0.6930, \quad 0.6930^{9/16} = 0.8048$$
$$1 + 0.8048 = 1.8048$$
$$1.8048^{4/9} = 1.8048^{0.4444} = 1.3003$$
$$Ra_L^{1/4} = (6.90 \times 10^8)^{0.25} = 162.1$$
$$\bar{Nu}_{L,v} = 0.68 + \frac{0.670 \times 162.1}{1.3003} = 0.68 + 83.52 = 84.20$$
$$\bar{h}_v = \frac{84.20 \times 0.0263}{0.6} = 3.690 \ \text{W/m}^2\text{K}$$

**Step 3 — Inclined plate (φ = 45°, cos φ = 0.7071):**
$$Ra_L^{*} = Ra_L \cos\phi = 6.90 \times 10^8 \times 0.7071 = 4.88 \times 10^8$$
$$(Ra_L^{*})^{1/4} = (4.88 \times 10^8)^{0.25} = 148.5$$
$$\bar{Nu}_{L,i} = 0.68 + \frac{0.670 \times 148.5}{1.3003} = 0.68 + 76.52 = 77.20$$
$$\bar{h}_i = \frac{77.20 \times 0.0263}{0.6} = 3.384 \ \text{W/m}^2\text{K}$$

**(b) Percentage Change (7 marks)**

**Step 4 — Heat transfer rates (per unit area):**
$$q_v/A = \bar{h}_v \cdot \Delta T = 3.690 \times 40 = 147.6 \ \text{W/m}^2$$
$$q_i/A = \bar{h}_i \cdot \Delta T = 3.384 \times 40 = 135.4 \ \text{W/m}^2$$

**Step 5 — Percentage change:**
$$\%\Delta q = \frac{q_i - q_v}{q_v} \times 100 = \frac{135.4 - 147.6}{147.6} \times 100 = -8.27\%$$

**Final Answer:** Tilting from vertical to 45° **reduces** the heat transfer by approximately **8.3%** because the effective driving gravity component decreases by the factor cos(45°) ≈ 0.707.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Angle convention confusion (most common mistake):** KTU measures φ from the **vertical**, not the horizontal. If the problem says "plate inclined at 60° to the horizontal", you must convert: φ_from_vertical = 90° − 60° = 30°, so cos φ = cos 30° = 0.866. Marks are lost for this conversion.
> 2. **Forgetting to use Ra⋆_L (not Ra_L):** In the inclined case, students often plug in the unmodified Ra_L into the vertical-plate correlation. Always multiply by cos(φ) **first**, then use the correlation.
> 3. **Wrong film temperature:** Use $T_f = (T_s + T_\infty)/2$. Using $T_s$ or $T_\infty$ alone causes property errors and lost marks.
> 4. **Boundary-layer validity:** Churchill–Chu laminar form is valid only for $Ra_L \le 10^9$. For higher Ra, the full-range or Vliet correlation must be used.
> 5. **Units mismatch:** Ra must be **dimensionless**; ensure SI units for all properties. β in K⁻¹, ν and α in m²/s, L in m.
> 6. **Two-sided plate:** If heat transfer is from both sides, multiply q by 2.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ⭐ **Inclined plate φ is measured from VERTICAL** (KTU 2024 standard), not horizontal.
- ⭐ **Modified Grashof & Rayleigh numbers** use $g \cos\phi$ in place of g.
- ⭐ **Vertical-plate correlations are valid for inclined plates** provided $Ra_L$ is replaced by $Ra_L^{*}$.
- ⭐ **Regime cut-off:** Laminar below $Ra_L^{*} = 10^9$, transition up to $10^{13}$, turbulent beyond.
- ⭐ **Churchill–Chu laminar:** $\bar{Nu}_L = 0.68 + \dfrac{0.670 (Ra_L^{*})^{1/4}}{[1 + (0.492/Pr)^{9/16}]^{4/9}}$
- ⭐ **Churchill–Chu full-range (laminar + transition):** $\bar{Nu}_L = \left\{0.825 + \dfrac{0.387 (Ra_L^{*})^{1/6}}{[1 + (0.492/Pr)^{9/16}]^{8/27}}\right\}^{2}$
- ⭐ **Vliet turbulent:** $\bar{Nu}_L = 0.13 (Ra_L^{*})^{1/3}$ for $10^5 < Ra_L^{*} < 10^{11}$ (turbulent regime).
- ⭐ **Heat transfer rate:** $q = \bar{h} A (T_s - T_\infty)$ with $\bar{h} = \bar{Nu}_L k / L$.
- ⭐ **Properties evaluated at film temperature** $T_f = (T_s + T_\infty)/2$.
- ⭐ **Sanity check:** At φ = 0° (vertical), cos φ = 1 and inclined correlation reduces exactly to the vertical-plate form.
- ⭐ **Limiting case:** As φ → 90° (horizontal facing up), cos φ → 0 and inclined-plate correlation **fails**; use horizontal-plate correlations instead.
- ⭐ **Physical meaning of g·cos(φ):** only the along-plate gravity component drives the convective boundary layer.
- ⭐ **Orientation effect:** Hot surface facing up (φ > 60°) → boundary-layer instability, plume-like; hot surface facing down → stabilized, weaker convection.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cylinder and Sphere
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET754 - HEAT AND MASS TRANSFER
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Cylinder and Sphere

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Definition in KTU 2024 Syllabus Context

In **PEPET754 – Heat and Mass Transfer**, Module 2 is dedicated to **Transient (Unsteady-State) Heat Conduction**. The topic "Cylinder and Sphere" specifically addresses the analytical and chart-based determination of time-dependent temperature distributions and total heat transferred inside cylindrical and spherical geometries whose surface temperature (or convection conditions) is suddenly changed. The solutions are built upon one-dimensional Fourier heat-conduction PDEs solved using **separation of variables** and **infinite series** (eigenfunction expansion), and are reduced to compact chart-based lookups called the **Heisler Charts**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
> The cylinder–sphere module explicitly demands mastery of:
> - **Lumped system analysis** (uniform body temperature assumption)
> - **Validity criterion**: Biot number $Bi \le 0.1$ (lumped) and $Bi \ge 100$ (internal resistance controls)
> - **Heisler Charts** for cylinder and sphere (centre-line, mid-plane, and total-heat-transfer charts)
> - **Critical radius of insulation** for cylindrical pipes and spherical vessels

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview (Real-World Analogy)

Imagine a **hot steel ball bearing** dropped into a bucket of cold water. The instant it touches the water, the surface temperature drops sharply, but the *core* of the ball is still hot. Over time, the cold front creeps inward until the entire ball reaches the water temperature.

- **Lumped System** – The ball is so tiny and made of such a conductive metal (high $k$) that the *entire* sphere cools uniformly, almost like a small water droplet that has the same temperature everywhere. There is **no spatial temperature gradient inside**. This happens when $Bi \le 0.1$.
- **Cylinder/Sphere with Internal Resistance** – When the ball is large or the metal is poorly conducting (think of a large frozen chicken leg or a thick-walled steam pipe), the *surface cools quickly* but the *centre stays hot* for a long time. The temperature is **not uniform** — it depends on radial position $r$ and time $t$. This is when we must use **Heisler Charts**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy Summary:**
> - $Bi \le 0.1$ → *Tiny, soupy lump* → One uniform temperature equation.
> - $Bi \ge 100$ → *Thick, sluggish mass* → Surface immediately matches the fluid, core dictates response.
> - $0.1 < Bi < 100$ → *The Real World* → Heisler Charts give graphical precision in seconds.

---

## 1.3 Governing Physical Quantities (Bold Constants)

| Symbol | Quantity | Value / Units |
|---|---|---|
| $T_\infty$ | Fluid / ambient temperature | K or °C |
| $T_i$ | Initial uniform body temperature | K or °C |
| $T_0$ | Surface temperature | K or °C |
| $h$ | Convective heat-transfer coefficient | **W/m²·K** |
| $k$ | Thermal conductivity of solid | **W/m·K** |
| $\rho$ | Density of solid | **kg/m³** |
| $c_p$ | Specific heat capacity | **J/kg·K** |
| $\alpha$ | Thermal diffusivity, $\alpha = k/(\rho c_p)$ | **m²/s** |
| $r$ | Radial coordinate | m |
| $R$ | Outer radius | m |
| $t$ | Time elapsed | s |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Biot Number (Bi) — Decisive Parameter

The Biot number is the **dimensionless ratio of internal conductive resistance to external convective resistance** at the surface:

$$Bi = \frac{h L_c}{k}$$

where $L_c$ is the **characteristic length**:
- Plane wall: $L_c = \text{half-thickness} = L/2$
- **Cylinder: $L_c = R/2$**
- **Sphere: $L_c = R/3$**

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical meaning:** $Bi \ll 1$ means the surface has negligible resistance compared with the interior — the body behaves as a *lumped* system with one uniform temperature. $Bi \gg 1$ means the surface responds instantly and the interior resistance dominates.

---

## 2.2 The Fourier Number (Fo) — Time Dimensionless

$$Fo = \frac{\alpha \, t}{L_c^{\,2}}$$

It scales real time $t$ to the body's thermal diffusion time. As $Fo \to \infty$, the body reaches steady state.

---

## 2.3 Lumped System Analysis — *Uniform Body Temperature*

When $Bi \le 0.1$, the entire solid can be modeled as a single thermal capacitance with uniform temperature $T(t)$:

$$\frac{T(t) - T_\infty}{T_i - T_\infty} = \exp\!\left(-\frac{h A}{\rho V c_p}\, t\right) = \exp(-Bi \cdot Fo)$$

**Time constant (e-folding time)** for cylinder and sphere:

$$\tau = \frac{\rho V c_p}{h A} = \frac{\rho c_p L_c}{h}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The shape of the body enters **only through $L_c$**. The lumped temperature response is identical for a sphere, cylinder, or slab with the same $L_c$.

---

## 2.4 Transient Conduction in Cylinders and Spheres — *Heisler Charts*

When $Bi > 0.1$, the temperature varies with both $r$ and $t$. The transient conduction equation in cylindrical coordinates:

$$\frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial}{\partial r}\!\left(r \frac{\partial T}{\partial r}\right) = \frac{1}{\alpha}\frac{\partial T}{\partial t}$$

and in spherical coordinates:

$$\frac{1}{r^{2}}\frac{\partial}{\partial r}\!\left(r^{2} \frac{\partial T}{\partial r}\right) = \frac{1}{\alpha}\frac{\partial T}{\partial t}$$

are solved with boundary conditions $T(r,0) = T_i$, $T(R,t) = T_\infty$ (or convection at $r = R$). The result is a **one-term approximation**:

$$\frac{T(r,t) - T_\infty}{T_i - T_\infty} = \theta_0^{*}\, J_0\!\left(\zeta_1 \frac{r}{R}\right) \exp\!\left(-\zeta_1^{2}\, Fo\right) \quad \text{(Cylinder)}$$

$$\frac{T(r,t) - T_\infty}{T_i - T_\infty} = \theta_0^{*}\, \frac{\sin\!\left(\zeta_1 r/R\right)}{\zeta_1 r/R} \exp\!\left(-\zeta_1^{2}\, Fo\right) \quad \text{(Sphere)}$$

where $\zeta_1$ is the first root of the transcendental eigenvalue equation.

---

## 2.5 Critical Radius of Insulation (Cylinder and Sphere)

Adding insulation to a pipe *does not* always reduce heat loss. For a cylinder or sphere of outer radius $r_1$ with insulation outer radius $r_2$:

$$Q = \frac{T_i - T_\infty}{\dfrac{\ln(r_2/r_1)}{2\pi k L} + \dfrac{1}{h (2\pi r_2 L)}}$$

$$Q = \frac{T_i - T_\infty}{\dfrac{1}{4\pi k}\!\left(\frac{1}{r_1} - \frac{1}{r_2}\right) + \dfrac{1}{h (4\pi r_2^{2})}}$$

The heat loss $Q$ is **maximum** at the **critical radius**:

$$r_{cr} = \frac{k}{h} \quad \text{(Cylinder)}$$

$$r_{cr} = \frac{2k}{h} \quad \text{(Sphere)}$$

> [!WARNING]
> If $r_2 < r_{cr}$, adding insulation **increases** heat loss — a classic KTU trick question!

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Applies To | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Bi = h L_c / k$ | All shapes | dimensionless |
| 2 | $Fo = \alpha t / L_c^{2}$ | All shapes | dimensionless |
| 3 | $\dfrac{T - T_\infty}{T_i - T_\infty} = e^{-Bi\,Fo}$ | Lumped ($Bi \le 0.1$) | dimensionless |
| 4 | $L_c = R/2$ | Cylinder | m |
| 5 | $L_c = R/3$ | Sphere | m |
| 6 | Time constant $\tau = \rho V c_p / (h A) = \rho c_p L_c / h$ | Lumped | s |
| 7 | Centreline ratio for cylinder: $\theta_0^{*} = f(Bi, Fo)$ from Heisler chart | Cylinder | dimensionless |
| 8 | Centreline ratio for sphere: $\theta_0^{*} = f(Bi, Fo)$ from Heisler chart | Sphere | dimensionless |
| 9 | Total heat ratio: $Q/Q_0 = 1 - 3\theta_0^{*}/(Bi\,Fo)$ | Cylinder | dimensionless |
| 10 | Total heat ratio: $Q/Q_0 = 1 - 3\theta_0^{*}/(Bi\,Fo)$ | Sphere | dimensionless |
| 11 | $r_{cr} = k/h$ | Cylinder | m |
| 12 | $r_{cr} = 2k/h$ | Sphere | m |
| 13 | $Q_{cyl} = 2\pi k L (T_i - T_\infty) / \ln(r_2/r_1)$ | Cylinder, steady | W |
| 14 | $Q_{sph} = 4\pi k (T_i - T_\infty) / [1/r_1 - 1/r_2]$ | Sphere, steady | W |
| 15 | $r_{cr}$ exists only when $h$ is finite; no critical radius for plane wall | General | — |

---

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Cylindrical steam pipes** (insulation design, condensation control)
- **Spherical LPG tanks** (rapid cool-down during refilling)
- **Nuclear fuel pellets** (transient heating during reactor scram)
- **Pharmaceutical glass vials** (sterilisation cycle calculations)
- **Cryogenic storage spheres** (boil-off rate prediction)
- **Underground power cables** (transient ampacity during load surges)

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Lumped System Equation (Cylinder/Sphere)

**Starting point — energy balance on the entire solid:**

Energy entering – Energy leaving = Rate of change of internal energy

$$h A (T_\infty - T) = \rho V c_p \frac{dT}{dt}$$

**Step 1** — Separate variables $T$ and $t$:

$$dt = -\frac{\rho V c_p}{h A}\frac{dT}{T_\infty - T}$$

**Step 2** — Integrate from $t = 0$ (where $T = T_i$) to $t$ (where $T = T(t)$):

$$\int_0^{t} dt = -\frac{\rho V c_p}{h A}\int_{T_i}^{T}\frac{dT}{T_\infty - T}$$

**Step 3** — Evaluate the integral (note sign flip):

$$t = \frac{\rho V c_p}{h A}\bigl[\ln(T_\infty - T) - \ln(T_\infty - T_i)\bigr] = \frac{\rho V c_p}{h A}\ln\!\left(\frac{T - T_\infty}{T_i - T_\infty}\right)$$

**Step 4** — Rearrange to obtain the canonical form:

$$\frac{T(t) - T_\infty}{T_i - T_\infty} = \exp\!\left(-\frac{h A}{\rho V c_p}\, t\right)$$

**Step 5** — Introduce $L_c$ via $A/V = 1/L_c$:

$$\frac{T(t) - T_\infty}{T_i - T_\infty} = \exp\!\left(-\frac{h L_c}{k}\cdot\frac{\alpha t}{L_c^{2}}\right) = \exp(-Bi \cdot Fo)$$

This single equation holds for **any geometry** (slab, cylinder, sphere) as long as $Bi \le 0.1$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Critical Radius for a Sphere

**Geometry:** Inner sphere radius $r_1$ at temperature $T_i$, outer radius $r_2 = r_1 + t_{ins}$ exposed to ambient $T_\infty$ with coefficient $h$.

**Step 1** — Resistances in series:

$$R_{th} = \underbrace{\frac{1}{4\pi k}\!\left(\frac{1}{r_1} - \frac{1}{r_2}\right)}_{\text{Conduction}} + \underbrace{\frac{1}{h\, 4\pi r_2^{2}}}_{\text{Convection}}$$

**Step 2** — Heat flow:

$$Q = \frac{T_i - T_\infty}{R_{th}} = \frac{4\pi (T_i - T_\infty)}{\dfrac{1}{k}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{r_1} - \dfrac{1}{r_2}\right) + \dfrac{1}{h\, r_2^{2}}}$$

**Step 3** — To find the maximum of $Q$ with respect to $r_2$, differentiate $1/R_{th}$:

$$\frac{d}{dr_2}\!\left(\frac{1}{R_{th}}\right) = 0 \;\Longrightarrow\; -\frac{1}{k}\frac{d}{dr_2}\!\left(\frac{1}{r_1} - \frac{1}{r_2}\right) - \frac{1}{h}\frac{d}{dr_2}\!\left(\frac{1}{r_2^{2}}\right) = 0$$

$$\Rightarrow -\frac{1}{k}\!\left(\frac{1}{r_2^{2}}\right) + \frac{2}{h\, r_2^{3}} = 0$$

**Step 4** — Cancel $1/r_2^{2}$:

$$\frac{2}{h\, r_2} = \frac{1}{k} \;\Longrightarrow\; r_2 = r_{cr} = \frac{2k}{h}$$

**Step 5** — Same procedure for cylinder gives:

$$r_{cr} = \frac{k}{h}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The factor of **2** appears only for the sphere because its surface area scales as $r^{2}$ while its conduction path scales as $1/r$, leading to a different critical condition.

---

## 3.3 Total Heat Transfer Using Heisler Approximation

The total heat transferred up to time $t$ is:

$$Q = \rho V c_p (T_i - T_\infty)\left[1 - \frac{3\theta_0^{*}}{Bi\,Fo}\right] \quad \text{(sphere/cylinder, Bi > 0.1)}$$

where $\theta_0^{*}$ is the **dimensionless centre temperature** read from the Heisler chart at the given $(Bi, Fo)$.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Decision Flow — Lumped vs. Heisler vs. Critical Radius

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start: Transient cooling/heating problem"] --> B["Compute Bi = h L_c / k"]
    B --> C{"Bi ≤ 0.1?"}
    C -- "Yes" --> D["Use Lumped System Equation"]
    D --> D1["θ = exp -Bi·Fo"]
    D1 --> D2["Compute T t and total heat Q"]
    C -- "No" --> E{"Geometry?"}
    E -- "Cylinder" --> F["Use Heisler Cylinder Charts"]
    E -- "Sphere" --> G["Use Heisler Sphere Charts"]
    E -- "Plane wall" --> H["Use Heisler Plane Wall Charts"]
    F --> I["Read θ0 from chart at given Bi,Fo"]
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J["Compute T centerline T r,t and total heat Q"]
    J --> K["Check: insulation design?"]
    K -- "Yes" --> L{"Outer radius r2 vs rcr?"}
    L -- "r2 < rcr" --> M["WARNING: Adding insulation increases Q"]
    L -- "r2 = rcr" --> N["Q is maximum"]
    L -- "r2 > rcr" --> O["Q decreases with more insulation"]
```

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Heat Flow Path in Insulated Cylinder

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph InnerPipe["Hot Inner Pipe r1"]
        T1["Temperature T1 inside fluid"]
    end
    subgraph Insulation["Insulation Layer r1 to r2"]
        Rcond["R_cond = ln r2/r1 divided by 2π k L"]
    end
    subgraph Ambient["Ambient Fluid"]
        Rconv["R_conv = 1 divided by h 2π r2 L"]
        Tinf["Ambient T∞"]
    end
    T1 --> Rcond --> Rconv --> Tinf
    style InnerPipe fill:#ffd9b3,stroke:#cc6600
    style Insulation fill:#ffe680,stroke:#b38600
    style Ambient fill:#b3d9ff,stroke:#0066cc
```

---

## 4.3 Schematic — Transient Temperature Profile Inside a Cylinder

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph Axis["Centre axis r = 0"]
        C0["T centerline highest"]
    end
    subgraph Mid["Mid radius r = R/2"]
        C1["T mid = parabolic drop"]
    end
    subgraph Wall["Surface r = R"]
        C2["T surface closest to T∞"]
    end
    C0 --> C1 --> C2
    style Axis fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#990000
    style Mid fill:#ffe680,stroke:#b38600
    style Wall fill:#b3d9ff,stroke:#0066cc
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — 3-Mark Short Questions

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define the Biot number and state its significance in transient heat conduction.**

**Model Answer:**
The Biot number is a dimensionless quantity defined as $Bi = h L_c / k$, where $h$ is the convective heat transfer coefficient, $k$ is the thermal conductivity of the solid, and $L_c$ is the characteristic length. For cylinders $L_c = R/2$, for spheres $L_c = R/3$. Its significance: when $Bi \le 0.1$ the internal conduction resistance is negligible, and the entire solid can be treated as a single lumped capacitance with a uniform temperature distribution. When $Bi > 0.1$ the internal resistance becomes significant, and the transient temperature distribution inside the cylinder or sphere must be determined using Heisler charts or series solutions.

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
**Define the critical radius of insulation and write its expression for a cylinder and a sphere.**

**Model Answer:**
