The critical radius of insulation is the outer radius of insulation at which the rate of heat transfer from the insulated body to the surroundings is **maximum**. Adding insulation beyond this radius reduces heat loss; adding insulation below this radius *increases* heat loss because the increased surface area outweighs the added conductive resistance. The critical radius for a cylinder is $r_{cr} = k/h$, and for a sphere is $r_{cr} = 2k/h$, where $k$ is the thermal conductivity of insulation and $h$ is the convective heat transfer coefficient of the surrounding fluid.

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Module Internal Choice Questions

### Question A — Option 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
A long **cylindrical steel billet** of radius $R = 75$ mm, initially at a uniform temperature $T_i = 540°\text{C}$, is suddenly immersed in an oil bath at $T_\infty = 65°\text{C}$ with $h = 280\;\text{W/m}^2\text{K}$. Properties of steel: $k = 33\;\text{W/m·K}$, $\rho = 7800\;\text{kg/m}^3$, $c_p = 480\;\text{J/kg·K}$.

**(a) [7 Marks]** Verify whether the lumped capacitance method is applicable. If valid, determine the time required for the centre temperature of the billet to drop to $250°\text{C}$.

**(b) [7 Marks]** If the lumped method is *not* applicable, use the Heisler chart procedure to find (i) the centre temperature at $t = 1.5$ hour, and (ii) the total heat removed in that time.

---

### Model Solution for Question A

**Part (a):** Apply the lumped-system criterion.

**Step 1** — Characteristic length for cylinder:

$$L_c = \frac{R}{2} = \frac{0.075}{2} = 0.0375\;\text{m}$$

**Step 2** — Compute $Bi$:

$$Bi = \frac{h L_c}{k} = \frac{280 \times 0.0375}{33} = 0.318$$

**Step 3** — Since $Bi = 0.318 > 0.1$, the **lumped system method is NOT valid**. (The question's "if valid" branch returns false.) We must therefore proceed to part (b) using Heisler charts.

> [!NOTE]
> This question deliberately traps students who blindly plug into the exponential without checking $Bi$. KTU examiners award **zero marks** for a lumped solution when $Bi > 0.1$.

---

**Part (b)(i) — Centre temperature at $t = 1.5$ h:**

**Step 1** — Thermal diffusivity:

$$\alpha = \frac{k}{\rho c_p} = \frac{33}{7800 \times 480} = 8.81 \times 10^{-6}\;\text{m}^2/\text{s}$$

**Step 2** — Fourier number:

$$t = 1.5 \times 3600 = 5400\;\text{s}$$

$$Fo = \frac{\alpha t}{L_c^{2}} = \frac{8.81 \times 10^{-6} \times 5400}{(0.0375)^{2}} = 33.83$$

**Step 3** — At $Bi = 0.318$ and $Fo = 33.83$, read from Heisler centreline chart (cylinder):

$$\theta_0^{*} = \frac{T_0 - T_\infty}{T_i - T_\infty} \approx 0.22$$

**Step 4** — Solve for centre temperature:

$$T_0 = T_\infty + \theta_0^{*}(T_i - T_\infty) = 65 + 0.22(540 - 65) = 65 + 0.22(475) \approx 169.5°\text{C}$$

$$\boxed{T_0 \approx 170°\text{C}}$$

**Part (b)(ii) — Total heat removed in $t = 1.5$ h:**

**Step 1** — Per-unit-length cylinder volume:

$$\frac{V}{L} = \pi R^{2} = \pi (0.075)^{2} = 0.01767\;\text{m}^2$$

**Step 2** — Maximum heat that could be transferred (entire body cooling to $T_\infty$):

$$\frac{Q_{\max}}{L} = \rho c_p \frac{V}{L} (T_i - T_\infty) = 7800 \times 480 \times 0.01767 \times 475 = 3.14 \times 10^{7}\;\text{J/m}$$

**Step 3** — Compute $Bi \cdot Fo$:

$$Bi \cdot Fo = 0.318 \times 33.83 = 10.76$$

**Step 4** — Total heat ratio for cylinder (one-term Heisler approximation):

$$\frac{Q}{Q_{\max}} = 1 - \frac{3\,\theta_0^{*}}{Bi \cdot Fo} = 1 - \frac{3 \times 0.22}{10.76} = 1 - 0.0614 = 0.939$$

**Step 5** — Total heat removed:

$$\frac{Q}{L} = 0.939 \times 3.14 \times 10^{7} = 2.95 \times 10^{7}\;\text{J/m}$$

$$\boxed{Q/L \approx 2.95 \times 10^{7}\;\text{J/m}}$$

---

### Question B — Option 2 [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
A **spherical pressure vessel** of outer radius $R = 0.5$ m, made of stainless steel, contains superheated steam. The outer surface is suddenly exposed to ambient air at $T_\infty = 25°\text{C}$ with $h = 25\;\text{W/m}^2\text{K}$. Initial temperature $T_i = 250°\text{C}$. Properties: $k = 18\;\text{W/m·K}$, $\rho = 7900\;\text{kg/m}^3$, $c_p = 510\;\text{J/kg·K}$.

**(a) [7 Marks]** Check the applicability of the lumped system analysis. If valid, calculate the temperature after 30 minutes.

**(b) [7 Marks]** The vessel is to be insulated with a material of $k_{ins} = 0.04\;\text{W/m·K}$. Determine the **critical radius** of insulation and comment on the heat-loss behaviour as insulation thickness is increased.

---

### Model Solution for Question B

**Part (a):**

**Step 1** — Characteristic length for sphere:

$$L_c = \frac{R}{3} = \frac{0.5}{3} = 0.1667\;\text{m}$$

**Step 2** — Biot number:

$$Bi = \frac{h L_c}{k} = \frac{25 \times 0.1667}{18} = 0.231$$

**Step 3** — Since $Bi = 0.231 > 0.1$, the **lumped method is NOT applicable**.

> [!WARNING]
> Students commonly skip the $Bi$ check and directly apply $e^{-Bi\,Fo}$, losing **3 of the 7 marks** allotted to part (a).

**Part (b):**

**Step 1** — Critical radius of insulation for a **sphere**:

$$r_{cr} = \frac{2 k_{ins}}{h} = \frac{2 \times 0.04}{25} = 0.0032\;\text{m} = 3.2\;\text{mm}$$

**Step 2** — Behavioural comment:
- The outer radius of the vessel is $R = 0.5$ m = 500 mm, which is much greater than $r_{cr} = 3.2$ mm.
- Since the bare outer radius already far exceeds the critical radius, **adding insulation will monotonically reduce heat loss**.
- Only when insulation thickness is so small that $r_2 < 3.2$ mm would the insulation cause heat loss to **increase**. In this practical vessel, that scenario is irrelevant.

**Step 3** — Bonus qualitative reasoning:
The critical radius exists because adding insulation simultaneously (i) increases conduction resistance, but (ii) increases external surface area available for convection. Below $r_{cr}$, the surface-area effect dominates and $Q$ rises; above $r_{cr}$, the conduction effect dominates and $Q$ falls.

$$\boxed{r_{cr} = 3.2\;\text{mm};\quad \text{insulation always reduces heat loss for } r_2 > 3.2\;\text{mm}}$$

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 ways students lose marks in Cylinder-Sphere transient problems:**
> 1. **Skipping the $Bi$ check** — Using $e^{-Bi\,Fo}$ when $Bi > 0.1$ costs full marks.
> 2. **Wrong characteristic length** — Mixing up $L_c = R/2$ (cylinder) and $L_c = R/3$ (sphere).
> 3. **Confusing units of $\alpha$** — Using $\alpha$ in cm²/s instead of m²/s, leading to Fo off by $10^4$.
> 4. **Critical radius misinterpretation** — Forgetting the factor of **2** for the sphere.
> 5. **Forgetting $Q_{\max}$ baseline** in the total-heat formula — Mistaking $Q/Q_{\max}$ for a temperature ratio.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Bi = $hL_c / k$** is the *gatekeeper*. Always check before applying any formula.
- ✅ **$L_c = R/2$ for cylinder, $L_c = R/3$ for sphere.**
- ✅ Lumped-system validity: **$Bi \le 0.1$**; otherwise use Heisler charts.
- ✅ **Time constant** $\tau = \rho c_p L_c / h$ — a unique number per geometry.
- ✅ For Heisler centreline chart: read **$\theta_0^{*}$ at $(Bi, Fo)$** then $T_0 = T_\infty + \theta_0^{*}(T_i - T_\infty)$.
- ✅ Total heat removed: $Q = Q_{\max}\!\left[1 - \dfrac{3\theta_0^{*}}{Bi\,Fo}\right]$ (one-term Heisler approximation).
- ✅ **Critical radius**: $r_{cr} = k/h$ (cylinder), $r_{cr} = 2k/h$ (sphere).
- ✅ For cylinders/sphere **only**, a critical radius exists — plane walls never show this anomaly.
- ✅ Always convert $t$ to **seconds** before computing $Fo$.
- ✅ Always use **consistent SI units** for $k$, $\rho$, $c_p$, $h$, $R$.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Radiation Laws and Radiative Properties
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET754 - HEAT AND MASS TRANSFER
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Radiation Laws and Radiative Properties

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Thermal Radiation** is the energy emitted by a body in the form of electromagnetic waves as a consequence of its temperature. All bodies above absolute zero (0 K) emit thermal radiation, and the rate and spectral character of this emission are governed by fundamental **Radiation Laws** and **Radiative Properties** of the surface.

Radiation is unique among heat transfer modes because it does **not** require a medium—it propagates through vacuum at the speed of light. Unlike conduction and convection, it is a **surface phenomenon**: emission originates from the outermost atomic layers (within ~1 µm depth).

The classical electromagnetic spectrum places thermal radiation primarily in the **infrared region** (0.1–1000 µm), although bodies above ~800 K begin to emit appreciable visible light (incandescence).

### 1.2 Fundamental Radiative Properties (Surface Quantities)

| Property | Symbol | Definition |
|----------|--------|------------|
| Emissivity | ε | Ratio of radiation emitted by a real surface to that emitted by a blackbody at the same temperature |
| Absorptivity | α | Fraction of incident radiation absorbed by the surface |
| Reflectivity | ρ | Fraction of incident radiation reflected by the surface |
| Transmissivity | τ | Fraction of incident radiation transmitted through the surface |

For any real surface, the energy balance on incident radiation gives:
$$\alpha + \rho + \tau = 1$$

For an **opaque body** (most engineering surfaces, τ = 0), this simplifies to:
$$\alpha + \rho = 1$$

### 1.3 The Blackbody Idealization

> [!NOTE]
> A **Blackbody** is an idealized surface that absorbs **all** incident radiation at every wavelength, direction, and temperature. By definition, α = 1, ρ = 0, τ = 0. It is also the most efficient emitter at any given temperature.

A near-perfect physical approximation is a small opening in a large, isothermal cavity (Hohlraum), since any radiation entering is effectively absorbed after multiple internal reflections.

### 1.4 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a white painted wall and a freshly tarred road on a sunny day. Even when both receive identical solar radiation, the **tarred road becomes far hotter**. Why? The white wall *reflects* most of the incident rays (high ρ, low α), while the tar absorbs nearly all of them (high α). Kirchhoff's Law (covered later) then dictates that a strong absorber is also a strong emitter—so the black road also radiates heat efficiently back to its surroundings.

This everyday observation captures the **duality of absorption and emission**, the foundational idea behind all radiation laws.

---

### 1.5 Key Physical Constants

- **Stefan-Boltzmann constant**: **σ = 5.67 × 10⁻⁸ W/(m²·K⁴)**
- **Planck's first radiation constant**: **C₁ = 2πhc² = 3.7418 × 10⁻¹⁶ W·m²**
- **Planck's second radiation constant**: **C₂ = hc/k = 1.4388 × 10⁻² m·K**
- **Speed of light**: c = 2.998 × 10⁸ m/s
- **Planck's constant**: h = 6.626 × 10⁻³⁴ J·s
- **Boltzmann's constant**: k = 1.381 × 10⁻²³ J/K

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Four Pillars of Thermal Radiation

#### **Pillar 1 — Planck's Law (1901)**

Describes the **spectral distribution** of blackbody emissive power at wavelength λ and temperature T:

$$E_{b\lambda}(\lambda, T) = \frac{C_1}{\lambda^{5} \left[\exp\!\left(\dfrac{C_2}{\lambda T}\right) - 1\right]}$$

where E_bλ has units of **W/m²·µm** (per unit wavelength interval). Planck's law is the *exact* description; all other laws are special cases derived from it.

#### **Pillar 2 — Wien's Displacement Law**

Differentiating Planck's distribution and setting dE_bλ/dλ = 0 yields the wavelength of maximum emission:

$$\lambda_{\max} \cdot T = 2898 \;\; \mu m \cdot K$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As temperature rises, the peak shifts to **shorter wavelengths** (toward visible light). This is why a heated iron first glows dull red, then orange, then white as temperature climbs.

#### **Pillar 3 — Stefan-Boltzmann Law (1879–1884)**

Integrating Planck's distribution over all wavelengths (0 to ∞) gives the **total hemispherical emissive power** of a blackbody:

$$E_b = \int_{0}^{\infty} E_{b\lambda}\, d\lambda = \sigma T^{4}$$

This is the **fourth-power law of thermal radiation**—one of the most consequential relations in heat transfer.

#### **Pillar 4 — Kirchhoff's Law (1859)**

For a surface in **thermal equilibrium** with its surroundings at the same temperature, emissivity equals absorptivity at every wavelength:

$$\varepsilon_\lambda(T) = \alpha_\lambda(T)$$

For *total* (spectrally averaged) properties when the surface behaves as a **diffuse gray body**:

$$\varepsilon = \alpha$$

> [!NOTE]
> This relation is **only** valid for surfaces in thermal equilibrium with their environment. For a cold surface in a warm surrounding (or vice-versa), α and ε are evaluated at different temperatures and need not match.

### 2.2 Real Surfaces vs. Idealizations

| Surface Type | α | ε | ρ | τ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Blackbody** (ideal) | 1 | 1 | 0 | 0 |
| **Diffuse Gray Surface** | ε (constant) | ε = α | 1 − ε | 0 |
| **Diffuse Real Surface** | α(λ) | ε(λ) | ρ(λ) | 0 |
| **Specular Mirror** | 0 | 0 | 1 | 0 |
| **Transparent Glass** | small | small | small | ~1 |

A **gray body** is one whose emissivity is **independent of wavelength**. This simplification is widely used in engineering because it reduces integral relations to simple algebraic ones.

### 2.3 Lambert's Cosine Law

A **diffuse surface** emits radiation such that the intensity (W/m²·sr) is independent of direction, but the projected area decreases as cos θ. The result is:

$$I_\theta = I_n \cos\theta$$

where I_n is the intensity along the normal. The total hemispherical emissive power relates to the normal intensity as:

$$E_b = \pi I_n$$

This is why integrating over a hemisphere gives a factor of π (the projected solid angle), not 2π.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **complete high-yield formula set** for this topic — every numerical problem in the KTU ESE from this module can be solved using only these equations.

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Use |
|---|---------|-------------|-------------|
| 1 | $E_{b\lambda} = \dfrac{C_1}{\lambda^{5}\left[\exp(C_2/\lambda T) - 1\right]}$ | Planck's spectral distribution | Wavelength-specific emission |
| 2 | $\lambda_{\max} T = 2898\ \mu m \cdot K$ | Wien's displacement | Peak emission wavelength |
| 3 | $E_b = \sigma T^{4}$ | Stefan-Boltzmann (blackbody total) | Total emitted flux |
| 4 | $E = \varepsilon \sigma T^{4}$ | Gray body total emission | Real-surface heat loss |
| 5 | $q = \varepsilon \sigma (T_s^{4} - T_{sur}^{4})$ | Net radiative heat flux | Surface to surroundings |
| 6 | $\alpha + \rho + \tau = 1$ | Energy balance | Opaque: τ = 0 |
| 7 | $\varepsilon_\lambda = \alpha_\lambda$ | Kirchhoff (monochromatic) | At equilibrium |
| 8 | $\varepsilon = \alpha$ | Kirchhoff (gray, total) | Diffuse gray body |
| 9 | $C_1 = 3.7418 \times 10^{-16}\ W\cdot m^{2}$ | Planck constant 1 | Spectral calc |
| 10 | $C_2 = 1.4388 \times 10^{-2}\ m\cdot K$ | Planck constant 2 | Spectral calc |
| 11 | $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}\ W/(m^{2}\cdot K^{4})$ | Stefan-Boltzmann constant | Total flux |
| 12 | $F_{\lambda \to T} = \dfrac{\int_{0}^{\lambda} E_{b\lambda}\,d\lambda}{\sigma T^{4}}$ | Fractional blackbody function | Spectral band problems |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Furnace and boiler design**: sizing combustion chambers uses σT⁴ limits and emissivity of refractory walls.
- **Spacecraft thermal control**: low-α/ε white paints (α/ε < 1) prevent solar heating; high-α/ε coatings maximize radiation cooling.
- **Solar collectors**: selective surfaces (high α for solar wavelengths, low ε for IR) maximize absorbed energy while minimizing thermal re-radiation.
- **Pyrometers and IR thermography**: rely on spectral and total radiation laws for non-contact temperature measurement.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Stefan-Boltzmann Law from Planck's Distribution

The Stefan-Boltzmann law is obtained by integrating Planck's spectral equation over the entire wavelength spectrum. We start from:

$$E_b = \int_{0}^{\infty} E_{b\lambda}(\lambda, T)\, d\lambda = \int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{C_1}{\lambda^{5}\left[\exp(C_2/\lambda T) - 1\right]} d\lambda$$

**Step 1 — Introduce non-dimensional substitution.** Let $x = \dfrac{C_2}{\lambda T}$, so $\lambda = \dfrac{C_2}{x T}$ and $d\lambda = -\dfrac{C_2}{x^{2} T}\, dx$.

**Step 2 — Transform the limits.** When λ → 0, x → ∞; when λ → ∞, x → 0. The minus sign flips integration limits back to 0 → ∞.

**Step 3 — Substitute into the integral.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_b &= \int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{C_1}{\left(\dfrac{C_2}{xT}\right)^{5} \left[\exp(x) - 1\right]} \cdot \frac{C_2}{x^{2} T}\, dx \\
&= \int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{C_1 \, x^{5} T^{5}}{C_2^{5} \left[\exp(x) - 1\right]} \cdot \frac{C_2}{x^{2} T}\, dx \\
&= \frac{C_1 T^{4}}{C_2^{4}} \int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{x^{3}}{\exp(x) - 1}\, dx
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the standard integral.** The integral is a known Riemann zeta product:

$$\int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{x^{3}}{e^{x} - 1}\, dx = \frac{\pi^{4}}{15}$$

**Step 5 — Assemble constants.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_b &= \frac{C_1 T^{4}}{C_2^{4}} \cdot \frac{\pi^{4}}{15} \\
&= \frac{2\pi h c^{2} T^{4}}{(hc/k)^{4}} \cdot \frac{\pi^{4}}{15} \\
&= \frac{2\pi h c^{2} T^{4} k^{4}}{h^{4} c^{4}} \cdot \frac{\pi^{4}}{15} \\
&= \frac{2\pi^{5} k^{4}}{15 h^{3} c^{2}}\, T^{4}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Identify Stefan-Boltzmann constant σ.** Plugging in the numerical values:

$$\sigma = \frac{2\pi^{5} k^{4}}{15 h^{3} c^{2}} = 5.670 \times 10^{-8}\ W/(m^{2} \cdot K^{4})$$

Hence we obtain the celebrated result:
$$\boxed{\,E_b = \sigma T^{4}\,}$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Wien's Displacement Law

Starting again from Planck's distribution:

$$E_{b\lambda} = \frac{C_1}{\lambda^{5} \left[\exp(C_2/\lambda T) - 1\right]}$$

**Step 1 — Differentiate with respect to λ** and set the derivative to zero at $\lambda = \lambda_{\max}$:

$$\frac{dE_{b\lambda}}{d\lambda} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{d}{d\lambda}\!\left\{\frac{1}{\lambda^{5}\left[\exp(C_2/\lambda T) - 1\right]}\right\} = 0$$

**Step 2 — Apply the quotient rule** and simplify, letting $y = C_2 / (\lambda_{\max} T)$:

$$5\left(1 - e^{-y}\right) = y$$

**Step 3 — Solve the transcendental equation.** Numerical solution gives:

$$y = 4.9651 \quad \text{i.e.} \quad \frac{C_2}{\lambda_{\max} T} = 4.9651$$

**Step 4 — Solve for λ_max:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lambda_{\max} T &= \frac{C_2}{4.9651} = \frac{0.014388}{4.9651} \\
\lambda_{\max} T &= 2.898 \times 10^{-3}\ m \cdot K \\
\end{aligned}
$$

Or equivalently:
$$\boxed{\,\lambda_{\max} T = 2898\ \mu m \cdot K\,}$$

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: When to Use Each Law

| Problem Scenario | Governing Law | Key Output |
|---|---|---|
| Find total heat emitted by a surface | Stefan-Boltzmann | $E = \varepsilon \sigma T^{4}$ |
| Find wavelength of peak emission | Wien's displacement | $\lambda_{\max} = 2898/T$ |
| Find spectral intensity at specific λ | Planck's law | $E_{b\lambda}(\lambda, T)$ |
| Find absorbed radiation given spectrum | $\alpha = \sum \alpha_\lambda F_\lambda$ | Spectrally weighted α |
| Find net radiative heat flux | Stefan-Boltzmann (net) | $q = \varepsilon \sigma (T_1^{4} - T_2^{4})$ |
| Verify surface at equilibrium | Kirchhoff | $\alpha = \varepsilon$ |
| Directional emission from diffuse surface | Lambert's cosine | $I_\theta = I_n \cos\theta$ |

---

### 3.4 Sample Analytical Calculation (KTU Standard)

**Problem:** A blackbody surface is at 1500 K. Determine (a) the wavelength of maximum emission, (b) the total emissive power, and (c) the spectral emissive power at λ = 2 µm.

**Solution:**

(a) From Wien's law:

$$\lambda_{\max} = \frac{2898}{T} = \frac{2898}{1500} = 1.932\ \mu m$$

(b) Total emissive power:

$$E_b = \sigma T^{4} = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times (1500)^{4} = 2.87 \times 10^{5}\ W/m^{2}$$

(c) Spectral emissive power at λ = 2 µm:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{b\lambda}(2, 1500) &= \frac{3.7418 \times 10^{-16}}{(2 \times 10^{-6})^{5}\left[\exp\!\left(\dfrac{1.4388 \times 10^{-2}}{2 \times 10^{-6} \times 1500}\right) - 1\right]} \\
&= \frac{3.7418 \times 10^{-16}}{3.2 \times 10^{-26}\left[\exp(4.796) - 1\right]} \\
&= \frac{3.7418 \times 10^{-16}}{3.2 \times 10^{-26} \times (120.93 - 1)} \\
&= \frac{3.7418 \times 10^{-16}}{3.837 \times 10^{-24}} \\
&\approx 9.75 \times 10^{7}\ W/(m^{2} \cdot \mu m)
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity check**: Multiplying the spectral value by ~2 µm wavelength gives ~1.95 × 10⁸ W/m² — which sits plausibly below the peak (~5–10 × 10⁷ integrated over the spectrum to reach 2.87 × 10⁵ W/m²). ✓

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Blackbody Radiation Spectrum — Spectral Distribution at Different Temperatures

The figure below shows the **spectral emissive power E_bλ** as a function of wavelength λ at three different blackbody temperatures. Note how increasing T (a) raises the curve's overall magnitude, (b) shifts the peak to shorter wavelengths (Wien's law), and (c) spreads the spectrum.

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph Inputs["Input Parameters"]
        T1["Temperature T1 = 800 K"]
        T2["Temperature T2 = 1500 K"]
        T3["Temperature T3 = 3000 K"]
    end

    subgraph Process["Planck Spectral Distribution"]
        P1["Compute x = C2 divided by lambda times T"]
        P2["Evaluate E_b_lambda = C1 divided by lambda^5 times exp(x) minus 1"]
        P3["Integrate over all lambda to get E_b = sigma T^4"]
        P4["Find lambda_max where dE_b_lambda divided by d lambda equals 0"]
    end

    subgraph Outputs["Output Quantities"]
        O1["Spectral curve E_b_lambda vs lambda"]
        O2["Peak wavelength lambda_max = 2898 divided by T"]
        O3["Total hemispherical emissive power E_b = sigma T^4"]
    end

    T1 --> P1
    T2 --> P1
    T3 --> P1
    P1 --> P2
    P2 --> O1
    P2 --> P4
    P4 --> O2
    P2 --> P3
    P3 --> O3

    style Inputs fill:#FFF8E1,stroke:#FFB300,stroke-width:1px
    style Process fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1976D2,stroke-width:1px
    style Outputs fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#388E3C,stroke-width:1px
    style T1 fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#C62828
    style T2 fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#EF6C00
    style T3 fill:#FCE4EC,stroke:#AD1457
```

**Reading the diagram**: The flow is read top-to-bottom. Temperature inputs feed into Planck's law computation (P1→P2), from which three outputs are produced simultaneously — the spectral curve (O1), the peak wavelength via differentiation (O2), and the total emissive power via integration (O3).

---

### 4.2 Surface Radiation Properties — Block Architecture

This block diagram maps the **energy partitioning** at an arbitrary real surface and the **Kirchhoff linkage** between absorption and emission properties.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["Incident Radiation Q_in"]:::inflow
    A --> B["Surface Interaction Zone"]
    B --> C["Absorbed Fraction alpha times Q_in"]:::abs
    B --> D["Reflected Fraction rho times Q_in"]:::ref
    B --> E["Transmitted Fraction tau times Q_in"]:::tra
    B --> F["Emitted Fraction epsilon times sigma T^4"]:::emi

    C --> G["Surface Body Temperature T"]
    F --> G
    G --> H["Kirchhoff Linkage: alpha = epsilon at equilibrium"]:::kirch

    classDef inflow fill:#E1F5FE,stroke:#01579B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef abs fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#2E7D32,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef ref fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef tra fill:#F8BBD0,stroke:#880E4F,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef emi fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#B71C1C,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef kirch fill:#FFF59D,stroke:#F57F17,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
```

**Reading the diagram**: An incoming radiation flux Q_in is split at the surface into three interactions — absorbed (C), reflected (D), transmitted (E) — while the surface simultaneously emits its own thermal radiation (F). The Kirchhoff block (H) couples absorption and emission when the surface reaches thermal equilibrium.

---

### 4.3 Radiation Laws Hierarchy — Conceptual Map

```mermaid
graph TB
    ROOT["Thermal Radiation Theory"]:::root
    P["Planck Law — Exact Spectral Distribution"]:::p
    W["Wien Displacement Law — Peak Wavelength"]:::w
    S["Stefan-Boltzmann Law — Total Emissive Power"]:::s
    K["Kirchhoff Law — Absorptivity equals Emissivity"]:::k
    R["Radiative Properties: alpha, rho, tau, epsilon"]:::r

    P --> W
    P --> S
    K --> R
    S --> E["Engineering Net Heat Flux: q equals epsilon times sigma times T_s^4 minus T_sur^4"]:::eng

    classDef root fill:#EDE7F6,stroke:#4527A0,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
    classDef p fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#0D47A1,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef w fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef s fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#B71C1C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef k fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#1B5E20,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef r fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#4A148C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef eng fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F57F17,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
```

**Reading the diagram**: Planck's law is the *exact* master equation. Wien and Stefan-Boltzmann are derived by differentiation and integration of Planck's law respectively. Kirchhoff's law bridges the property definitions to a real engineering surface, finally leading to the practical **net radiation heat flux equation** used in virtually every KTU numerical problem.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### **PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)**

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
*State and explain Wien's Displacement Law. A furnace has a surface temperature of 2000 K. Find the wavelength at which maximum emission occurs.*

**Model Answer:**

Wien's displacement law states that the wavelength at which the spectral emissive power of a blackbody is maximum is inversely proportional to its absolute temperature:

$$\lambda_{\max} \cdot T = 2898\ \mu m \cdot K$$

For T = 2000 K:

$$\lambda_{\max} = \frac{2898}{2000} = 1.449\ \mu m$$

This lies in the **near-infrared** region, which is why such furnaces glow with a dull orange-red color to the eye.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define emissivity, absorptivity, and reflectivity. State Kirchhoff's law.*

**Model Answer:**

- **Emissivity (ε)**: Ratio of radiation emitted by a real surface to that emitted by a blackbody at the same temperature.
- **Absorptivity (α)**: Fraction of incident radiation absorbed by the surface.
- **Reflectivity (ρ)**: Fraction of incident radiation reflected by the surface.

For an opaque surface: α + ρ = 1.

**Kirchhoff's Law**: At thermal equilibrium, the monochromatic emissivity of a surface equals its monochromatic absorptivity at the same wavelength and temperature, i.e., ε_λ(T) = α_λ(T). For a diffuse gray body, this extends to total properties: ε = α.

---

### **PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each, Internal Choice)**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert**: Many students confuse **monochromatic** (spectral) and **total** (hemispherical) emissivity. Kirchhoff's law in the form ε = α holds **only** for diffuse gray surfaces. For selective (spectrally varying) surfaces, ε_λ = α_λ holds **at each wavelength**, but total ε ≠ total α unless the incident spectrum equals the emission spectrum (equilibrium condition).

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**(a)** A tungsten filament has a surface area of 1.5 cm² and an emissivity of 0.35. It operates at 2800 K inside a large evacuated bulb whose inner surface is at 400 K. Calculate:
- (i) Total emissive power of the filament.
- (ii) Total radiation incident on the filament from the bulb walls.
- (iii) Net radiation heat loss from the filament.

**(b)** With a neat sketch, explain the spectral distribution of blackbody radiation and discuss how it varies with temperature.

---

### **Model Solution**

**(a)** Given: A_s = 1.5 × 10⁻⁴ m², ε_f = 0.35, T_f = 2800 K, T_w = 400 K, σ = 5.67 × 10⁻⁸ W/(m²·K⁴).

**(i) Total emissive power of filament:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_f &= \varepsilon_f \sigma T_f^{4} \\
&= 0.35 \times 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times (2800)^{4} \\
&= 0.35 \times 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times 6.1466 \times 10^{13} \\
&= 1.2196 \times 10^{6}\ W/m^{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Incident radiation on filament (treating filament as blackbody absorbing from surroundings):**

For radiation *from* the bulb walls *on* the filament, the filament absorbs fraction α_f = ε_f = 0.35 (gray body assumption) of the blackbody radiation the walls emit:

$$
\begin{aligned}
G_f &= \alpha_f \sigma T_w^{4} \\
&= 0.35 \times 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times (400)^{4} \\
&= 0.35 \times 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times 2.56 \times 10^{10} \\
&= 508.0\ W/m^{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification**: $400^4 = 2.56 \times 10^{10}$ — confirmed.

**(iii) Net radiation heat loss:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
q_{net} &= \varepsilon_f \sigma (T_f^{4} - T_w^{4}) \\
&= 0.35 \times 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times \left[(2800)^{4} - (400)^{4}\right] \\
&= 0.35 \times 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times (6.1466 \times 10^{13} - 2.56 \times 10^{10}) \\
&\approx 0.35 \times 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times 6.144 \times 10^{13} \\
&= 1.219 \times 10^{6}\ W/m^{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The incident radiation G_f ≈ 508 W/m² is **negligible** (~0.04%) compared to the emissive power (~1.22 × 10⁶ W/m²). For high-temperature sources, the surroundings' contribution is often ignored in preliminary calculations.

**Net heat loss from the entire filament:**

$$Q_{net} = q_{net} \times A_s = 1.219 \times 10^{6} \times 1.5 \times 10^{-4} = 182.9\ W$$

---

**(b)** Spectral distribution explanation — see Section 4.1 diagram above. Key points:

- Spectral emissive power E_bλ has a **continuous spectrum** with a single peak.
- Maximum value of E_bλ increases as T⁵ (Wien's fifth-power law).
- Peak wavelength shifts to shorter λ as T rises (Wien's displacement law).
- **Area under the curve** = σT⁴ (Stefan-Boltzmann).
- At low T (~300 K), peak is in IR (~10 µm); at high T (~6000 K, sun), peak is in visible (~0.5 µm).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]

**(a)** Derive Planck's law in spectral form and show that Wien's displacement law follows from it. A blackbody at 5800 K (Sun's approximate surface temperature) emits maximum radiation at what wavelength? In which region of the spectrum does this lie?

**(b)** A small metal plate has emissivity ε = 0.6 on both sides. It is placed between two large parallel plates maintained at 800 K and 300 K respectively. If the steady-state temperature of the small plate is T_s, find T_s assuming the plate exchanges radiation only with the two large plates.

---

### **Model Solution**

**(a) Derivation of Wien's from Planck's:** Refer to the derivation in Section 3.2 above (students should reproduce the differentiation and the transcendental equation $5(1 - e^{-y}) = y$ yielding $y = 4.9651$).

**Numerical application:**

$$\lambda_{\max} = \frac{2898}{5800} = 0.4997\ \mu m \approx 0.5\ \mu m = 500\ nm$$

This lies in the **visible spectrum** (green-yellow region), which is why human vision evolved sensitivity to peak solar output.

---

**(b) Energy balance on small plate:**

For a small plate between two large parallel plates (view factor F = 1 from each side), with both surfaces at steady state, the net radiative gain = 0.

Let F_1 and F_2 be view factors from the small plate to hot plate and cold plate. Since the plate is small, F_1 = 0.5 and F_2 = 0.5 (sides face equal solid angle).

**Energy balance:**

$$\alpha \sigma F_1 T_1^{4} + \alpha \sigma F_2 T_2^{4} = 2 \varepsilon \sigma T_s^{4}$$

For the small gray plate, α = ε = 0.6 (Kirchhoff for gray body). The factor 2 on the right accounts for emission from **both sides**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
0.5 (T_1^{4} + T_2^{4}) &= 2 T_s^{4} \\
T_s^{4} &= \frac{T_1^{4} + T_2^{4}}{4}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Substituting values** (T_1 = 800 K, T_2 = 300 K):

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_s^{4} &= \frac{(800)^{4} + (300)^{4}}{4} \\
&= \frac{4.096 \times 10^{11} + 8.1 \times 10^{9}}{4} \\
&= \frac{4.177 \times 10^{11}}{4} \\
&= 1.044 \times 10^{11}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,T_s = (1.044 \times 10^{11})^{1/4} \approx 569.6\ K\,}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Note that T_s = 569.6 K is **greater than** the arithmetic mean (550 K) but **less than** the geometric mean of the fourth powers would suggest. This is because the T⁴ weighting strongly favors the hotter surface.

---

### **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-density rapid revision checklist — print or screenshot this for last-minute review.**

- ☐ **Blackbody** absorbs everything (α=1) and emits the maximum possible radiation at any given T.
- ☐ **Planck's law** is the master equation; Wien's and Stefan-Boltzmann are its derivative and integral.
- ☐ **Wien's displacement law**: $\lambda_{\max} T = 2898\ \mu m \cdot K$ — hotter = shorter peak wavelength.
- ☐ **Stefan-Boltzmann law**: $E_b = \sigma T^{4}$, with **σ = 5.67 × 10⁻⁸ W/(m²·K⁴)**.
- ☐ **Kirchhoff's law**: at thermal equilibrium, $\varepsilon_\lambda = \alpha_\lambda$. For gray bodies, $\varepsilon = \alpha$.
- ☐ **Energy balance on incident radiation**: $\alpha + \rho + \tau = 1$; opaque surface gives $\alpha + \rho = 1$.
- ☐ **Gray body emission**: $E = \varepsilon \sigma T^{4}$ (just multiply blackbody value by ε).
- ☐ **Net heat flux** (surface to large surroundings): $q = \varepsilon \sigma (T_s^{4} - T_{sur}^{4})$.
- ☐ **Lambert's cosine law**: emissive intensity $I_\theta = I_n \cos\theta$; total $E_b = \pi I_n$.
- ☐ **Constants**: $C_1 = 3.7418 \times 10^{-16}\ W\cdot m^{2}$, $C_2 = 1.4388 \times 10^{-2}\ m\cdot K$.
- ☐ Common mistake: applying ε = α when surface and surroundings are at different temperatures — invalid.
- ☐ Common mistake: ignoring that ε depends on temperature for many real materials (e.g., metals).
- ☐ Common mistake: confusing total hemispherical E_b (W/m²) with spectral E_bλ (W/m²·µm).
- ☐ Sun's λ_max ≈ 0.5 µm is the basis of human visible-light sensitivity.
- ☐ In *all* KTU numericals, the first move is to **classify the surface** (blackbody / gray / real), then apply the appropriate formula.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Black Body and Gray Body Radiation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET754 - HEAT AND MASS TRANSFER
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Black Body and Gray Body Radiation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Black Body Radiation — The Ideal Radiator

### Formal KTU 2024 Definition
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Black Body**: A **black body** is defined as a perfect emitter and perfect absorber of thermal radiation. It is an idealized physical body that absorbs **all incident electromagnetic radiation**, regardless of frequency or angle of incidence. Consequently, it also emits the **maximum possible thermal radiation** at any given thermodynamic temperature.

Mathematically, a black body satisfies:

$$\alpha_\lambda = 1 \quad \text{(for all wavelengths } \lambda \text{ and all directions)}$$

$$\rho_\lambda = 0, \quad \tau_\lambda = 0$$

The spectral absorptivity of a black body is unity across the entire electromagnetic spectrum — this is the defining thermodynamic signature of a black body.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Ultimate Sponges of Light"
> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Picture**: Imagine a surface painted with **Vantablack** (the world's darkest known substance). If you shine a flashlight on it, almost no light bounces back — every photon is swallowed. A black body behaves similarly, but in the *infrared spectrum* (heat radiation). It is the **"gold standard"** of thermal emitters — no real material matches it, but we use it as a reference benchmark.
>
> **Engineering Analogy**: Think of a black body like an *ideal voltage source* in electrical circuits. Just as the ideal voltage source provides maximum, lossless power, the black body provides maximum, lossless thermal radiation output.

### The Stefan–Boltzmann Constant — A Universal Number
> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Stefan–Boltzmann constant** is a fundamental physical constant:
>
> $$\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \ \text{W/m}^2\text{K}^4$$
>
> This value is **NOT to be memorized with units error** — examiners specifically deduct marks for writing it as $5.67 \times 10^{-8} \ \text{W/m}^2\text{K}$ or similar wrong powers.

---

## 1.2 Gray Body Radiation — The Realistic Engineering Model

### Formal KTU 2024 Definition
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Gray Body**: A **gray body** is a real (non-ideal) surface whose **monochromatic (spectral) emissivity is constant and independent of wavelength** across the entire spectrum, but is less than unity. It absorbs/emits only a fixed fraction of what a black body would at the same temperature.

The defining property of a gray body:

$$\varepsilon_\lambda = \varepsilon = \text{constant} \quad (< 1)$$

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Tinted Window"
> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Picture**: A gray body is to a black body what a *50% tinted window* is to clear glass. A clear window lets 100% of sunlight through (transparent), a black wall blocks 100% (opaque absorber). A gray body is in between — it consistently absorbs and emits a *constant fraction* of the black body's energy, irrespective of wavelength.
>
> **Real-World Examples**:
> - Polished metals (ε ≈ 0.05 to 0.2)
> - Human skin (ε ≈ 0.95 to 0.98 — close to gray!)
> - Concrete, asphalt, wood (ε ≈ 0.85 to 0.93)
> - **Most engineering materials used at moderate temperatures behave approximately as gray bodies**, which is why this concept is so critical for KTU problems.

---

## 1.3 Key Radiative Properties — The Radiation "Big Four"

Every real surface interacting with thermal radiation exhibits four fundamental properties:

| Property | Symbol | Range | Definition |
|----------|--------|-------|------------|
| **Absorptivity** | $\alpha$ | $0 \le \alpha \le 1$ | Fraction of incident radiation absorbed |
| **Reflectivity** | $\rho$ | $0 \le \rho \le 1$ | Fraction of incident radiation reflected |
| **Transmissivity** | $\tau$ | $0 \le \tau \le 1$ | Fraction of incident radiation transmitted |
| **Emissivity** | $\varepsilon$ | $0 \le \varepsilon \le 1$ | Ratio of actual emission to black body emission |

### The Fundamental Energy Balance
> [!IMPORTANT]
> By the **Law of Conservation of Energy**, the total of absorbed, reflected, and transmitted radiation fractions must equal unity:
>
> $$\alpha + \rho + \tau = 1$$
>
> For **opaque surfaces** (most engineering materials like metals, concrete, wood), $\tau = 0$, simplifying to:
>
> $$\alpha + \rho = 1$$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hemispherical vs Directional Properties — A Critical Distinction

KTU problems frequently test whether students correctly distinguish between *directional* and *hemispherical* properties.

### Directional (Angular) Properties
These depend on the **angle of incidence or emission** measured from the surface normal.

- $\alpha_\theta$ — Directional absorptivity at angle $\theta$
- $\varepsilon_\theta$ — Directional emissivity at angle $\theta$

### Hemispherical Properties
These are **integrated (averaged) over the entire hemisphere** above the surface.

- $\alpha$ — Hemispherical absorptivity
- $\varepsilon$ — Hemispherical emissivity

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip**: The relationship between directional and hemispherical properties involves integration over the hemisphere:
>
> $$\varepsilon = \frac{1}{\pi} \int_{0}^{2\pi} \int_{0}^{\pi/2} \varepsilon_\theta \cos\theta \sin\theta \, d\theta \, d\phi$$
>
> The $\cos\theta$ factor is the **Lambert's cosine law** weighting.

---

## 2.2 Black Body Radiation Laws — The Three Pillars

### Pillar 1: Planck's Distribution Law (Spectral Distribution)
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Planck's Law** gives the spectral emissive power of a black body as a function of wavelength and temperature. It is the **fundamental law** of black body radiation.
>
> $$E_{b\lambda} = \frac{2\pi h c^2}{\lambda^5 \left[e^{hc/\lambda k T} - 1\right]}$$
>
> Where:
> - $E_{b\lambda}$ = Spectral emissive power [W/m²·μm]
> - $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s (Planck's constant)
> - $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s (speed of light)
> - $k = 1.381 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K (Boltzmann constant)
> - $\lambda$ = Wavelength [m or μm]
> - $T$ = Absolute temperature [K]

### Pillar 2: Wien's Displacement Law
The wavelength at which $E_{b\lambda}$ is **maximum** is inversely proportional to temperature:

$$\lambda_{\max} \cdot T = 2.898 \times 10^{-3} \ \text{m·K}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight**: As temperature increases, the peak emission shifts to **shorter wavelengths** (visible spectrum from infrared). This is why a heated iron rod first glows red, then orange, then white-hot.

### Pillar 3: Stefan–Boltzmann Law (Total Emissive Power)
> [!IMPORTANT]
> The total hemispherical emissive power of a black body is proportional to the **fourth power** of absolute temperature:
>
> $$E_b = \sigma T^4$$
>
> $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \ \text{W/m}^2\text{K}^4$

---

## 2.3 Kirchhoff's Law — The Bridge Between Emission and Absorption

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Kirchhoff's Law** states that for a surface in **thermal equilibrium** with its surroundings, the **emissivity of a surface equals its absorptivity** at every wavelength and direction:
>
> $$\varepsilon_\lambda = \alpha_\lambda$$
>
> This holds because a body in thermal equilibrium must emit exactly as much energy as it absorbs at each wavelength — otherwise it would heat up or cool down, violating equilibrium.

### Special Consequence — The Gray Body Identity
For a **gray body** (where $\varepsilon_\lambda = \varepsilon$ = constant), Kirchhoff's law gives:

$$\varepsilon = \alpha \quad \text{(gray body property)}$$

This is the most important equation for KTU gray body problems!

---

## 2.4 Emissive Power of a Gray Body

A gray body emits only a fraction of the black body radiation at the same temperature:

$$E = \varepsilon E_b = \varepsilon \sigma T^4$$

The total emissive power of a gray body is **always less than** that of a black body at the same temperature.

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $\alpha + \rho + \tau = 1$ | Energy balance (general surface) | Dimensionless |
| 2 | $\alpha + \rho = 1$ | Energy balance (opaque surface) | Dimensionless |
| 3 | $E_b = \sigma T^4$ | Stefan–Boltzmann Law | W/m² |
| 4 | $E = \varepsilon \sigma T^4$ | Gray body emissive power | W/m² |
| 5 | $\varepsilon_\lambda = \alpha_\lambda$ | Kirchhoff's Law | Dimensionless |
| 6 | $\varepsilon = \alpha$ | Kirchhoff for gray body | Dimensionless |
| 7 | $\lambda_{\max} T = 2.898 \times 10^{-3}$ | Wien's Displacement Law | m·K |
| 8 | $E_{b\lambda} = \frac{2\pi h c^2}{\lambda^5 [e^{hc/\lambda kT}-1]}$ | Planck's Law | W/m²·m |
| 9 | $q = \sigma (T_1^4 - T_2^4)$ | Net heat transfer between black bodies | W/m² |
| 10 | $q = \varepsilon \sigma (T_1^4 - T_2^4)$ | Net heat transfer between gray bodies (parallel infinite planes) | W/m² |

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Industrial Applications of Black Body and Gray Body Concepts**:
> 1. **Solar Thermal Collectors**: Designing absorber surfaces to maximize $\alpha$ while minimizing $\varepsilon$ (selective surfaces).
> 2. **Furnace Design**: Refractory furnace linings are designed to behave as near-gray bodies with $\varepsilon \approx 0.9$.
> 3. **Thermal Insulation (Multi-layer Foil)**: Low-emissivity aluminum foils ($\varepsilon \approx 0.05$) used in building insulation.
> 4. **Spacecraft Thermal Control**: White paints with tailored $\alpha/\varepsilon$ ratios to manage heat balance in space.
> 5. **Infrared Thermography**: Medical and military thermal cameras use Stefan–Boltzmann law to convert radiance to temperature.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Stefan–Boltzmann Law from Planck's Law

The Stefan–Boltzmann law can be rigorously derived by integrating Planck's spectral distribution over all wavelengths from 0 to ∞.

### Starting Point
The total emissive power of a black body is the integral of spectral emissive power over the entire wavelength spectrum:

$$E_b = \int_0^{\infty} E_{b\lambda} \, d\lambda$$

### Step 1: Substitute Planck's Spectral Distribution

$$E_b = \int_0^{\infty} \frac{2\pi h c^2}{\lambda^5 \left[ e^{hc/\lambda k T} - 1 \right]} \, d\lambda$$

### Step 2: Apply the Substitution
Let us define a dimensionless variable $x$ to simplify the integral:

$$x = \frac{hc}{\lambda k T} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \lambda = \frac{hc}{xkT}$$

Differentiating both sides with respect to $x$:

$$d\lambda = -\frac{hc}{x^2 kT} \, dx$$

When $\lambda \to 0$, $x \to \infty$, and when $\lambda \to \infty$, $x \to 0$.

### Step 3: Transform the Integral

$$E_b = \int_{\infty}^{0} \frac{2\pi h c^2}{\left(\frac{hc}{xkT}\right)^5 [e^x - 1]} \cdot \left(-\frac{hc}{x^2 kT}\right) dx$$

### Step 4: Simplify the Denominator

$$\left(\frac{hc}{xkT}\right)^5 = \frac{(hc)^5}{x^5 (kT)^5}$$

Substituting back:

$$E_b = \int_0^{\infty} \frac{2\pi h c^2 \cdot x^5 (kT)^5}{(hc)^5 [e^x - 1]} \cdot \frac{hc}{x^2 kT} \, dx$$

### Step 5: Collect and Cancel Terms

$$E_b = \int_0^{\infty} \frac{2\pi h c^2 \cdot x^5 \cdot (kT)^5 \cdot hc}{(hc)^5 \cdot x^2 \cdot kT \cdot [e^x - 1]} \, dx$$

$$E_b = \int_0^{\infty} \frac{2\pi (kT)^5}{(hc)^3} \cdot \frac{x^3}{[e^x - 1]} \, dx$$

### Step 6: Recognize the Standard Integral

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The integral $\int_0^{\infty} \frac{x^3}{e^x - 1} dx$ is a famous result from statistical mechanics equal to $\pi^4 / 15$.
>
> $$\int_0^{\infty} \frac{x^3}{e^x - 1} \, dx = \frac{\pi^4}{15}$$

### Step 7: Final Result — Stefan–Boltzmann Law

$$E_b = \frac{2\pi (kT)^5}{(hc)^3} \cdot \frac{\pi^4}{15} = \frac{2\pi^5 k^4}{15 h^3 c^2} T^4$$

Defining the Stefan–Boltzmann constant:

$$\sigma = \frac{2\pi^5 k^4}{15 h^3 c^2}$$

We arrive at the elegant final form:

$$\boxed{E_b = \sigma T^4}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Wien's Displacement Law

To find $\lambda_{\max}$, we differentiate $E_{b\lambda}$ with respect to $\lambda$ and set the derivative to zero.

### Step 1: Set Up the Differentiand

$$\frac{d E_{b\lambda}}{d\lambda} = 0$$

Let $C_1 = 2\pi h c^2$ (constant). Then:

$$E_{b\lambda} = \frac{C_1}{\lambda^5 (e^{hc/\lambda kT} - 1)}$$

### Step 2: Define Substitution
Let $y = \frac{hc}{\lambda k T}$, so $E_{b\lambda} = \frac{C_1 k^5 T^5}{(hc)^5} \cdot \frac{y^5}{e^y - 1}$.

### Step 3: Differentiate with Respect to $y$
At maximum, $\frac{d}{dy}\left(\frac{y^5}{e^y - 1}\right) = 0$:

$$\frac{5y^4(e^y - 1) - y^5 e^y}{(e^y - 1)^2} = 0$$

### Step 4: Simplify

$$5(e^y - 1) - y e^y = 0$$

$$5 e^y - 5 - y e^y = 0$$

$$e^y (5 - y) = 5$$

### Step 5: Solve Numerically
The transcendental equation $e^y(5 - y) = 5$ yields:

$$y = \frac{hc}{\lambda_{\max} k T} \approx 4.965$$

### Step 6: Rearrange for $\lambda_{\max}$

$$\lambda_{\max} = \frac{hc}{4.965 \, kT} = \frac{2.898 \times 10^{-3}}{T} \ \text{m·K}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Wien's Displacement Law**:
>
> $$\boxed{\lambda_{\max} \cdot T = 2.898 \times 10^{-3} \ \text{m·K}}$$

---

## 3.3 Proof of Kirchhoff's Law (Conceptual Thermodynamic Derivation)

Consider two large parallel infinite plates facing each other in vacuum, both at the **same temperature T** in thermal equilibrium.

### Step 1: Energy Balance in Equilibrium
> Since the system is in equilibrium, plate 1 must absorb exactly as much energy as it emits (otherwise it would heat up or cool down).

Let $E_1$ = emissive power of plate 1, $E_2$ = emissive power of plate 2, and $G$ = irradiation (incident radiation on plate 1 from plate 2).

### Step 2: Apply Equilibrium Condition
The net heat exchange between the two plates must be zero:

$$E_1 - \alpha_1 G = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad E_1 = \alpha_1 G$$

Similarly for plate 2:

$$E_2 = \alpha_2 G$$

### Step 3: Take the Ratio
Dividing the two equations:

$$\frac{E_1}{E_2} = \frac{\alpha_1}{\alpha_2}$$

If plate 2 is replaced by a **black body** ($\alpha_2 = 1$, $E_2 = E_b$):

$$\frac{E_1}{E_b} = \alpha_1$$

But by definition, $\varepsilon_1 = E_1 / E_b$, therefore:

$$\boxed{\varepsilon = \alpha}$$

> This is **Kirchhoff's Law** — proved for surfaces in thermal equilibrium with their surroundings.

---

## 3.4 Net Heat Transfer Between Two Infinite Parallel Gray Plates

Consider two infinite parallel plates at temperatures $T_1$ and $T_2$ ($T_1 > T_2$), with emissivities $\varepsilon_1$ and $\varepsilon_2$ and no back-side emission.

### Step 1: Define Emissive Power
$$E_1 = \varepsilon_1 \sigma T_1^4 \quad \text{(emitted by plate 1)}$$

$$E_2 = \varepsilon_2 \sigma T_2^4 \quad \text{(emitted by plate 2)}$$

### Step 2: Calculate Irradiation on Each Plate
- Plate 1 receives radiation from plate 2: $G_1 = E_2$
- Plate 2 receives radiation from plate 1: $G_2 = E_1$

### Step 3: Apply Energy Balance (Including Multiple Reflections)
After accounting for infinite reflections, the irradiation on plate 1 becomes:

$$G_1 = \frac{E_2}{1 - (1-\varepsilon_1)(1-\varepsilon_2)}$$

### Step 4: Net Heat Transfer to Plate 2
$$q = \alpha_2 G_1 - E_2 = \frac{\sigma(T_1^4 - T_2^4)}{\frac{1}{\varepsilon_1} + \frac{1}{\varepsilon_2} - 1}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Two-Plate Radiation Formula** (KTU 2024 High-Priority):
>
> $$\boxed{q_{1 \to 2} = \frac{\sigma (T_1^4 - T_2^4)}{\dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_1} + \dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_2} - 1}}$$

### Step 5: Special Case — When Both Are Black Bodies
If $\varepsilon_1 = \varepsilon_2 = 1$:

$$q = \sigma (T_1^4 - T_2^4)$$

This recovers the simple Stefan–Boltzmann form.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Classification of Bodies Based on Radiation Behavior

```mermaid

    style MR3 fill:#0891b2,stroke:#155e75,color:#ffffff
```

---

## 4.2 Radiation Energy Balance Flow for a Semi-Transparent Surface

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IN["Incoming Radiation G"] --> SPLIT{"Energy Splits"}
    
    SPLIT --> ABS["Absorbed alpha times G"]
    SPLIT --> REF["Reflected rho times G"]
    SPLIT --> TRANS["Transmitted tau times G"]
    
    ABS --> STORE["Stored as Internal Energy"]
    REF --> OUT1["Returns to Surroundings"]
    TRANS --> OUT2["Passes Through Surface"]
    
    STORE --> EMIT["Re-emitted as epsilon times sigma T4"]
    EMIT --> OUT3["Radiated to Environment"]
    
    style IN fill:#0ea5e9,stroke:#0c4a6e,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    style SPLIT fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#92400e,stroke-width:3px,color:#000000

    style REF fill:#22c55e,stroke:#14532d,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
```
