# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Emissivity
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**Question**: Define emissivity. How is it related to absorptivity for a gray body?

**Model Answer** (3 marks):

> **Definition (2 marks)**: Emissivity ($\varepsilon$) of a surface is defined as the ratio of the emissive power of the surface to the emissive power of a black body at the same temperature:
>
> $$\varepsilon = \frac{E}{E_b}$$
>
> It represents how efficiently a real surface emits thermal radiation compared to an ideal black body at the same temperature.
>
> **Relation for Gray Body (1 mark)**: By **Kirchhoff's Law**, for a gray body, the emissivity equals the absorptivity:
>
> $$\varepsilon = \alpha$$

---

### Question 2: State Stefan–Boltzmann Law
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**Question**: State the Stefan–Boltzmann law of thermal radiation. Mention its significance.

**Model Answer** (3 marks):

> **Statement (2 marks)**: The total emissive power of a black body is directly proportional to the fourth power of its absolute temperature:
>
> $$E_b = \sigma T^4$$
>
> where $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \ \text{W/m}^2\text{K}^4$ is the Stefan–Boltzmann constant.
>
> **Significance (1 mark)**: This law is the foundation for calculating radiation heat transfer between surfaces and is used in furnace design, solar collectors, and spacecraft thermal analysis.

---

## 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks Total)

### Question A
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 3 Full Question]**

> A small metal sphere of diameter 10 mm is heated to a temperature of 1500 K. If the sphere behaves as a **gray body** with emissivity $\varepsilon = 0.6$, calculate:
>
> **(a)** The total emissive power of the sphere, the radiant exitance (emitted flux per unit area), and the rate of energy emission by the sphere. (7 marks)
>
> **(b)** The wavelength at which maximum emission occurs and the spectral emissive power at this wavelength. (7 marks)

### Solution to Part A (a) — 7 Marks

**Given Data:**
- Diameter $D = 10 \ \text{mm} = 0.01 \ \text{m}$
- Temperature $T = 1500 \ \text{K}$
- Emissivity $\varepsilon = 0.6$
- Stefan–Boltzmann constant $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \ \text{W/m}^2\text{K}^4$

**Step 1: Calculate Total Emissive Power of a Black Body (1 mark)**

$$E_b = \sigma T^4 = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times (1500)^4$$

Computing $1500^4$:
$$1500^2 = 2.25 \times 10^6$$
$$1500^4 = (2.25 \times 10^6)^2 = 5.0625 \times 10^{12}$$

Therefore:
$$E_b = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times 5.0625 \times 10^{12} = 2.87 \times 10^5 \ \text{W/m}^2$$

**Step 2: Emissive Power of the Gray Body (2 marks)**

$$E = \varepsilon E_b = 0.6 \times 2.87 \times 10^5 = 1.722 \times 10^5 \ \text{W/m}^2$$

$$\boxed{E = 1.722 \times 10^5 \ \text{W/m}^2 = 172.2 \ \text{kW/m}^2}$$

**Step 3: Surface Area of the Sphere (1 mark)**

$$A = \pi D^2 = \pi \times (0.01)^2 = 3.1416 \times 10^{-4} \ \text{m}^2$$

**Step 4: Total Rate of Energy Emission (3 marks)**

$$Q = E \times A = 1.722 \times 10^5 \times 3.1416 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$\boxed{Q = 54.10 \ \text{W}}$$

---

### Solution to Part A (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1: Apply Wien's Displacement Law to Find $\lambda_{\max}$ (3 marks)**

$$\lambda_{\max} = \frac{2.898 \times 10^{-3}}{T} = \frac{2.898 \times 10^{-3}}{1500}$$

$$\boxed{\lambda_{\max} = 1.932 \times 10^{-6} \ \text{m} = 1.932 \ \mu\text{m}}$$

This is in the **infrared region**, which is consistent with the moderate temperature of 1500 K.

**Step 2: Calculate Spectral Emissive Power at $\lambda_{\max}$ (4 marks)**

Using Planck's law at $\lambda_{\max}$, we have $y = hc/(\lambda_{\max} kT) = 4.965$. The maximum spectral emissive power for a black body is:

$$E_{b\lambda_{\max}} = \frac{2\pi h c^2}{\lambda_{\max}^5 [e^{4.965} - 1]}$$

Computing each component:
- $\lambda_{\max}^5 = (1.932 \times 10^{-6})^5 = 2.689 \times 10^{-29} \ \text{m}^5$
- $e^{4.965} = 142.91$
- $e^{4.965} - 1 = 141.91$
- $2\pi h c^2 = 2\pi \times 6.626 \times 10^{-34} \times (3 \times 10^8)^2 = 3.742 \times 10^{-16} \ \text{W·m}^2$

Therefore:
$$E_{b\lambda_{\max}} = \frac{3.742 \times 10^{-16}}{2.689 \times 10^{-29} \times 141.91}$$

$$E_{b\lambda_{\max}} = \frac{3.742 \times 10^{-16}}{3.815 \times 10^{-27}}$$

$$\boxed{E_{b\lambda_{\max}} = 9.81 \times 10^{10} \ \text{W/m}^2\text{·m}}$$

For the gray body:
$$E_{\lambda_{\max}} = \varepsilon E_{b\lambda_{\max}} = 0.6 \times 9.81 \times 10^{10} = 5.886 \times 10^{10} \ \text{W/m}^2\text{·m}$$

---

## 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

### Question B
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 3 Alternate]**

> Two large parallel plates, separated by a large distance compared to their dimensions, are maintained at temperatures $T_1 = 1000 \ \text{K}$ and $T_2 = 500 \ \text{K}$. The emissivities of the two surfaces are $\varepsilon_1 = 0.8$ and $\varepsilon_2 = 0.5$ respectively. If plate 1 has a backside perfectly insulated, calculate:
>
> **(a)** The net heat transfer rate per unit area from plate 1 to plate 2, treating both surfaces as **gray bodies**. (7 marks)
>
> **(b)** Repeat the calculation assuming both surfaces are **black bodies**, and comment on the difference. (7 marks)

### Solution to Part B (a) — 7 Marks

**Given Data:**
- $T_1 = 1000 \ \text{K}$, $T_2 = 500 \ \text{K}$
- $\varepsilon_1 = 0.8$, $\varepsilon_2 = 0.5$
- $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \ \text{W/m}^2\text{K}^4$

**Step 1: Apply the Two-Plate Gray Body Formula (3 marks)**

$$q = \frac{\sigma (T_1^4 - T_2^4)}{\frac{1}{\varepsilon_1} + \frac{1}{\varepsilon_2} - 1}$$

**Step 2: Calculate $T_1^4$ and $T_2^4$ (1 mark)**

$$T_1^4 = (1000)^4 = 10^{12} \ \text{K}^4$$
$$T_2^4 = (500)^4 = 6.25 \times 10^{10} \ \text{K}^4$$

$$T_1^4 - T_2^4 = 10^{12} - 6.25 \times 10^{10} = 9.375 \times 10^{11} \ \text{K}^4$$

**Step 3: Calculate the Denominator (1 mark)**

$$\frac{1}{\varepsilon_1} + \frac{1}{\varepsilon_2} - 1 = \frac{1}{0.8} + \frac{1}{0.5} - 1 = 1.25 + 2.0 - 1 = 2.25$$

**Step 4: Final Calculation (2 marks)**

$$q = \frac{5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times 9.375 \times 10^{11}}{2.25}$$

$$q = \frac{53.156 \times 10^3}{2.25} = 23.625 \times 10^3 \ \text{W/m}^2$$

$$\boxed{q_{\text{gray}} = 23,625 \ \text{W/m}^2 = 23.63 \ \text{kW/m}^2}$$

---

### Solution to Part B (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1: Apply Stefan–Boltzmann for Black Bodies (3 marks)**

For black bodies, the simple formula applies:

$$q_{\text{black}} = \sigma (T_1^4 - T_2^4) = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times 9.375 \times 10^{11}$$

$$q_{\text{black}} = 53.156 \times 10^3 \ \text{W/m}^2$$

$$\boxed{q_{\text{black}} = 53,156 \ \text{W/m}^2 = 53.16 \ \text{kW/m}^2}$$

**Step 2: Compare the Two Results (2 marks)**

$$\text{Ratio} = \frac{q_{\text{gray}}}{q_{\text{black}}} = \frac{23.63}{53.16} = 0.4445$$

This ratio equals:

$$\frac{1}{\frac{1}{\varepsilon_1} + \frac{1}{\varepsilon_2} - 1} = \frac{1}{2.25} = 0.4445 \checkmark$$

**Step 3: Engineering Comment (2 marks)**

> **Comment**: The gray body heat transfer is **only 44.45% of the black body heat transfer** for the same temperature difference. This significant reduction occurs because real surfaces reflect a portion of incident radiation back, reducing the net exchange. The reduction factor is sometimes called the **"effective emissivity"** of the system:
>
> $$\varepsilon_{\text{eff}} = \frac{1}{\frac{1}{\varepsilon_1} + \frac{1}{\varepsilon_2} - 1} = 0.4445$$
>
> In practical applications, low emissivities are often desirable for insulation (e.g., polished aluminum foil with $\varepsilon \approx 0.05$ reduces radiation heat transfer drastically).

---

## 5.4 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark Deduction Traps in Black/Gray Body Problems**:
>
> 1. **Forgetting the $T^4$ in the energy term** — Students often write $E_b = \sigma T^3$ (a single error losing 1–2 marks).
> 2. **Unit error in $\sigma$** — Writing $5.67 \times 10^{-8} \ \text{W/m}^2\text{K}$ instead of $\text{W/m}^2\text{K}^4$ is a guaranteed 1-mark deduction.
> 3. **Mixing directional and hemispherical properties** — Hemispherical values are used in all standard KTU formulas; do NOT use $\varepsilon_\theta$ directly in $E = \varepsilon \sigma T^4$.
> 4. **Forgetting "infinite parallel plate" assumption** — The two-plate formula $q = \sigma(T_1^4 - T_2^4)/(\frac{1}{\varepsilon_1} + \frac{1}{\varepsilon_2} - 1)$ is valid only for **infinite parallel plates with no edge effects**.
> 5. **Applying $\varepsilon = \alpha$ to non-gray bodies** — Kirchhoff's identity $\varepsilon = \alpha$ holds for gray bodies only, NOT for selective (non-gray) surfaces.
> 6. **Wien's law units** — $\lambda_{\max} T = 2.898 \times 10^{-3} \ \text{m·K}$ — if you use $\mu\text{m·K}$ the constant becomes $2898$.

---

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**
>
> ☑ **Black body** has $\varepsilon = 1$, $\alpha = 1$, $\rho = 0$, $\tau = 0$ (opaque by default)
>
> ☑ **Gray body** has constant $\varepsilon$ and $\alpha$ at all wavelengths, both less than 1
>
> ☑ **Stefan–Boltzmann Law**: $E_b = \sigma T^4$, where $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \ \text{W/m}^2\text{K}^4$
>
> ☑ **Wien's Displacement Law**: $\lambda_{\max} \cdot T = 2.898 \times 10^{-3} \ \text{m·K}$ (note: meter-kelvin)
>
> ☑ **Kirchhoff's Law**: $\varepsilon = \alpha$ for gray body in thermal equilibrium
>
> ☑ **Energy balance**: $\alpha + \rho + \tau = 1$; for opaque: $\alpha + \rho = 1$
>
> ☑ **Two-plate gray body formula**: $q = \frac{\sigma (T_1^4 - T_2^4)}{\frac{1}{\varepsilon_1} + \frac{1}{\varepsilon_2} - 1}$ — only for **infinite** parallel plates
>
> ☑ **Gray body emissive power**: $E = \varepsilon E_b = \varepsilon \sigma T^4$
>
> ☑ **Planck's constants** to remember: $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s, $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s, $k = 1.381 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K
>
> ☑ **Lower emissivity** → less radiation emission and less absorption (used in insulation, e.g., aluminum foil)
>
> ☑ **Higher emissivity** → more radiation emission and more absorption (e.g., black-painted surfaces, refractory furnace linings)
>
> ☑ **Selective surfaces** (non-gray) have $\alpha/\varepsilon$ ratio > 1 — used in solar collectors to absorb sun and minimize re-radiation
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Radiosity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET754 - HEAT AND MASS TRANSFER
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Radiosity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Radiosity – Core Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Radiosity (J)** is defined as the **total radiation energy leaving a surface per unit area per unit time**. It is the *sum total* of two distinct physical contributions emerging from any real surface:

$$
J = \underbrace{\varepsilon \, E_b}_{\text{Emitted part}} + \underbrace{\rho \, G}_{\text{Reflected part}}
$$

Where:
- $\varepsilon$ = **emissivity** of the surface (dimensionless, $0 \le \varepsilon \le 1$)
- $E_b = \sigma T^4$ = **blackbody emissive power** (W/m²)
- $\sigma = \mathbf{5.67 \times 10^{-8} \, W/m^2 \cdot K^4}$ = **Stefan-Boltzmann constant**
- $\rho$ = **reflectivity** of the surface (dimensionless)
- $G$ = **irradiation** — total radiation incident on the surface per unit area per unit time (W/m²)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Radiosity is NOT the same as Emissive Power (E).** Emissive power accounts *only* for what the surface emits. Radiosity accounts for what the surface emits **PLUS** what it reflects back into the enclosure. It is also called the **"Total Leaving Flux Density"** or **"Radiosity"**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy – The "Loud Speaker with Microphone" Model

Imagine a **concert hall stage**:
- A spotlight shines on the actor (this is like **irradiation G** falling on a surface).
- The actor's body emits its own body heat (this is the **emitted radiation $\varepsilon E_b$**).
- Some of the spotlight light bounces off the actor's costume back into the hall (this is the **reflected radiation $\rho G$**).

**Radiosity (J)** is what an infrared camera would "see" leaving the actor's body toward the hall — it captures **both** the actor's own thermal emission **and** the reflected stage light. The radiosity is what the rest of the enclosure actually *receives* from that surface.

## 1.3 Special Case: Diffuse, Gray, and Opaque Surfaces

For most engineering surfaces in KTU problems, we assume the surface is:
1. **Diffuse** – radiation is uniform in all directions
2. **Gray** – $\varepsilon$ and $\alpha$ are independent of wavelength
3. **Opaque** – transmissivity $\tau = 0$

By energy balance: $\alpha + \rho + \tau = 1$. With $\tau = 0$ and $\alpha = \varepsilon$ (Kirchhoff's law for gray surfaces):

$$
J = \varepsilon \, E_b + (1 - \varepsilon) \, G
$$

This is the **workhorse equation** used in nearly every KTU exam problem.

> [!NOTE]
> **Sign Convention Reminder:** All quantities ($J$, $G$, $E_b$, $q$) are taken as **positive magnitudes** in KTU convention. The *direction* of heat flow is determined by algebraic sign in the network equations, not by inserting negative numbers into these basic formulas.

---

## 1.4 Why is Radiosity Important?

In multi-surface enclosures (furnaces, solar collectors, boiler fireboxes), every surface *both emits and reflects*. Without the radiosity concept, the **reflected radiation** must be tracked as a separate energy stream in every calculation — making problems nearly unsolvable. **Radiosity collapses emission and reflection into one single outgoing flux**, enabling elegant **electrical-network analogy** solutions.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Physical Interpretation – Step-by-Step Logic

The derivation of the radiosity concept follows a strict logical chain required by KTU board answers:

- **Step 1:** Every real surface with temperature $T > 0$ emits thermal radiation equal to $\varepsilon \, \sigma \, T^4 = \varepsilon \, E_b$.
- **Step 2:** Simultaneously, the surface receives irradiation $G$ from surrounding surfaces and external sources.
- **Step 3:** A fraction $\rho$ of this incident radiation is reflected back out into the enclosure (since the surface is opaque, no transmission).
- **Step 4:** A fraction $\alpha = \varepsilon$ (for gray surfaces) is absorbed and re-emitted, but this is already included in $\varepsilon E_b$.
- **Step 5:** The **total energy leaving** the surface per unit area = emitted + reflected = $\varepsilon E_b + \rho G$.
- **Step 6:** Define this total as **Radiosity $J$** — a single, convenient variable.

## 2.2 The Three Key Relations in KTU Network Method

Once $J$ is defined, the **net radiation leaving a surface** $q_i$ (W/m²) becomes beautifully simple:

$$
q_i = J_i - G_i
$$

Since $G_i$ cannot be measured directly, we eliminate it. From the radiosity equation solved for $G$:

$$
G_i = \frac{J_i - \varepsilon_i \, E_{b,i}}{1 - \varepsilon_i}
$$

Substituting back gives the **KTU's most-used radiosity relation**:

$$
q_i = \frac{\varepsilon_i}{1 - \varepsilon_i} \, \left( E_{b,i} - J_i \right)
$$

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Name / Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $J = \varepsilon E_b + \rho G$ | **General Radiosity Definition** | W/m² |
| 2 | $J = \varepsilon E_b + (1-\varepsilon)G$ | Radiosity (diffuse, gray, opaque) | W/m² |
| 3 | $E_b = \sigma T^4$ | Blackbody emissive power | W/m² |
| 4 | $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ | Stefan–Boltzmann constant | W/m²·K⁴ |
| 5 | $q = J - G$ | Net heat flux leaving surface | W/m² |
| 6 | $q = \dfrac{\varepsilon}{1-\varepsilon}(E_b - J)$ | Net heat flux in terms of J and $E_b$ | W/m² |
| 7 | $R_{surf} = \dfrac{1-\varepsilon}{\varepsilon A}$ | **Surface Resistance** | m⁻² |
| 8 | $R_{space} = \dfrac{1}{A_i F_{ij}}$ | **Space (Geometric) Resistance** | m⁻² |
| 9 | $Q = \dfrac{E_b - J}{R_{surf}}$ | Heat flow analogous to Ohm's law | W |
| 10 | $\alpha + \rho + \tau = 1$ | Energy balance on incident radiation | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Trick:** In the network method, $E_b$ behaves like a **voltage source**, $J$ behaves like a **node voltage**, and resistances are actual resistors. The analogy is *exact*, allowing KTU students to use nodal/Kirchhoff analysis directly.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Radiosity network analysis is the design backbone of:
- **Furnace and boiler** internal lining optimization
- **Solar collector** absorber plate design
- **Thermal protection tiles** of spacecraft re-entry vehicles
- **Building energy simulation** (heat gain through walls, ceilings)
- **Glass furnace** radiative cooling analysis
- **Compact heat exchangers** with low-emissivity coatings

The concept allows engineers to compute the steady-state temperature distribution in an enclosure without solving a full integral equation.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 – Radiosity from Energy Balance (Opaque Gray Surface)

Consider a small elemental area $dA$ on a diffuse, gray, opaque surface at temperature $T$.

**Step 1 — Incoming radiation (irradiation $G$):**
By definition, irradiation $G$ (W/m²) is the total radiant flux incident on the surface from *all* directions.

**Step 2 — Energy partitioning:**
For an opaque surface, $\tau = 0$. From energy conservation on incident radiation:

$$
\alpha + \rho = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \rho = 1 - \alpha
$$

For a **gray surface**, Kirchhoff's law gives $\alpha = \varepsilon$. Therefore:

$$
\rho = 1 - \varepsilon
$$

**Step 3 — Reflected flux:**
The portion of $G$ reflected back into space is:

$$
\text{Reflected flux} = \rho \, G = (1 - \varepsilon) \, G
$$

**Step 4 — Emitted flux:**
The portion emitted thermally by the surface at temperature $T$ is:

$$
\text{Emitted flux} = \varepsilon \, E_b = \varepsilon \, \sigma \, T^4
$$

**Step 5 — Total leaving flux = Radiosity:**

$$
\boxed{J = \varepsilon \, \sigma \, T^4 + (1 - \varepsilon) \, G}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2 – Net Heat Flux in Terms of $E_b$ and $J$

This is the **most-frequently-asked 7-mark derivation** in KTU Module 3 exams.

**Step 1 — Start from the radiosity definition:**

$$
J = \varepsilon \, E_b + (1 - \varepsilon) \, G
$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $G$:**
Subtract $\varepsilon E_b$ from both sides:

$$
J - \varepsilon \, E_b = (1 - \varepsilon) \, G
$$

Divide by $(1 - \varepsilon)$:

$$
G = \frac{J - \varepsilon \, E_b}{1 - \varepsilon}
$$

**Step 3 — Net radiation leaving the surface:**

$$
q = J - G
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the expression for $G$:**

$$
q = J - \frac{J - \varepsilon \, E_b}{1 - \varepsilon}
$$

**Step 5 — Common denominator $(1 - \varepsilon)$:**

$$
q = \frac{J(1 - \varepsilon) - (J - \varepsilon E_b)}{1 - \varepsilon}
$$

**Step 6 — Expand numerator:**

$$
q = \frac{J - \varepsilon J - J + \varepsilon E_b}{1 - \varepsilon}
$$

The $J$ terms cancel:

$$
q = \frac{\varepsilon E_b - \varepsilon J}{1 - \varepsilon}
$$

**Step 7 — Final factored form:**

$$
\boxed{q = \frac{\varepsilon}{1 - \varepsilon} \left( E_b - J \right)}
$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 – Resistance Form for Network Analysis

**Step 1 — Multiply both sides of the final $q$ equation by area $A$:**

$$
Q = A \, q = \frac{\varepsilon \, A}{1 - \varepsilon} \, \left( E_b - J \right)
$$

**Step 2 — Rearrange to resemble Ohm's law ($V = IR$):**

$$
Q = \frac{E_b - J}{\dfrac{1 - \varepsilon}{\varepsilon A}}
$$

**Step 3 — Identify the surface resistance:**

$$
\boxed{R_{surf} = \frac{1 - \varepsilon}{\varepsilon A} \quad \text{(units: m}^{-2}\text{)}}
$$

Thus, $Q = \dfrac{\Delta(\text{potential})}{R_{surf}}$, exactly analogous to $I = \dfrac{V}{R}$.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix – Quick-Reference for Problem-Solving

| Given in Question | Use This Radiosity Form | Apply This Step |
|---|---|---|
| $T$, $\varepsilon$ of a surface alone | $J = \varepsilon \sigma T^4$ | Vacuum surface ($\rho G = 0$) |
| $G$ and $\varepsilon$ given | $J = \varepsilon E_b + (1-\varepsilon)G$ | Direct substitution |
| Need $q_{net}$ leaving | $q = J - G$ | Compute $G$ first if absent |
| $E_b$ and $J$ given | $q = \dfrac{\varepsilon}{1-\varepsilon}(E_b - J)$ | Best for KTU board problems |
| Network/Enclosure problem | $R_{surf} = \dfrac{1-\varepsilon}{\varepsilon A}$ | Pair with $R_{space}$ and nodal analysis |

## 3.5 Comprehensive Solved Example – KTU Style (14-Mark Format)

**Problem Statement:**
Two large parallel plates form an enclosure. Plate 1 (emissivity $\varepsilon_1 = 0.8$, $T_1 = 1000$ K) and Plate 2 ($\varepsilon_2 = 0.5$, $T_2 = 500$ K). The view factor $F_{12} = 1$. Determine:
**(a)** The radiosity of each plate.
**(b)** The net heat transfer from Plate 1 to Plate 2.

**Given Data:**
- $\varepsilon_1 = 0.8$, $T_1 = 1000$ K
- $\varepsilon_2 = 0.5$, $T_2 = 500$ K
- $F_{12} = F_{21} = 1$ (parallel infinite plates)

**Step 1 — Compute blackbody emissive powers:**

$$
E_{b,1} = \sigma T_1^4 = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times (1000)^4 = 56{,}700 \text{ W/m}^2
$$

$$
E_{b,2} = \sigma T_2^4 = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times (500)^4 = 3{,}543.75 \text{ W/m}^2
$$

**Step 2 — Set up radiosity equations:**

For an enclosure with two surfaces, each surface's irradiation equals the radiosity of the *other* surface (since $F = 1$):

$$
G_1 = J_2, \quad G_2 = J_1
$$

**Step 3 — Write the two radiosity equations:**

$$
J_1 = \varepsilon_1 E_{b,1} + (1 - \varepsilon_1) J_2
$$

$$
J_2 = \varepsilon_2 E_{b,2} + (1 - \varepsilon_2) J_1
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute numerical values:**

$$
J_1 = 0.8 \times 56700 + 0.2 \times J_2 = 45360 + 0.2 J_2
$$

$$
J_2 = 0.5 \times 3543.75 + 0.5 \times J_1 = 1771.875 + 0.5 J_1
$$

**Step 5 — Solve simultaneously.**
Substitute the second equation into the first:

$$
J_1 = 45360 + 0.2 \left( 1771.875 + 0.5 J_1 \right)
$$

$$
J_1 = 45360 + 354.375 + 0.1 J_1
$$

$$
0.9 J_1 = 45714.375
$$

$$
\boxed{J_1 = 50{,}793.75 \text{ W/m}^2}
$$

Back-substitute:

$$
J_2 = 1771.875 + 0.5 \times 50793.75 = 1771.875 + 25396.875
$$

$$
\boxed{J_2 = 27{,}168.75 \text{ W/m}^2}
$$

**Step 6 — Net heat flux from Plate 1:**

$$
q_1 = \frac{\varepsilon_1}{1 - \varepsilon_1}\left( E_{b,1} - J_1 \right)
$$

$$
q_1 = \frac{0.8}{0.2}\left( 56700 - 50793.75 \right) = 4 \times 5906.25
$$

$$
\boxed{q_1 = 23{,}625 \text{ W/m}^2}
$$

**Verification — Energy balance:**
$q_2 = -q_1$ (heat lost by 1 = heat gained by 2):

$$
q_2 = \frac{0.5}{0.5}(3543.75 - 27168.75) = -23{,}625 \text{ W/m}^2 \checkmark
$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Concept Map – Radiosity Energy Flow

The following block diagram shows the **functional flow of energy** at a typical gray, opaque, diffuse surface:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Other Surfaces in Enclosure] -->|Irradiation G| nodeB[Surface dA, Temperature T]
    nodeB -->|Reflected: rho times G| nodeC[Total Leaving Flux Density J]
    nodeB -->|Emitted: epsilon times Eb| nodeC
    nodeC -->|Reaches other surfaces| nodeA
    nodeB -.->|Absorbed fraction alpha times G| nodeD[Internal Energy of Surface]
```

**How to read this diagram:**
- Solid arrows show the **two contributions** that combine to form Radiosity $J$: emitted radiation $\varepsilon E_b$ and reflected radiation $\rho G$.
- The dashed arrow shows the absorbed portion ($\alpha G$) that increases surface internal energy — this does *not* contribute to $J$.

## 4.2 Network Analogy – Radiosity as a Circuit

In the **electrical-network analogy** (used in KTU's radiation enclosure method), radiosity $J$ plays the role of **node voltage**:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    srcA["Eb1 (Voltage Source)"] --- rA["R surf 1 = (1-eps1)/(eps1*A1)"]
    jA["J1 (Node)"] --- rSpA["R space 12 = 1/(A1*F12)"]
    jA --- rB["R surf 2 = (1-eps2)/(eps2*A2)"]
    rSpA --- jB["J2 (Node)"]
    jB --- srcB["Eb2 (Voltage Source)"]
```

**Reading guide:**
- **$E_b$** values act as **EMF sources** (battery voltages).
- **$J$** values are **node potentials** at each surface.
- **$R_{surf}$** is the resistance *inside* each surface (depends only on $\varepsilon$ and $A$).
- **$R_{space}$** is the resistance *between* surfaces (depends on geometry and view factor).
- Heat flow $Q$ between any two nodes follows Ohm's law: $Q = \dfrac{J_i - J_j}{R}$.

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture – Multi-Surface Enclosure

For an enclosure with **N surfaces**, the radiosity network can be generalized:

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Enclosure [N Surface Radiation Enclosure]
        S1["Surface 1: Eb1, eps1"]
        S2["Surface 2: Eb2, eps2"]
        S3["Surface 3: Eb3, eps3"]
        SN["Surface N: EbN, epsN"]
    end
    S1 -->|R surf 1| J1["J1 Node"]
    S2 -->|R surf 2| J2["J2 Node"]
    S3 -->|R surf 3| J3["J3 Node"]
    SN -->|R surf N| JN["JN Node"]
    J1 ---|R space ij| J2
    J2 ---|R space jk| J3
    J3 ---|R space kN| JN
    J1 ---|R space iN| JN
```

**KTU Exam Application:** For a two-surface problem, write **2 nodal equations** (one per $J$). For three surfaces, write **3 equations**. The system is solved simultaneously, then $q$ at any surface is obtained from the $E_b - J$ drop across its $R_{surf}$.

## 4.4 Decision Flowchart – Which Radiosity Form to Use

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Given Surface Data] --> Q1{Surface Opaque?}
    Q1 -->|No| Skip[Use full radiation balance, J = eps Eb plus rho G]
    Q1 -->|Yes| Q2{Surface Gray?}
    Q2 -->|No| Wavelength[Use spectral integration not needed in KTU]
    Q2 -->|Yes| Q3{Know G or J other surface?}
    Q3 -->|Know G| Use1[Use J = eps Eb plus 1 minus eps times G]
    Q3 -->|Know J other surface| Use2[Use J = eps Eb plus 1 minus eps times J_other for F equals 1]
    Q3 -->|Need q directly| Use3[Use q = eps divided by 1 minus eps times Eb minus J]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define Radiosity. How does it differ from emissive power?**

**Model Answer:**
Radiosity $J$ is defined as the **total rate of radiation energy leaving a surface per unit area per unit time**, accounting for both **thermal emission** and **reflection** of incident irradiation. Mathematically:

$$
J = \varepsilon \, E_b + \rho \, G
$$

Emissive power $E$ represents only the radiation **emitted by the surface** itself ($E = \varepsilon E_b$ for a real gray surface). The key difference is that **radiosity includes the reflected component** ($\rho G$), whereas emissive power does not. Radiosity is also called the *total leaving flux density* and is essential when a surface exchanges radiation with other surfaces in an enclosure.

---

### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**State Kirchhoff's law as applicable to radiosity formulation.**

**Model Answer:**
Kirchhoff's law for a surface in thermal equilibrium states that **the emissivity of a surface equals its absorptivity** at every wavelength:

$$
\varepsilon_\lambda = \alpha_\lambda
$$

For a **gray surface** (where this holds at all wavelengths), $\varepsilon = \alpha$. Combined with the energy balance $\alpha + \rho + \tau = 1$, and assuming an opaque surface ($\tau = 0$), this yields $\rho = 1 - \varepsilon$, which is the substitution used to simplify the radiosity equation into its gray-surface form $J = \varepsilon E_b + (1 - \varepsilon) G$.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Module Choice Questions

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Read Carefully!**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse $J$ with $E$ or $E_b$.** Many students write $q = \varepsilon \sigma T^4 - G$ which is wrong — they forget the reflected term.
> 2. **Do NOT drop the factor $(1 - \varepsilon)$.** Omitting it loses 2 marks immediately.
> 3. **Sign convention:** $q$ is **positive** when leaving the surface, **negative** when entering. Mark examiners will deduct for confused signs.
> 4. **Units:** Always include W/m² for flux and K⁴ inside $T^4$. A naked number without units loses a mark.

### Question A (Choice 1) – 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive an expression for the net heat flux leaving a diffuse, gray, opaque surface in terms of its emissivity $\varepsilon$, blackbody emissive power $E_b$, and radiosity $J$.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Two large parallel plates, 1 m apart, have emissivities $\varepsilon_1 = 0.3$ and $\varepsilon_2 = 0.8$ with temperatures $T_1 = 800$ K and $T_2 = 300$ K respectively. The view factor is $F_{12} = 1$. Using the radiosity method, compute the net heat transfer per unit area from plate 1 to plate 2.

---

**Model Solution (a):**

Starting from the definition of radiosity for a diffuse, gray, opaque surface:

$$
J = \varepsilon E_b + (1 - \varepsilon) G
$$

Solving for $G$:

$$
G = \frac{J - \varepsilon E_b}{1 - \varepsilon}
$$

The net radiation leaving the surface is the difference between outgoing radiosity and incoming irradiation:

$$
q = J - G
$$

Substituting:

$$
q = J - \frac{J - \varepsilon E_b}{1 - \varepsilon}
$$

Common denominator $(1 - \varepsilon)$:

$$
q = \frac{J(1 - \varepsilon) - J + \varepsilon E_b}{1 - \varepsilon}
$$

Simplifying the numerator:

$$
q = \frac{J - \varepsilon J - J + \varepsilon E_b}{1 - \varepsilon} = \frac{\varepsilon E_b - \varepsilon J}{1 - \varepsilon}
$$

Factorising:

$$
\boxed{q = \frac{\varepsilon}{1 - \varepsilon}\left( E_b - J \right)}
$$

Multiplying by area gives:

$$
Q = \frac{E_b - J}{(1 - \varepsilon)/(\varepsilon A)} = \frac{E_b - J}{R_{surf}}
$$

This is the standard form used in radiation network analysis.

---

**Model Solution (b):**

**Given:**
- $\varepsilon_1 = 0.3$, $T_1 = 800$ K
- $\varepsilon_2 = 0.8$, $T_2 = 300$ K
- $F_{12} = 1$

**Step 1 — Blackbody emissive powers:**

$$
E_{b,1} = \sigma T_1^4 = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times (800)^4 = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times 4.096 \times 10^{11}
$$

$$
E_{b,1} = 23{,}222.4 \text{ W/m}^2
$$

$$
E_{b,2} = \sigma T_2^4 = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times (300)^4 = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times 8.1 \times 10^{9}
$$

$$
E_{b,2} = 459.27 \text{ W/m}^2
$$

**Step 2 — Apply radiosity equations:**
Since $F_{12} = 1$, the irradiation on each plate equals the radiosity of the other:

$$
J_1 = \varepsilon_1 E_{b,1} + (1 - \varepsilon_1) J_2
$$

$$
J_2 = \varepsilon_2 E_{b,2} + (1 - \varepsilon_2) J_1
$$

Substituting:

$$
J_1 = 0.3 \times 23222.4 + 0.7 J_2 = 6966.72 + 0.7 J_2 \quad \ldots (1)
$$

$$
J_2 = 0.8 \times 459.27 + 0.2 J_1 = 367.416 + 0.2 J_1 \quad \ldots (2)
$$

**Step 3 — Solve simultaneously:**
Substitute (2) into (1):

$$
J_1 = 6966.72 + 0.7 (367.416 + 0.2 J_1)
$$

$$
J_1 = 6966.72 + 257.19 + 0.14 J_1
$$

$$
0.86 J_1 = 7223.91
$$

$$
J_1 = 8399.90 \text{ W/m}^2
$$

From (2):

$$
J_2 = 367.416 + 0.2 \times 8399.90 = 367.416 + 1679.98 = 2047.40 \text{ W/m}^2
$$

**Step 4 — Net heat flux from Plate 1:**

$$
q_1 = \frac{\varepsilon_1}{1 - \varepsilon_1}\left( E_{b,1} - J_1 \right) = \frac{0.3}{0.7}\left( 23222.4 - 8399.90 \right)
$$

$$
q_1 = 0.4286 \times 14822.5 = 6352.5 \text{ W/m}^2
$$

**Verification:**

$$
q_2 = \frac{0.8}{0.2}(459.27 - 2047.40) = 4 \times (-1588.13) = -6352.5 \text{ W/m}^2
$$

The energy balance $q_1 = -q_2$ is satisfied, confirming the answer.

$$
\boxed{q_{1\to 2} = 6352.5 \text{ W/m}^2}
$$

---

### Question B (Choice 2) – 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat sketch, explain the concept of radiosity. Show that for an opaque, diffuse, gray surface, $J = \varepsilon E_b + (1 - \varepsilon) G$.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A furnace cavity consists of three surfaces. Surfaces 1 and 2 are small compared to surface 3, and both face surface 3. Given $A_3 = 10 \text{ m}^2$, $T_3 = 600$ K, $\varepsilon_3 = 0.7$, and surface 1 and 2 are reradiating ($q_1 = q_2 = 0$). The reradiating surfaces are at $T_1 = 800$ K, $T_2 = 900$ K$ with $\varepsilon_1 = 0.5$, $\varepsilon_2 = 0.6$. Compute the heat transfer rate to surface 3.

*(Simplified two-reradiating-surface problem solvable by standard radiosity method.)*

---

**Model Solution (a):**

[Sketch description] Consider a small elemental area $dA$ on a surface at temperature $T$. It receives irradiation $G$ from all surrounding sources. By Kirchhoff's law for a gray surface, $\alpha = \varepsilon$. Since the surface is opaque, $\tau = 0$, hence $\rho = 1 - \alpha = 1 - \varepsilon$.

The reflected flux per unit area is $\rho G = (1 - \varepsilon) G$, and the emitted flux per unit area is $\varepsilon E_b = \varepsilon \sigma T^4$. Summing these two contributions gives the total flux leaving the surface:

$$
\boxed{J = \varepsilon E_b + (1 - \varepsilon) G}
$$

**Model Solution (b) — Outline:**

For a reradiating surface, $q = 0$, so $E_b = J$ (from $q = \dfrac{\varepsilon}{1-\varepsilon}(E_b - J) = 0$). Therefore:

$$
J_1 = \sigma T_1^4 = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times (800)^4 = 23{,}222.4 \text{ W/m}^2
$$

$$
J_2 = \sigma T_2^4 = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times (900)^4 = 37{,}135.65 \text{ W/m}^2
$$

The net heat transfer to surface 3 (per unit area) is found from an overall energy balance on the furnace: heat from sources = heat absorbed by surface 3. Detailed network setup is left as standard KTU procedure for the student to complete.

$$
\boxed{q_3 = \frac{E_{b,3} - J_3}{(1 - \varepsilon_3)/(\varepsilon_3 A_3)}}
$$

where $J_3$ is obtained by solving the nodal equation for surface 3 involving $J_1$, $J_2$ and the view factor $F_{31}, F_{32}$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Radiosity = Total Leaving Flux Density** = Emitted + Reflected ($J = \varepsilon E_b + \rho G$).
- ✅ For **diffuse, gray, opaque** surfaces: $J = \varepsilon E_b + (1 - \varepsilon) G$.
- ✅ **Net heat flux leaving a surface**: $q = \dfrac{\varepsilon}{1 - \varepsilon}\left( E_b - J \right)$.
- ✅ **Surface resistance** in network analogy: $R_{surf} = \dfrac{1 - \varepsilon}{\varepsilon A}$.
- ✅ **Space resistance** between two surfaces: $R_{space} = \dfrac{1}{A_i F_{ij}}$.
- ✅ **Stefan–Boltzmann constant**: $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ W/m²·K⁴ — must be remembered exactly.
- ✅ If a surface is **reradiating** ($q = 0$), then $J = E_b = \sigma T^4$.
- ✅ **Sign convention**: $q > 0$ means heat leaves the surface; $q < 0$ means heat enters.
- ✅ For **two-surface enclosures with $F = 1$**: each surface's irradiation equals the *other's* radiosity.
- ✅ Always **state assumptions** at the start of a solution (diffuse, gray, opaque, steady state).
- ✅ Always carry **units** throughout the calculation — naked numbers lose marks.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute KTU Board Strategy:**
> For a 14-mark question, allocate ~3 marks for the sketch, ~4 marks for derivation steps, and ~7 marks for the numerical solution with units. Examiners reward **clearly labeled diagrams** and **stepwise algebra** far more than a single final answer.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: View Factor Relations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPET754 - HEAT AND MASS TRANSFER > - **Module:** Module Module 3 > - **Topic:** View Factor Relations # MODULE 3 — VIEW FACTOR RELATIONS ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **View Factor (Configuration Factor / Shape Factor / Geometric Factor)** $F_{i \to j}$ is defined as the fraction of the total radiation **leaving surface $i$** in all directions that **directly strikes surface $j$**. It is a purely **geometric** quantity — independent of surface temperature, emissivity, or any thermophysical property. Mathematically, for two finite, diffuse, opaque surfaces $A_1$ and $A_2$: $$F_{1 \to 2} = \frac{1}{A_1} \int_{A_1}\int_{A_2} \frac{\cos\theta_1 \cos\theta_2}{\pi r^2}\, dA_2\, dA_1$$ where: - $r$ = distance between elemental areas $dA_1$ and $dA_2$ - $\theta_1$ = angle between normal to $dA_1$ and the line connecting $dA_1$–$dA_2$ - $\theta_2$ = angle between normal to $dA_2$ and the line connecting $dA_2$–$dA_1$ **Notation convention used throughout KTU board papers:** $F_{ij} \equiv F_{i \to j}$ — the "from" surface is the first subscript. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are standing inside a dark room holding a glowing light bulb at point P. You spray the entire room uniformly with photons. Out of every **100 photons** you emit, suppose **35** hit the mirror on the north wall, **20** hit the window, and **45** get absorbed or escape. The view factor $F_{P \to \text{mirror}}$ = **0.35**. > [!NOTE] > The view factor is essentially the **"geometric probability"** that a photon leaving one surface will reach another specific surface. Since it depends only on geometry (shapes, sizes, orientations, separation distance), it is computed **once** for a given configuration and reused for any temperature or emissivity. ### 1.3 Standard Constants and Metrics | Quantity | Symbol | Value | |----------|--------|-------| | Stefan–Boltzmann Constant | $\sigma$ | **$5.67 \times 10^{-8}\ \mathrm{W/m^2 \cdot K^4}$** | | View Factor | $F_{ij}$ | Dimensionless, $0 \le F_{ij} \le 1$ | | Hemispherical Emissive Power | $E_b$ | $\sigma T^4$ in $\mathrm{W/m^2}$ | ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Four Cardinal View Factor Relations These four relations form the **backbone of all radiation network problems** in the KTU syllabus. Memorize them at the cost of your life. #### (A) Summation Rule (Closure Rule) For a surface $i$ inside a **closed enclosure** of $N$ surfaces: $$\sum_{j=1}^{N} F_{i \to j} = 1$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > All radiation leaving surface $i$ **must** strike some other surface in a closed enclosure. Hence the sum is exactly unity. KTU frequently tests this on a two-surface enclosure: $F_{12} + F_{11} = 1$. #### (B) Reciprocity Relation (Mutuality Theorem) $$A_i F_{i \to j} = A_j F_{j \to i}$$ > [!NOTE] > This is **THE** most-used relation in KTU problems. It allows you to compute the *hard* view factor from the *easy* one. KTU board problems almost always involve two surfaces of unequal area — reciprocity is mandatory. #### (C) Superposition Rule For a composite surface $k = (j + m)$: $$A_i F_{i \to k} = A_j F_{i \to j} + A_m F_{i \to m}$$ $$F_{i \to k} = \frac{A_j F_{i \to j} + A_m F_{i \to m}}{A_j + A_m}$$ #### (D) Symmetry Rule If two surfaces are **geometrically identical and symmetrically positioned** (e.g., two equal parallel plates): $$F_{i \to j} = F_{j \to i}$$ ### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Relation / Formula | Mathematical Statement | Application Scenario | |---|---------------------|------------------------|---------------------| | 1 | Summation Rule (closed enclosure) | $\sum_{j} F_{i \to j} = 1$ | Verify radiation balance | | 2 | Reciprocity | $A_i F_{i \to j} = A_j F_{j \to i}$ | Convert $F_{12} \leftrightarrow F_{21}$ | | 3 | Superposition | $A_i F_{i \to (j+m)} = A_j F_{i \to j} + A_m F_{i \to m}$ | Composite receiving surfaces | | 4 | Symmetry | $F_{ij} = F_{ji}$ for equal areas | Parallel plates, concentric spheres | | 5 | Two-surface enclosure | $F_{11} + F_{12} = 1$ and $A_1 F_{12} = A_2 F_{21}$ | Furnace-type problems | | 6 | View factor integral | $F_{1 \to 2} = \dfrac{1}{A_1}\!\int_{A_1}\!\int_{A_2}\!\dfrac{\cos\theta_1\cos\theta_2}{\pi r^2}\,dA_2\,dA_1$ | Analytical derivation | | 7 | Hottel's crossed strings (2D) | $F_{1 \to 2} = \dfrac{\sum \text{crossed} - \sum \text{uncrossed}}{2\,L_1}$ | Parallel plates, 2D geometries | | 8 | Infinite parallel plates | $F_{1 \to 2} = 1$ if $A_1 \le A_2$ and they face each other | Very common KTU problem | | 9 | Concentric cylinders/spheres (outer sees inner) | $F_{2 \to 1} = 1$ | Inner fully enclosed | | 10 | Concentric cylinders/spheres (inner sees outer) | $F_{1 \to 2} = 1$ | Inner fully enclosed | ### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Furnace & Boiler Design:** Radiant section heat transfer between flame, tube banks, and refractory walls. - **Solar Concentrators:** Computing energy captured by a receiver from a heliostat field. - **Building Energy Analysis:** Radiation exchange between glass facades and interior walls. - **Aerospace:** Re-entry vehicle thermal protection — radiation to surroundings. - **Electronics Cooling:** Radiation between closely spaced circuit boards. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of the View Factor Integral **Step 1 — Radiation intensity concept.** The intensity of radiation $I_b$ leaving a diffuse surface in any direction is: $$I_b = \frac{E_b}{\pi} = \frac{\sigma T^4}{\pi}$$ **Step 2 — Radiative flux leaving $dA_1$ toward $dA_2$.** The element $dA_1$ emits isotropically. The solid angle subtended by $dA_2$ at $dA_1$ is: $$d\omega_{2-1} = \frac{\cos\theta_2\, dA_2}{r^2}$$ **Step 3 — Energy leaving $dA_1$ toward $dA_2$.** $$dQ_{1 \to 2} = I_b \cos\theta_1\, dA_1\, d\omega_{2-1} = I_b \frac{\cos\theta_1 \cos\theta_2}{r^2}\, dA_1\, dA_2$$ **Step 4 — Total energy leaving $dA_1$ to all of $A_2$.** Integrate over $A_2$: $$dQ_{1 \to 2,\text{total}} = dA_1 \int_{A_2} I_b \frac{\cos\theta_1 \cos\theta_2}{r^2}\, dA_2$$ **Step 5 — Total radiative flux from $A_1$.** For a diffuse black surface, total emissive power integrated over a hemisphere is $E_b = \pi I_b$. The total rate leaving $dA_1$ is: $$dQ_{1,\text{total}} = E_b\, dA_1 = \pi I_b\, dA_1$$ **Step 6 — Define view factor.** $$F_{1 \to 2} = \frac{dQ_{1 \to 2,\text{total}}}{dQ_{1,\text{total}}} = \frac{1}{\pi A_1}\int_{A_1}\int_{A_2} \frac{\cos\theta_1\cos\theta_2}{r^2}\, dA_2\, dA_1$$ $$\boxed{F_{1 \to 2} = \frac{1}{A_1}\int_{A_1}\int_{A_2} \frac{\cos\theta_1 \cos\theta_2}{\pi r^2}\, dA_2\, dA_1}$$ ### 3.2 Derivation of the Reciprocity Relation **Step 1 — Energy interchange between $dA_1$ and $dA_2$.** $$d^2Q_{1 \to 2} = I_{b1} \frac{\cos\theta_1 \cos\theta_2}{r^2}\, dA_1\, dA_2$$ **Step 2 — Energy interchange in the reverse direction.** $$d^2Q_{2 \to 1} = I_{b2} \frac{\cos\theta_1 \cos\theta_2}{r^2}\, dA_2\, dA_1$$ **Step 3 — Equate the geometric kernel.** Since $I_{b1}$ and $I_{b2}$ are both multiplied by the *same* geometric term: $$\frac{d^2Q_{1 \to 2}}{I_{b1}\, dA_1} = \frac{d^2Q_{2 \to 1}}{I_{b2}\, dA_2}$$ **Step 4 — Integrate over both surfaces.** $$\int_{A_1}\int_{A_2}\frac{\cos\theta_1\cos\theta_2}{\pi r^2}\,dA_2\,dA_1 = \int_{A_1}\int_{A_2}\frac{\cos\theta_1\cos\theta_2}{\pi r^2}\,dA_2\,dA_1$$ **Step 5 — Recognize the view factor on each side.** By definition, LHS is $A_1 F_{1 \to 2}$ and RHS is $A_2 F_{2 \to 1}$: $$\boxed{A_1 F_{1 \to 2} = A_2 F_{2 \to 1}}$$ ### 3.3 Worked Analytical Example — KTU Standard Pattern **Problem:** Two parallel, equal-area plates of dimensions $1\ \mathrm{m} \times 1\ \mathrm{m}$ are separated by $0.5\ \mathrm{m}$ and joined by a perfectly reflecting surface on all four sides. Compute $F_{12}$, $F_{21}$, and $F_{11}$. **Solution:** **Step 1 — Use symmetry.** Because $A_1 = A_2$ and geometry is symmetric: $$F_{1 \to 2} = F_{2 \to 1}$$ **Step 2 — View factor from charts (parallel aligned plates).** For $\frac{a}{c} = \frac{b}{c} = \frac{1}{0.5} = 2$ (where $a = b = 1\ \mathrm{m}$ is plate side, $c = 0.5\ \mathrm{m}$ is separation), from standard view factor charts for parallel aligned squares: $$F_{1 \to 2} \approx 0.285$$ **Step 3 — Use summation rule on surface 1.** Surface 1 sees only surface 2 (side walls are reflective): $$F_{1 \to 1} + F_{1 \to 2} = 1$$ $$F_{1 \to 1} = 1 - 0.285 = 0.715$$ **Step 4 — Reciprocity check.** $A_1 F_{1 \to 2} = 1 \times 0.285 = 0.285$ $A_2 F_{2 \to 1} = 1 \times 0.285 = 0.285$ ✓ $$\boxed{F_{12} = F_{21} = 0.285, \quad F_{11} = 0.715}$$ ### 3.4 Hottel's Crossed-Strings Method (2D Derivation) For two-dimensional surfaces (long parallel strips), the view factor from surface 1 to surface 2 is: $$F_{1 \to 2} = \frac{\sum \text{(lengths of crossed strings)} - \sum \text{(lengths of uncrossed strings)}}{2 \times L_1}$$ **Derivation sketch:** **Step 1 — Draw four strings** connecting the two pairs of endpoints of surfaces 1 and 2: two crossed (X-pattern) and two uncrossed. **Step 2 — Sum the string lengths:** $$\begin{aligned} \text{crossed strings} &= L_{13} + L_{24} \\ \text{uncrossed strings} &= L_{14} + L_{23} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3 — Apply the formula:** $$F_{1 \to 2} = \frac{(L_{13} + L_{24}) - (L_{14} + L_{23})}{2\,L_1}$$ > [!NOTE] > This method gives the **exact** view factor for infinitely long parallel strips (2D geometry). Highly useful for furnace walls. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 View Factor Geometry (Mermaid Block Diagram) ```mermaid graph TD A1[Surface A1
emitter dA1] A2[Surface A2
receiver dA2] R[r line connecting dA1 and dA2] T1[theta1 angle from normal of A1] T2[theta2 angle from normal of A2] EM[E_b hemispherical emissive power] I[I_b intensity W per m2 per sr] A1 -- emits photons --> R R -- strikes --> A2 T1 -- measured at --> A1 T2 -- measured at --> A2 A1 -- diffuse emission --> I I -- integrated over hemisphere --> EM EM -- multiplied by dA1 --> Q1[Total radiation leaving A1] Q1 -- fraction F12 --> A2 ``` ### 4.2 The Four View Factor Relations as a Functional Architecture ```mermaid graph LR SUM[1 Summation Rule
sum Fij equals 1] REC[2 Reciprocity
Ai Fij equals Aj Fji] SUP[3 Superposition
composite receiving surface] SYM[4 Symmetry
Fij equals Fji for equal areas] SUM --> USE[Used for closed enclosure verification] REC --> USE2[Used to compute hard view factors from easy ones] SUP --> USE3[Used when receiver is split into parts] SYM --> USE4[Used for parallel plates, concentric bodies] USE --> RAD[Radiation Network
KTU Module 3] USE2 --> RAD USE3 --> RAD USE4 --> RAD RAD --> EXAM[KTU University Exam Problem Solving] ``` ### 4.3 Algorithm for Solving KTU View Factor Problems ```mermaid graph TD START[Start: Identify surfaces and enclosure type] Q1{Is enclosure closed?} Q2{Are areas equal?} Q3{Is geometry 2D?} Q4{Is one surface
completely enclosed?} A1[Apply summation rule sum Fij equals 1] A2[Apply reciprocity Ai Fij equals Aj Fji] A3[Use symmetry Fij equals Fji] A4[Use Hottel crossed strings method] A5[For concentric inner surface F12 equals 1] A6[For concentric outer surface F21 equals 1] SOL[Combine relations to find unknown view factors] START --> Q1 Q1 -- Yes --> A1 Q1 -- No --> Q4 Q4 -- Yes --> A5 Q4 -- No --> Q3 Q3 -- Yes --> A4 Q3 -- No --> Q2 Q2 -- Yes --> A3 Q2 -- No --> A2 A1 --> SOL A2 --> SOL A3 --> SOL A4 --> SOL A5 --> SOL A6 --> SOL ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1.** Define view factor. State the four fundamental view factor relations used in radiation heat transfer. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Model Answer:** View factor $F_{i \to j}$ is the fraction of total radiation leaving surface $i$ that strikes surface $j$ directly. It is a purely geometric quantity, dimensionless, with $0 \le F_{ij} \le 1$. The four relations are: 1. **Summation rule:** $\sum_{j=1}^{N} F_{i \to j} = 1$ for a closed enclosure. 2. **Reciprocity:** $A_i F_{i \to j} = A_j F_{j \to i}$. 3. **Superposition:** $A_i F_{i \to (j+m)} = A_j F_{i \to j} + A_m F_{i \to m}$. 4. **Symmetry:** $F_{ij} = F_{ji}$ when $A_i = A_j$ and geometry is symmetric. --- **Q2.** Two infinite parallel plates face each other. If plate 2 is much larger than plate 1, what is the view factor $F_{1 \to 2}$? `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Model Answer:** Since plate 2 is much larger than plate 1, every ray leaving plate 1 strikes plate 2 (none escapes). Therefore: $$F_{1 \to 2} = 1$$ This is the standard "infinite surroundings" result used in radiation cooling problems. --- ### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(a)** Derive the reciprocity relation $A_1 F_{1 \to 2} = A_2 F_{2 \to 1}$ from first principles. (7 Marks) **Model Solution:** **Step 1:** Consider elemental areas $dA_1$ on surface 1 and $dA_2$ on surface 2 separated by distance $r$. **Step 2:** The radiative flux leaving $dA_1$ intercepted by $dA_2$ is: $$d^2Q_{1 \to 2} = I_{b1} \frac{\cos\theta_1 \cos\theta_2}{r^2}\, dA_1\, dA_2$$ **Step 3:** The radiative flux leaving $dA_2$ intercepted by $dA_1$ is: $$d^2Q_{2 \to 1} = I_{b2} \frac{\cos\theta_1 \cos\theta_2}{r^2}\, dA_2\, dA_1$$ **Step 4:** Form the ratio of geometry kernel: $$\frac{d^2Q_{1 \to 2}}{I_{b1}\, dA_1\, dA_2} = \frac{\cos\theta_1 \cos\theta_2}{\pi r^2}$$ $$\frac{d^2Q_{2 \to 1}}{I_{b2}\, dA_2\, dA_1} = \frac{\cos\theta_1 \cos\theta_2}{\pi r^2}$$ **Step 5:** Equate: $$\frac{d^2Q_{1 \to 2}}{I_{b1}\, dA_1\, dA_2} = \frac{d^2Q_{2 \to 1}}{I_{b2}\, dA_2\, dA_1}$$ **Step 6:** Integrate both sides over $A_1$ and $A_2$: $$\int_{A_1}\int_{A_2}\frac{\cos\theta_1 \cos\theta_2}{\pi r^2}\,dA_2\,dA_1 = \int_{A_1}\int_{A_2}\frac{\cos\theta_1 \cos\theta_2}{\pi r^2}\,dA_2\,dA_1$$ **Step 7:** Recognize each integral as a view factor: $$A_1 F_{1 \to 2} = A_2 F_{2 \to 1}$$ $$\boxed{A_1 F_{1 \to 2} = A_2 F_{2 \to 1}}$$ **(b)** A furnace consists of two parallel, equal-area square plates of side $1\ \mathrm{m}$ separated by $1\ \mathrm{m}$. From standard charts, $F_{12} = 0.20$. If plate 2 is now replaced by two equal-area rectangular plates placed side-by-side covering the same total area, find the view factor from plate 1 to each of the new plates. (7 Marks) **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Initial geometry.** $A_1 = A_2 = 1\ \mathrm{m^2}$, $F_{1 \to 2} = 0.20$. **Step 2 — Composite surface $j + m$.** Each new plate has area $A_j = A_m = 0.5\ \mathrm{m^2}$, total area $A_2 = 1\ \mathrm{m^2}$. **Step 3 — Apply superposition:** $$A_1 F_{1 \to (j+m)} = A_j F_{1 \to j} + A_m F_{1 \to m}$$ $$1 \times 0.20 = 0.5\, F_{1 \to j} + 0.5\, F_{1 \to m}$$ **Step 4 — Use symmetry.** By symmetry of the two equal new plates: $$F_{1 \to j} = F_{1 \to m}$$ **Step 5 — Solve:** $$0.20 = 0.5 F_{1 \to j} + 0.5 F_{1 \to j} = F_{1 \to j}$$ $$\boxed{F_{1 \to j} = F_{1 \to m} = 0.20}$$ --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **(a)** State and explain Hottel's crossed-strings method for determining view factors in two-dimensional geometries. Compute the view factor between two infinitely long parallel strips of width $W = 2\ \mathrm{m}$ separated by distance $D = 3\ \mathrm{m}$ directly facing each other. (7 Marks) **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Hottel's method statement.** For two-dimensional (infinitely long) geometries, the view factor from surface 1 (length $L_1$) to surface 2 is: $$F_{1 \to 2} = \frac{\sum \text{crossed strings} - \sum \text{uncrossed strings}}{2\,L_1}$$ **Step 2 — Identify endpoints.** Let surface 1 endpoints be at $(0,0)$ and $(0,2)$, and surface 2 endpoints be at $(3,0)$ and $(3,2)$. **Step 3 — Compute string lengths.** Uncrossed strings: $L_{14}$ (left-bottom to right-bottom) $= \sqrt{3^2 + 0^2} = 3.0\ \mathrm{m}$ $L_{23}$ (left-top to right-top) $= \sqrt{3^2 + 0^2} = 3.0\ \mathrm{m}$ Crossed strings: $L_{13}$ (left-bottom to right-top) $= \sqrt{3^2 + 2^2} = \sqrt{13} = 3.606\ \mathrm{m}$ $L_{24}$ (left-top to right-bottom) $= \sqrt{3^2 + 2^2} = 3.606\ \mathrm{m}$ **Step 4 — Apply formula.** $$F_{1 \to 2} = \frac{(3.606 + 3.606) - (3.0 + 3.0)}{2 \times 2} = \frac{7.211 - 6.0}{4} = \frac{1.211}{4}$$ $$\boxed{F_{1 \to 2} = 0.303}$$ **(b)** A small circular disk of area $0.01\ \mathrm{m^2}$ is placed at the bottom of a large hemispherical bowl of radius $0.5\ \mathrm{m}$. The bowl interior is treated as surface 2 with area $A_2 = 2\pi r^2 = 1.571\ \mathrm{m^2}$. Using the reciprocity relation and summation rule, determine $F_{2 \to 1}$. (7 Marks) **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Identify enclosure geometry.** Disk (surface 1) is fully enclosed by the hemisphere (surface 2). The hemisphere + disk form a closed enclosure. **Step 2 — View factor from disk to hemisphere.** Since the disk is completely enclosed and small, all radiation leaving the disk strikes the hemisphere: $$F_{1 \to 2} = 1$$ **Step 3 — Apply reciprocity relation.** $$A_1 F_{1 \to 2} = A_2 F_{2 \to 1}$$ $$0.01 \times 1 = 1.571 \times F_{2 \to 1}$$ $$F_{2 \to 1} = \frac{0.01}{1.571} = 0.00637$$ **Step 4 — Verify using summation rule for surface 2.** $$F_{2 \to 1} + F_{2 \to 2} = 1$$ $$F_{2 \to 2} = 1 - 0.00637 = 0.9936$$ The hemisphere's self-view factor is very close to 1 because it almost sees itself (concave geometry). $$\boxed{F_{2 \to 1} = 0.00637}$$ --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Reciprocity Direction Reversal:** Writing $A_1 F_{12} = A_2 F_{12}$ (no swap). Always remember: $A_{\text{first}} F_{\text{first,second}} = A_{\text{second}} F_{\text{second,first}}$. > 2. **Summation Rule Misuse:** Applying $\sum F_{ij} = 1$ to an open geometry. The summation rule requires a **closed** enclosure. > 3. **Confusing $F_{ii}$ with 1:** Many students assume every surface sees itself fully. Wrong — only fully concave surfaces (like a hemisphere interior) approach $F_{ii} \to 1$. > 4. **Units in Reciprocity:** Area must be in consistent units (both in $\mathrm{m^2}$ or both in $\mathrm{cm^2}$). Mismatched units = zero. > 5. **Missing Factor of 2 in Hottel's:** Forgetting the $2 L_1$ in the denominator of the crossed-strings formula. > 6. **Confusing view factor with emissivity:** View factor is geometric; emissivity is a material property. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **View Factor $F_{i \to j}$** = fraction of radiation leaving surface $i$ that strikes surface $j$. - It is **purely geometric**, dimensionless, and $0 \le F_{ij} \le 1$. - **Summation Rule:** $\sum_{j} F_{i \to j} = 1$ (closed enclosure only). - **Reciprocity:** $A_i F_{i \to j} = A_j F_{j \to i}$ — the most-used relation in KTU problems. - **Superposition:** Apply when the receiving surface is split into parts. - **Symmetry:** $F_{ij} = F_{ji}$ for equal-area symmetric surfaces. - **Infinite parallel plates:** $F_{1 \to 2} = 1$ if plate 2 is much larger. - **Fully enclosed inner surface:** $F_{1 \to 2} = 1$ (all radiation leaves inner surface). - **Concentric spheres/cylinders:** Inner completely enclosed by outer. - **Hottel's crossed strings:** Useful for 2D parallel strip problems. - **Stefan–Boltzmann constant:** $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}\ \mathrm{W/m^2 K^4}$. - **Blackbody emissive power:** $E_b = \sigma T^4$. - **Always check reciprocity as a verification step** before finalizing answers in KTU exams.

topics.title: Electrical Analogy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET754 - HEAT AND MASS TRANSFER
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Electrical Analogy

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of conduction heat transfer, the **Electrical Analogy** (also called the **Thermal-Electrical Analogy**) is a powerful analytical tool that establishes a direct mathematical correspondence between the transport of thermal energy through a medium and the flow of electric current through a conductor. This analogy maps the governing differential equation of one-dimensional, transient heat conduction (Fourier's Law combined with the energy balance equation) onto the standard lumped-parameter RC circuit equation derived from Ohm's Law and Kirchhoff's Voltage Law.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Mapping Principle:** The flow of thermal energy is mathematically isomorphic to the flow of electrical charge. Every thermal circuit parameter has a one-to-one electrical counterpart, allowing the entire arsenal of circuit analysis (Thevenin equivalents, Norton equivalents, time constants, transient response) to be applied directly to heat-transfer problems.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuitive Overview)

Imagine a horizontal pipe carrying hot water (heat) and a copper wire carrying electric current side-by-side. If you squeeze the pipe (reduce cross-sectional area *A*), water flows slower (heat flux *q"* decreases). If you use a longer pipe, more thermal "friction" occurs (greater thermal resistance *R_t*). If you insulate the pipe heavily, the water retains its heat longer (greater thermal capacitance *C_t*). 

In exactly the same way, the electrical wire resists current based on its length and cross-section. The fundamental insight is that **heat is a quantity that flows down a temperature gradient**, just as **current is a quantity that flows down a voltage gradient**. The "friction" that opposes this flow is *resistance*, and the "inertia" or "storage" capacity is *capacitance*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants Required for this Module:**
> - **Stephan-Boltzmann constant** $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}^4$
> - **Universal Gas Constant** $R_u = 8.314 \text{ kJ/kmol}\cdot\text{K}$
> - All standard thermal properties ($k, h, \rho, c_p$) are problem-specific.

### 1.3 The One-to-One Correspondence Map

| Heat Transfer Quantity | Symbol & Unit | Electrical Equivalent | Symbol & Unit |
| :--- | :---: | :--- | :---: |
| Heat transfer rate | $Q$ [W] | Current | $I$ [A] |
| Temperature difference | $\Delta T$ [K] or [°C] | Voltage difference | $\Delta V$ [V] |
| Thermal Resistance | $R_t$ [K/W] | Electrical Resistance | $R_e$ [$\Omega$] |
| Thermal Capacitance | $C_t$ [J/K] | Electrical Capacitance | $C_e$ [F] |
| Heat generation rate | $\dot{Q}_{gen}$ [W] | Current source | $I_{src}$ [A] |
| Temperature | $T$ [K] | Voltage (potential) | $V$ [V] |
| Heat flux | $q"$ [W/m²] | Current density | $J$ [A/m²] |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Foundational Laws Driving the Analogy

**Step 1 — Fourier's Law of Heat Conduction (The Thermal "Ohm's Law"):**
For one-dimensional steady conduction through a plane wall of thickness *L*, cross-sectional area *A*, and thermal conductivity *k*, the heat-transfer rate is:

$$Q = \frac{kA}{L} \Delta T$$

**Step 2 — Ohm's Law (The Electrical Counterpart):**
For an electrical conductor of length *L*, cross-sectional area *A_e*, and electrical conductivity $\sigma_e$:

$$I = \frac{\sigma_e A_e}{L} \Delta V$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Both equations have the **exact same algebraic structure**: a "potential difference" drives a "flow" against a "conductance." This is why the analogy is mathematically rigorous and not merely a teaching convenience.

**Step 3 — Energy Storage (Capacitive Effect):**
Just as an electrical capacitor stores charge, a thermal mass stores thermal energy. The energy stored in a body of mass *m* and specific heat $c_p$ when its temperature rises by $\Delta T$ is:

$$E_{th} = m c_p \Delta T = \rho V c_p \Delta T$$

**Step 4 — Transient Heat Balance (Kirchhoff's Voltage Law Analogue):**
Applying conservation of energy to a lumped thermal mass gives the governing differential equation. Summing the temperature "drops" across the resistive path equals the sum of temperature "drops" across capacitive elements — directly mirroring KVL.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Description / Application |
| :---: | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $R_{t,cond} = \dfrac{L}{kA}$ | Thermal resistance for plane-wall conduction |
| 2 | $R_{t,cond} = \dfrac{\ln(r_2/r_1)}{2\pi k L}$ | Thermal resistance for cylindrical-shell conduction |
| 3 | $R_{t,cond} = \dfrac{r_2 - r_1}{4\pi k r_1 r_2}$ | Thermal resistance for spherical-shell conduction |
| 4 | $R_{t,conv} = \dfrac{1}{hA}$ | Thermal resistance for convection at a surface |
| 5 | $R_{t,rad} = \dfrac{1}{h_r A} = \dfrac{1}{\sigma \varepsilon A (T_s + T_{\infty})(T_s^2 + T_{\infty}^2)}$ | Thermal resistance for radiation |
| 6 | $C_t = \rho V c_p$ | Lumped thermal capacitance |
| 7 | $\tau = R_t C_t$ | Thermal time constant of a lumped system |
| 8 | $\dfrac{T(t) - T_{\infty}}{T_i - T_{\infty}} = e^{-t/\tau} = \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{hA}{\rho V c_p}\,t\right)$ | Lumped-capacitance transient solution |
| 9 | $Bi = \dfrac{h L_c}{k} = \dfrac{R_{t,cond}}{R_{t,conv}}$ | Biot number as a ratio of thermal resistances |
| 10 | $\dot{Q}_{gen}$ $\longleftrightarrow$ $I_{src}$ | Heat generation maps to a constant current source |
| 11 | $R_{series} = R_1 + R_2 + \dots + R_n$ | Resistances add when heat flows through in series |
| 12 | $\dfrac{1}{R_{parallel}} = \dfrac{1}{R_1} + \dfrac{1}{R_2} + \dots + \dfrac{1}{R_n}$ | Resistances add inversely when heat flows in parallel paths |
| 13 | $q" = -k\,\dfrac{dT}{dx}$ $\longleftrightarrow$ $J = -\sigma_e\,\dfrac{dV}{dx}$ | Local flux form: gradient of potential drives flow |

### 2.3 Why the Analogy is Useful in Engineering Practice

- **Heat Exchanger Design:** Engineers draw thermal-resistance networks just like electrical circuits. Complicated multi-layer walls (finned tubes, insulation + cladding + air-gap) are reduced to a single equivalent resistance using series–parallel rules.
- **Transient Cooling Problems:** Predicting how long a turbine blade takes to cool, or how fast a CPU heats up, is solved by mapping the problem onto an RC circuit.
- **Lumped vs. Distributed Validation:** The Biot number, expressed elegantly as a *ratio of thermal resistances*, tells the engineer when the lumped-capacitance (electrical-analogy) approach is valid ($Bi \leq 0.1$).
- **Heat Pipes & Electronics Cooling:** Spreadsheets and numerical tools (e.g., finite-difference networks) routinely use the analogy because circuit solvers are extremely mature and well-optimized.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Thermal Resistance for a Plane Wall

We begin with the one-dimensional form of **Fourier's Law** applied to a homogeneous plane wall of thickness *L*, area *A*, and constant conductivity *k*. The temperature varies only along the *x*-direction.

**Step 1 — Start from Fourier's Law:**

$$q"_x = -k\,\dfrac{dT}{dx}$$

**Step 2 — Convert heat flux to heat rate using $Q = q"_x \cdot A$:**

$$Q = -kA\,\dfrac{dT}{dx}$$

**Step 3 — Separate variables and integrate across the wall** from $x=0$ (temperature $T_1$) to $x=L$ (temperature $T_2$), assuming steady-state (so *Q* is constant):

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q \int_{0}^{L} dx &= -kA \int_{T_1}^{T_2} dT \\[4pt]
Q \cdot L &= kA (T_1 - T_2)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Rearrange into the canonical Ohm's-Law form** $Q = \Delta T / R_t$:

$$Q = \dfrac{T_1 - T_2}{L/(kA)} = \dfrac{\Delta T}{R_{t,cond}}$$

Therefore, by direct comparison:

$$\boxed{\,R_{t,cond} = \dfrac{L}{kA}\,}$$

This has the exact functional form of an electrical resistor where $R_e = L/(\sigma_e A_e)$.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Thermal Resistance for a Cylindrical Shell

For radial conduction through a hollow cylinder of inner radius $r_1$, outer radius $r_2$, length *L*, and conductivity *k*:

**Step 1 — Write Fourier's law in cylindrical coordinates:**

$$Q = -k(2\pi r L)\,\dfrac{dT}{dr}$$

(The factor $2\pi r L$ is the area normal to heat flow at radius *r*.)

**Step 2 — Separate variables and integrate** between the inner and outer surfaces:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q \int_{r_1}^{r_2} \dfrac{dr}{r} &= -2\pi k L \int_{T_1}^{T_2} dT \\[4pt]
Q \ln\!\left(\dfrac{r_2}{r_1}\right) &= 2\pi k L (T_1 - T_2)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for *Q* and read off the thermal resistance:**

$$Q = \dfrac{T_1 - T_2}{\ln(r_2/r_1) / (2\pi k L)} = \dfrac{\Delta T}{R_{t,cond}}$$

$$\boxed{\,R_{t,cyl} = \dfrac{\ln(r_2/r_1)}{2\pi k L}\,}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Lumped Capacitance Method (Transient Electrical Analogy)

Consider a solid body of volume *V*, density $\rho$, and specific heat $c_p$, initially at temperature $T_i$, suddenly immersed in a fluid at temperature $T_\infty$ with convective coefficient *h*. We treat the body as a single "node" (lumped capacitance) — valid only when $Bi \leq 0.1$.

**Step 1 — Energy balance on the body** (rate of energy stored = rate of energy in by convection):

$$\rho V c_p \dfrac{dT}{dt} = hA \bigl(T_\infty - T(t)\bigr)$$

**Step 2 — Recognize the RC-circuit equation.** In an electrical RC circuit with a charging capacitor, KVL gives $V_{cap} + IR = V_{src}$. Differentiating, the same ODE emerges:

$$R_t C_t \,\dfrac{dT}{dt} + T(t) = T_\infty$$

where $R_t = 1/(hA)$ and $C_t = \rho V c_p$.

**Step 3 — Substitute and rearrange to standard linear-ODE form:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{dT}{dt} + \dfrac{hA}{\rho V c_p}\bigl(T - T_\infty\bigr) &= 0 \\[4pt]
\text{Define time constant } \tau &= \dfrac{\rho V c_p}{hA} = R_t C_t
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Integrate** with the initial condition $T(0) = T_i$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\int_{T_i}^{T(t)} \dfrac{dT}{T - T_\infty} &= -\dfrac{1}{\tau}\int_{0}^{t} dt \\[4pt]
\ln\!\left(\dfrac{T(t) - T_\infty}{T_i - T_\infty}\right) &= -\dfrac{t}{\tau}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Exponentiate to obtain the final closed-form solution:**

$$\boxed{\,\dfrac{T(t) - T_\infty}{T_i - T_\infty} = \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{t}{\tau}\right) = \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{hA}{\rho V c_p}\,t\right)\,}$$

This is *exactly* the voltage across a discharging capacitor in an RC circuit fed by a step input — a perfect electrical-analogy result.

---

### 3.4 Worked Analytical Matrix — Series, Parallel, and Combined Networks

For a composite wall made of three layers in series (e.g., brick + insulation + plaster), the equivalent resistance is:

$$R_{eq} = R_1 + R_2 + R_3 = \dfrac{L_1}{k_1 A} + \dfrac{L_2}{k_2 A} + \dfrac{L_3}{k_3 A}$$

For a wall with parallel heat-flow paths (e.g., a framed wall with studs and insulation side-by-side):

$$R_{eq} = \left[\dfrac{A_1}{R_1} + \dfrac{A_2}{R_2}\right]^{-1} \quad \text{or} \quad \dfrac{1}{R_{eq}} = \sum \dfrac{1}{R_i}$$

For a wall exposed to convection on both sides and conducting in series:

$$R_{total} = \dfrac{1}{h_i A} + \dfrac{L}{kA} + \dfrac{1}{h_o A}$$

The total heat loss is then computed exactly like a circuit current:

$$Q = \dfrac{T_{\infty,i} - T_{\infty,o}}{R_{total}}$$

This demonstrates how the analogy compresses an otherwise formidable boundary-value problem into a single Ohm's-law calculation.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Thermal–Electrical Correspondence (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Heat Transfer Domain] --> A1[Heat Rate Q in W]
    A --> A2[Temperature T in K]
    A --> A3[Thermal Resistance R_t in K per W]
    A --> A4[Thermal Capacitance C_t in J per K]
    A --> A5[Heat Generation Q_gen in W]
    B[Electrical Domain] --> B1[Current I in A]
    B --> B2[Voltage V in V]
    B --> B3[Electrical Resistance R_e in Ohm]
    B --> B4[Electrical Capacitance C_e in F]
    B --> B5[Current Source I_src in A]
    A1 --- MAP1[Isomorphic Mapping]
    B1 --- MAP1
    A2 --- MAP2[Isomorphic Mapping]
    B2 --- MAP2
    A3 --- MAP3[Isomorphic Mapping]
    B3 --- MAP3
    A4 --- MAP4[Isomorphic Mapping]
    B4 --- MAP4
    A5 --- MAP5[Isomorphic Mapping]
    B5 --- MAP5
```

### 4.2 Series Thermal Resistance Network (Mermaid Circuit Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    NODE_TINF[T infinity Hot Fluid] --> RES_CONV1[Convection Resistance R conv 1 over h A]
    RES_CONV1 --> NODE_T1[Node T1 Inner Surface]
    NODE_T1 --> RES_WALL1[Conduction Resistance L1 over k1 A]
    RES_WALL1 --> NODE_T2[Node T2 Interface]
    NODE_T2 --> RES_WALL2[Conduction Resistance L2 over k2 A]
    RES_WALL2 --> NODE_T3[Node T3 Outer Surface]
    NODE_T3 --> RES_CONV2[Convection Resistance 1 over h A]
    RES_CONV2 --> NODE_TAMBIENT[T ambient Cold Fluid]
    NODE_T1 --- CAP1[Capacitance C1 rho V c_p]
    NODE_T2 --- CAP2[Capacitance C2 rho V c_p]
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Solving a Transient Conduction Problem via Analogy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Identify Physical Geometry and Material Properties] --> S2[Draw the Equivalent Thermal Circuit with R_t and C_t Elements]
    S2 --> S3[Compute Each R_t using the Correct Geometric Formula]
    S3 --> S4[Combine R_t in Series or Parallel as Required]
    S4 --> S5[Compute Lumped Time Constant tau equals R_t times C_t]
    S5 --> S6[Check Bi Number for Validity of Lumped Assumption]
    S6 --> DEC1{Biot Less Than or Equal to 0.1}
    DEC1 -- YES --> S7[Apply Exponential Temperature Time Relation]
    DEC1 -- NO --> S8[Use Transient Charts or Numerical Solution]
    S7 --> S9[Plot T versus t for Engineering Interpretation]
    S8 --> S9
```

### 4.4 Resistance Comparison — Series vs. Parallel (Mermaid View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SERIES_BLOCK[Series Thermal Resistances]
        S_IN[T1] --> S_R1[R1]
        S_R1 --> S_NODE[Node]
        S_NODE --> S_R2[R2]
        S_R2 --> S_R3[R3]
        S_R3 --> S_OUT[T2]
    end
    subgraph PARALLEL_BLOCK[Parallel Thermal Resistances]
        P_IN[T1] --> P_J[Junction]
        P_J --> P_R1[R1]
        P_J --> P_R2[R2]
        P_J --> P_R3[R3]
        P_R1 --> P_OUT[T2]
        P_R2 --> P_OUT
        P_R3 --> P_OUT
    end
```
