<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]**  
*State Fourier's law of heat conduction and draw its electrical analogue.*

**Model Answer:**  
Fourier's law states that the rate of heat transfer through a homogeneous material is proportional to the area normal to heat flow and the temperature gradient along the direction of flow:

$$Q = -kA\,\dfrac{dT}{dx}$$

The electrical analogue is Ohm's Law, $I = -(1/R_e)\,dV/dx$, where $Q \leftrightarrow I$, $T \leftrightarrow V$, and $k \leftrightarrow 1/R_e$. The thermal resistance $R_t = L/(kA)$ corresponds to electrical resistance $R_e$.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**  
*Define Biot number. What does it represent in terms of electrical analogy?*

**Model Answer:**  
The Biot number is the ratio of internal conduction resistance within a solid body to the external convection resistance at its surface:

$$Bi = \dfrac{h L_c}{k} = \dfrac{R_{t,cond}}{R_{t,conv}}$$

In electrical-analogy terms, *Bi* compares the thermal "voltage drop" inside the body to the "voltage drop" across the convective boundary. A small *Bi* ($\leq 0.1$) means the internal resistance is negligible compared to the convective resistance — the body's temperature is essentially uniform, justifying the lumped-capacitance approach.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Read Before Writing!**
> - **Do NOT** forget units on thermal resistance (K/W) and capacitance (J/K). Losing 1 mark per sub-question for missing units is extremely common.
> - **Do NOT** confuse the *time constant* $\tau = R_t C_t$ with the *thermal diffusivity* $\alpha = k/(\rho c_p)$. They are NOT the same.
> - **Do NOT** apply the lumped capacitance formula when $Bi > 0.1$. Examiners specifically test this awareness.
> - When converting log-radius problems, **always** keep $\ln$ — never use $\log_{10}$.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern]**

**(a)** A hot steel ball of diameter 8 cm, initially at a uniform temperature of 350 °C, is suddenly cooled in an oil bath at 25 °C. The convective heat-transfer coefficient is 450 W/m²·K. The thermal properties of steel are $k = 45$ W/m·K, $\rho = 7800$ kg/m³, $c_p = 480$ J/kg·K.  
(i) Justify whether the lumped-capacitance (electrical analogy) approach is valid. **(3 marks)**  
(ii) Determine the time required for the ball centre to cool to 100 °C. **(4 marks)**

**(b)** A composite wall consists of three layers in series: 5 cm of brick ($k_1 = 0.7$ W/m·K), 3 cm of plaster ($k_2 = 0.5$ W/m·K), and 10 cm of concrete ($k_3 = 1.4$ W/m·K). The wall area is 20 m². The inside air is at 35 °C with $h_i = 10$ W/m²·K, and the outside ambient is at 5 °C with $h_o = 25$ W/m²·K.  
(i) Draw the equivalent thermal resistance network. **(3 marks)**  
(ii) Calculate the steady-state heat loss through the wall. **(4 marks)**

**Model Solution to Question A:**

**(a)(i) — Validity of lumped capacitance:**
Characteristic length $L_c = V/A_s = (4/3 \pi r^3)/(4\pi r^2) = r/3 = 0.04/3 \approx 0.0133$ m.

$$
\begin{aligned}
Bi &= \dfrac{h L_c}{k} = \dfrac{450 \times 0.0133}{45} = \dfrac{5.985}{45} \approx 0.133
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $Bi \approx 0.13$ is **slightly above 0.1**, the lumped assumption is *borderline*; the answer should note that for an approximate KTU-level calculation, lumped capacitance may be applied with the understanding that the centre temperature differs slightly from the surface. *(In strict practice, a one-term series solution would be used.)*

**(a)(ii) — Time to cool to 100 °C:**
Parameters for steel ball:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_s &= 4\pi r^2 = 4\pi (0.04)^2 = 0.02011 \text{ m}^2 \\
V &= (4/3)\pi r^3 = (4/3)\pi (0.04)^3 = 2.681 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3 \\
R_t &= \dfrac{1}{h A_s} = \dfrac{1}{450 \times 0.02011} = 0.1105 \text{ K/W} \\
C_t &= \rho V c_p = 7800 \times 2.681 \times 10^{-4} \times 480 = 1003.7 \text{ J/K} \\
\tau &= R_t C_t = 0.1105 \times 1003.7 = 110.9 \text{ s}
\end{aligned}
$$

Applying the lumped formula:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{T - T_\infty}{T_i - T_\infty} &= \exp(-t/\tau) \\[4pt]
\dfrac{100 - 25}{350 - 25} &= \exp(-t/110.9) \\[4pt]
\dfrac{75}{325} = 0.2308 &= \exp(-t/110.9)
\end{aligned}
$$

Taking the natural log:

$$
\begin{aligned}
-\dfrac{t}{110.9} &= \ln(0.2308) = -1.466 \\
t &= 110.9 \times 1.466 \approx 162.6 \text{ s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**The ball takes approximately 163 seconds (~2.7 minutes) to cool to 100 °C.**

**(b)(i) — Thermal resistance network:**
The network consists of five resistances in series:
- $R_{conv,i} = 1/(h_i A)$
- $R_{brick} = L_1/(k_1 A)$
- $R_{plaster} = L_2/(k_2 A)$
- $R_{concrete} = L_3/(k_3 A)$
- $R_{conv,o} = 1/(h_o A)$

**(b)(ii) — Steady-state heat loss:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{conv,i} &= \dfrac{1}{10 \times 20} = 0.00500 \text{ K/W} \\
R_{brick} &= \dfrac{0.05}{0.7 \times 20} = 0.003571 \text{ K/W} \\
R_{plaster} &= \dfrac{0.03}{0.5 \times 20} = 0.003000 \text{ K/W} \\
R_{concrete} &= \dfrac{0.10}{1.4 \times 20} = 0.003571 \text{ K/W} \\
R_{conv,o} &= \dfrac{1}{25 \times 20} = 0.002000 \text{ K/W} \\[4pt]
R_{total} &= 0.005 + 0.003571 + 0.003 + 0.003571 + 0.002 \\
&= 0.017143 \text{ K/W}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &= \dfrac{T_{\infty,i} - T_{\infty,o}}{R_{total}} = \dfrac{35 - 5}{0.017143} \approx 1750 \text{ W}
\end{aligned}
$$

**The wall loses heat at approximately 1.75 kW under steady-state conditions.**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024]**

**(a)** A long cylindrical furnace wall of inner radius 5 cm and outer radius 12 cm is made of fireclay brick with $k = 1.0$ W/m·K. The inner surface is at 600 °C and the outer surface is exposed to ambient air at 30 °C with $h = 18$ W/m·K. The axial length is 4 m.  
(i) Derive the expression for thermal resistance of a cylindrical shell. **(3 marks)**  
(ii) Calculate the steady-state heat loss per metre length using the electrical-analogy approach. **(4 marks)**

**(b)** A small copper sphere of 2 cm diameter, initially at 90 °C, is dropped into a water bath at 20 °C. The convective coefficient is 600 W/m²·K. For copper: $k = 385$ W/m·K, $\rho = 8950$ kg/m³, $c_p = 380$ J/kg·K. Using the electrical analogy, determine the temperature of the sphere after 5 minutes and comment on the validity of the lumped assumption. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution to Question B:**

**(a)(i) — Derivation of cylindrical resistance:**
Starting from $Q = -kA\,(dT/dr)$ with $A = 2\pi r L$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q \int_{r_1}^{r_2} \dfrac{dr}{r} &= -2\pi k L \int_{T_1}^{T_2} dT \\
Q \ln(r_2/r_1) &= 2\pi k L (T_1 - T_2) \\
R_{t,cyl} &= \dfrac{\ln(r_2/r_1)}{2\pi k L}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(a)(ii) — Heat loss per metre length** (using $L = 1$ m):

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{cond} &= \dfrac{\ln(0.12/0.05)}{2\pi (1.0)(1)} = \dfrac{\ln 2.4}{6.2832} = \dfrac{0.8755}{6.2832} = 0.1394 \text{ K/W} \\
R_{conv} &= \dfrac{1}{18 \times (2\pi \times 0.12 \times 1)} = \dfrac{1}{13.57} = 0.0737 \text{ K/W} \\
R_{total} &= 0.1394 + 0.0737 = 0.2131 \text{ K/W} \\
Q &= \dfrac{600 - 30}{0.2131} = \dfrac{570}{0.2131} \approx 2675 \text{ W per metre length}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(b) — Transient cooling of copper sphere:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_c &= r/3 = 0.01/3 = 0.00333 \text{ m} \\
Bi &= \dfrac{h L_c}{k} = \dfrac{600 \times 0.00333}{385} \approx 0.0052
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $Bi \ll 0.1$, the **lumped capacitance (electrical analogy) approach is highly valid.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= 4\pi (0.01)^2 = 1.2566 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2 \\
V &= (4/3)\pi (0.01)^3 = 4.189 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}^3 \\
C_t &= \rho V c_p = 8950 \times 4.189 \times 10^{-7} \times 380 = 1.424 \text{ J/K} \\
R_t &= \dfrac{1}{hA} = \dfrac{1}{600 \times 1.2566 \times 10^{-3}} = 1.326 \text{ K/W} \\
\tau &= R_t C_t = 1.326 \times 1.424 = 1.889 \text{ s}
\end{aligned}
$$

For $t = 300$ s:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{T(300) - 20}{90 - 20} &= \exp(-300/1.889) = \exp(-158.8) \approx 0 \\
T(300) &\approx 20 \text{ °C}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** The sphere reaches thermal equilibrium with the bath in a tiny fraction of the 5-minute interval because copper's enormous conductivity and small size make the time constant only ~1.9 seconds. The sphere essentially reaches 20 °C within a few seconds.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- The **Electrical Analogy** maps heat conduction onto Ohm's Law: $Q \leftrightarrow I$, $\Delta T \leftrightarrow \Delta V$, $R_t \leftrightarrow R_e$, $C_t \leftrightarrow C_e$.
- Thermal resistance for conduction: **plane wall** $L/(kA)$; **cylinder** $\ln(r_2/r_1)/(2\pi kL)$; **sphere** $(r_2 - r_1)/(4\pi k r_1 r_2)$.
- Thermal resistance for convection: $1/(hA)$.
- Thermal capacitance: $\rho V c_p$.
- **Biot Number** $Bi = hL_c/k$ = (internal conduction resistance)/(external convection resistance).
- **Lumped Capacitance validity:** $Bi \leq 0.1$ — if greater, the lumped RC analogy fails and one-term series solutions or charts are required.
- Time constant $\tau = R_t C_t$ — governs the speed of thermal response.
- Series resistances add; parallel resistances add by reciprocal.
- Always express temperature in **K or °C consistently** when computing $\Delta T$.
- The transient solution $\dfrac{T - T_\infty}{T_i - T_\infty} = e^{-t/\tau}$ mirrors the RC-circuit discharge equation.
- For combined conduction–convection–radiation problems, all resistances are summed in series to yield an equivalent $R_t$.
- **Don't forget units** in your final answer — KTU examiners are strict on dimensional consistency.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Radiation Shields
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPET754 - HEAT AND MASS TRANSFER > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Radiation Shields # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology) A **Radiation Shield** is a thin, low-emissivity, highly reflecting metallic foil or sheet (typically **polished aluminum**, with emissivity $\varepsilon_s \approx 0.03$ to $0.05$) interposed between two radiating surfaces to reduce the net radiative heat exchange by introducing additional surface resistances in the radiation network, *without* changing the boundary surface temperatures. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** *"A radiation shield is an interposed surface of low emissivity that reduces the net rate of radiation heat transfer between two surfaces. Each shield adds an additional pair of surface resistances in the radiation network, thereby lowering the driving temperature difference effective across each gap."* ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition Imagine a **bonfire** in cold weather. The heat radiating from the fire is intense. Now place a **shiny aluminum baking tray** (not the dark-coated one) between your face and the fire. You will instantly feel less heat — even though the tray gets warm, it *re-radiates* far less than the fire itself because polished aluminum is a **poor emitter**. The same logic works in reverse in cryogenic and space applications: a satellite in space uses multiple layered **Multi-Layer Insulation (MLI)** — each thin aluminized Mylar layer reflects radiation back, dramatically reducing heat loss. ## 1.3 Why Polished Aluminum? — The Key Material Property > [!NOTE] > **Critical Board Tip:** Examiners frequently test *why* polished aluminum is preferred. > - **Emissivity ($\varepsilon$) of polished aluminum $\approx 0.03$ – $0.05$** > - **Emissivity of oxidized/corroded aluminum $\approx 0.30$ — useless as a shield!** > - Aluminum is cheap, lightweight, easily rolled into thin foils, and has a very low **hemispherical emissivity** in the infrared spectrum. The governing radiative heat transfer follows the **Stefan–Boltzmann Law**: $$q = \varepsilon \sigma T^4$$ where $\sigma = \mathbf{5.67 \times 10^{-8}\ W/m^2 \cdot K^4}$ is the **Stefan–Boltzmann constant**. A lower $\varepsilon$ means lower emission for the same temperature. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Reasoning The radiation shield works on the principle of **inserting series surface resistances** in the radiation exchange path: 1. **Without shield:** Two surfaces exchange radiation across a *single* gap. 2. **With one shield:** The single gap is split into *two* gaps. Each gap now sees an extra set of surface resistances from the shield's two faces. 3. **With $N$ shields:** The gap is divided into $(N+1)$ sub-gaps, each contributing its own resistance. 4. **Heat flux $q$ must be conserved** through every sub-gap (steady state, no heat generation in shields). 5. Therefore, $q$ *decreases* even though the boundary temperatures $T_1$ and $T_2$ remain unchanged. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Insight:** Radiation shields do **NOT** alter boundary temperatures; they only **reduce the net heat flux** by adding resistance. ## 2.2 The "Why" and "How" Behind Each Configuration ### (a) Infinite Parallel Plates — Why No View Factor? For two *infinite* parallel plates, every ray leaving plate 1 hits plate 2 (and vice-versa). Hence the **view factor** $F_{12} = F_{21} = 1$. This simplification yields the cleanest formula. ### (b) Concentric Cylinders / Spheres — Why $F_{12} = 1$? For an *inner* surface completely enclosed by an *outer* surface (concentric geometry), **all radiation leaving the inner surface must strike the outer surface**. Hence $F_{12} = 1$, but $F_{21} = A_1/A_2 < 1$. This area ratio appears in the resistance term. ### (c) Equal Emissivity Special Case — Why $T_3$ Simplifies? For steady state with identical emissivities on all surfaces, the temperature drop across each gap is equal in $T^4$ (because resistance is identical). This leads to the elegant midpoint formula: $$T_3^4 = \frac{T_1^4 + T_2^4}{2}$$ ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Universal Symbol Legend (KTU Board Convention):** > - $T_1$ — Hot surface temperature (K) > - $T_2$ — Cold surface temperature (K) > - $T_3, T_4, \ldots$ — Intermediate shield temperatures (K) > - $\varepsilon_1, \varepsilon_2$ — Emissivities of boundary surfaces > - $\varepsilon_3, \varepsilon_4, \ldots$ — Emissivities of shields > - $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}\ W/m^2 \cdot K^4$ > - $A$ — Area in $m^2$ | # | Configuration | Heat Transfer Equation | Special Condition | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Two parallel plates (no shield) | $q = \dfrac{\sigma(T_1^4 - T_2^4)}{\dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_1} + \dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_2} - 1}$ | $F_{12}=1$ | | 2 | Two parallel plates + **1 shield** | $q = \dfrac{\sigma(T_1^4 - T_2^4)}{\dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_1} + \dfrac{2}{\varepsilon_3} + \dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_2} - 2}$ | $F_{12}=1$ | | 3 | Two parallel plates + **$N$ equal shields** ($\varepsilon$) | $q_N = \dfrac{\sigma(T_1^4 - T_2^4)}{(N+1)\left(\dfrac{2}{\varepsilon} - 1\right)}$ | All $\varepsilon$ equal | | 4 | **Heat transfer reduction ratio** | $\dfrac{q_0}{q_N} = N + 1$ | All $\varepsilon$ equal | | 5 | Shield temperature (single shield, equal $\varepsilon$) | $T_3 = \left[\dfrac{T_1^4 + T_2^4}{2}\right]^{1/4}$ | All $\varepsilon$ equal | | 6 | Concentric cylinders (no shield) | $q = \dfrac{A_1 \sigma(T_1^4 - T_2^4)}{\dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_1} + \dfrac{A_1}{A_2}\left(\dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_2} - 1\right)}$ | $F_{12}=1$ | | 7 | Concentric cylinders + **$N$ shields** | $q_N = \dfrac{A_1 \sigma(T_1^4 - T_2^4)}{\left(\dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_1} + \dfrac{A_1}{A_{N+2}}\left(\dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_{N+2}} - 1\right)\right) + \sum_{i=3}^{N+2}\dfrac{A_1}{A_i}\left(\dfrac{2}{\varepsilon_i}\right)}$ | Series resistance network | | 8 | Concentric spheres | Same as concentric cylinders | $F_{12}=1$ | > [!WARNING] > **Critical Notation Warning:** Notice in formulas 2, 3 and 7, the denominator involves subtraction of integers like $(N+1)$ — this comes from the algebraic cancellation of the constant "$1$" terms in the resistance network when summing $(N+1)$ gaps. Students frequently miss this sign. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Cryogenic storage tanks (LNG, liquid nitrogen)** — multiple radiation shields drastically reduce boil-off. - **Thermocouples in furnaces** — radiation shields correct for *radiation error* between gas and bare probe. - **Spacecraft thermal control (MLI)** — 10 to 30 aluminized Mylar layers form the primary insulation in vacuum. - **Building insulation** — reflective foil barriers in attics reduce summer heat gain. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation — Heat Transfer Between Two Infinite Parallel Plates ### Setup Consider two large parallel plates of surface 1 (hot, $T_1$) and surface 2 (cold, $T_2$) separated by a medium that absorbs no radiation (e.g., air, vacuum). Let $J_1$ and $J_2$ be the **radiosities** (total radiation leaving per unit area). ### Steps **Step 1 — Write the energy balance for surface 1** The net heat *leaving* surface 1 equals its emissive power minus its radiosity: $$q = E_{b1} - J_1 = \sigma T_1^4 - J_1$$ **Step 2 — Relate radiosity to emissive power using surface resistance** For a real (gray) surface, the radiosity is the sum of reflected plus emitted radiation: $$J_1 = \varepsilon_1 E_{b1} + (1 - \varepsilon_1) G_1$$ where $G_1$ is the irradiation on surface 1. Rearranging: $$q = \frac{E_{b1} - J_1}{(1 - \varepsilon_1)/\varepsilon_1}$$ This is the **surface resistance** of plate 1: $R_{s1} = (1-\varepsilon_1)/(\varepsilon_1 A_1)$. **Step 3 — Write the space resistance** Since the plates are infinite and parallel, every ray from 1 hits 2 and vice-versa. Therefore: $$q = \frac{J_1 - J_2}{1/(A_1 F_{12})} = J_1 - J_2$$ since $A_1 F_{12} = A_1 \cdot 1 = A_1$. The **space resistance** is $R_{sp} = 1/(A_1 F_{12}) = 1/A_1$. **Step 4 — Write the energy balance for surface 2** $$q = J_2 - E_{b2} = J_2 - \sigma T_2^4$$ Surface resistance of plate 2: $R_{s2} = (1-\varepsilon_2)/(\varepsilon_2 A_2) = (1-\varepsilon_2)/(\varepsilon_2 A_1)$ (since $A_1 = A_2$ for parallel plates). **Step 5 — Sum the resistances (series analogy)** Heat flow through series resistances: $$q = \frac{E_{b1} - E_{b2}}{R_{s1} + R_{sp} + R_{s2}}$$ Substituting: $$q = \frac{\sigma(T_1^4 - T_2^4)}{\dfrac{1-\varepsilon_1}{\varepsilon_1 A_1} + \dfrac{1}{A_1} + \dfrac{1-\varepsilon_2}{\varepsilon_2 A_1}}$$ **Step 6 — Multiply numerator and denominator by $A_1$ and simplify** $$\begin{aligned} q &= \frac{A_1 \sigma(T_1^4 - T_2^4)}{(1-\varepsilon_1)/\varepsilon_1 + 1 + (1-\varepsilon_2)/\varepsilon_2} \\ &= \frac{A_1 \sigma(T_1^4 - T_2^4)}{1/\varepsilon_1 - 1 + 1 + 1/\varepsilon_2 - 1} \\ &= \frac{A_1 \sigma(T_1^4 - T_2^4)}{1/\varepsilon_1 + 1/\varepsilon_2 - 1} \end{aligned}$$ For *per unit area* ($A_1 = 1\ m^2$): $$\boxed{q = \frac{\sigma(T_1^4 - T_2^4)}{\dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_1} + \dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_2} - 1}}$$ --- ## 3.2 Derivation — Effect of a Single Radiation Shield ### Setup Place a thin, low-emissivity shield (surface 3, $T_3$, $\varepsilon_3$) between the two plates. Now there are **two gaps** instead of one. ### Steps **Step 1 — Identify the three series resistances for gap 1 (between plates 1 and 3)** - Surface resistance of plate 1: $R_{s1} = (1-\varepsilon_1)/\varepsilon_1$ - Space resistance (gap 1): $R_{sp,1} = 1$ - Surface resistance of shield (face 1, facing plate 1): $R_{s3a} = (1-\varepsilon_3)/\varepsilon_3$ **Step 2 — Same for gap 2 (between shield and plate 2)** - Surface resistance of shield (face 2, facing plate 2): $R_{s3b} = (1-\varepsilon_3)/\varepsilon_3$ - Space resistance (gap 2): $R_{sp,2} = 1$ - Surface resistance of plate 2: $R_{s2} = (1-\varepsilon_2)/\varepsilon_2$ **Step 3 — Total resistance (series sum)** $$R_{total} = \frac{1-\varepsilon_1}{\varepsilon_1} + 1 + \frac{1-\varepsilon_3}{\varepsilon_3} + \frac{1-\varepsilon_3}{\varepsilon_3} + 1 + \frac{1-\varepsilon_2}{\varepsilon_2}$$ **Step 4 — Expand and simplify** $$\begin{aligned} R_{total} &= \left(\frac{1}{\varepsilon_1} - 1\right) + 1 + 2\left(\frac{1}{\varepsilon_3} - 1\right) + 1 + \left(\frac{1}{\varepsilon_2} - 1\right) \\ &= \frac{1}{\varepsilon_1} + \frac{2}{\varepsilon_3} + \frac{1}{\varepsilon_2} - 2 \end{aligned}$$ **Step 5 — Final formula** $$\boxed{q_{with\ shield} = \frac{\sigma(T_1^4 - T_2^4)}{\dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_1} + \dfrac{2}{\varepsilon_3} + \dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_2} - 2}}$$ --- ## 3.3 Generalization — $N$ Identical Radiation Shields Let all $N$ shields have identical emissivity $\varepsilon_s$ and all surfaces have the same emissivity $\varepsilon$. The system now has $(N+1)$ gaps. ### Step 1 — Total resistance $$R_{total} = \left(\frac{1}{\varepsilon} - 1\right) + (N+1) \cdot 1 + (N+1)\left(\frac{1}{\varepsilon_s} - 1\right) + \left(\frac{1}{\varepsilon} - 1\right)$$ ### Step 2 — Simplify for the equal-emissivity case $\varepsilon_s = \varepsilon$ $$\begin{aligned} R_{total} &= 2\left(\frac{1}{\varepsilon} - 1\right) + (N+1)\left(\frac{1}{\varepsilon} - 1 + 1\right) \\ &= 2\left(\frac{1}{\varepsilon} - 1\right) + (N+1)\frac{1}{\varepsilon} \\ &= \frac{2}{\varepsilon} - 2 + \frac{N+1}{\varepsilon} \\ &= \frac{N+3}{\varepsilon} - 2 \end{aligned}$$ This is one common form. But if we factor differently (recognizing $N$ shields add $2N$ surface resistances, but only $(N+1)$ space resistances, with cancellation of $(N+1)$ unit terms): $$R_{total} = (N+1)\left(\frac{2}{\varepsilon} - 1\right)$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Board Verification:** For $N=0$ (no shield), $R_{total} = 2/\varepsilon - 1$, which matches formula 1. For $N=1$, $R_{total} = 2(2/\varepsilon - 1)$ — i.e., resistance doubles. ✓ ### Step 3 — Final formula for $N$ identical shields $$\boxed{q_N = \frac{\sigma(T_1^4 - T_2^4)}{(N+1)\left(\dfrac{2}{\varepsilon} - 1\right)}}$$ ### Step 4 — Reduction ratio $$\frac{q_0}{q_N} = \frac{\sigma(T_1^4 - T_2^4)/(2/\varepsilon - 1)}{\sigma(T_1^4 - T_2^4)/\left[(N+1)(2/\varepsilon - 1)\right]} = N+1$$ > [!NOTE] > **Engineering takeaway:** With $N$ identical shields, heat transfer is reduced by a factor of $(N+1)$. Two shields cut heat to 1/3; ten shields cut it to 1/11. This is why MLI blankets in spacecraft use 20+ layers. --- ## 3.4 Derivation — Concentric Cylinders (Closed Geometry) ### Setup Inner cylinder (surface 1, $A_1$, $T_1$, $\varepsilon_1$) is enclosed by outer cylinder (surface 2, $A_2$, $T_2$, $\varepsilon_2$). Since surface 1 *sees only* surface 2, $F_{12} = 1$, and by reciprocity $A_1 F_{12} = A_2 F_{21}$, giving $F_{21} = A_1/A_2$. ### Steps **Step 1 — Space resistance** $$R_{sp} = \frac{1}{A_1 F_{12}} = \frac{1}{A_1}$$ (since $F_{12} = 1$). **Step 2 — Surface resistance of inner cylinder (surface 1)** $$R_{s1} = \frac{1 - \varepsilon_1}{\varepsilon_1 A_1}$$ **Step 3 — Surface resistance of outer cylinder (surface 2)** By reciprocity, the *effective* area is $A_1$ (heat is transferred per unit area of the inner cylinder), so: $$R_{s2} = \frac{1 - \varepsilon_2}{\varepsilon_2 A_2}$$ But to add it in series with $R_{s1}$ (which is referenced to $A_1$), we must scale it: $$R_{s2,\text{referenced to }A_1} = \frac{A_1}{A_2} \cdot \frac{1 - \varepsilon_2}{\varepsilon_2}$$ ### Step 4 — Sum and solve $$\begin{aligned} q &= \frac{\sigma(T_1^4 - T_2^4)}{(1-\varepsilon_1)/\varepsilon_1 + 1 + (A_1/A_2)(1-\varepsilon_2)/\varepsilon_2} \\ &= \frac{A_1 \sigma(T_1^4 - T_2^4)}{1/\varepsilon_1 + (A_1/A_2)(1/\varepsilon_2 - 1)} \end{aligned}$$ $$\boxed{q_{12} = \frac{A_1 \sigma(T_1^4 - T_2^4)}{\dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_1} + \dfrac{A_1}{A_2}\left(\dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_2} - 1\right)}}$$ --- ## 3.5 Shield Temperature Derivation (Single Shield, Equal Emissivity) ### Setup For a single shield with $\varepsilon_1 = \varepsilon_2 = \varepsilon_3 = \varepsilon$, the heat flux through gap 1 (between plate 1 and shield) must equal that through gap 2 (between shield and plate 2). ### Step 1 — Equate heat fluxes $$\frac{\sigma(T_1^4 - T_3^4)}{2/\varepsilon - 1} = \frac{\sigma(T_3^4 - T_2^4)}{2/\varepsilon - 1}$$ ### Step 2 — Cancel common terms $$T_1^4 - T_3^4 = T_3^4 - T_2^4$$ ### Step 3 — Solve for $T_3$ $$T_3^4 = \frac{T_1^4 + T_2^4}{2}$$ $$\boxed{T_3 = \left[\frac{T_1^4 + T_2^4}{2}\right]^{1/4}}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > This is the famous **midpoint-in-$T^4$ rule**. The shield temperature is *not* the arithmetic mean — it is biased toward the hotter side because radiation scales with $T^4$. --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Radiation Shield Network Architecture The following Mermaid block diagram maps the *radiation resistance network* and the heat-flow architecture through multiple shields. It serves as a functional analog of the physical setup and clarifies how series resistances accumulate. ```mermaid flowchart LR A["EB1: Hot Surface
T1, eps1"] --> B["Rs1: Surface Resistance
1-eps1 divided by eps1"] B --> C["Rsp1: Space Gap 1
1 divided by A1"] C --> D["Rs3a: Shield Face 1
1-eps3 divided by eps3"] D --> E["J3: Shield Radiosity
Temperature T3"] E --> F["Rs3b: Shield Face 2
1-eps3 divided by eps3"] F --> G["Rsp2: Space Gap 2
1 divided by A1"] G --> H["Rs2: Cold Surface
1-eps2 divided by eps2"] H --> I["EB2: Cold Surface
T2, eps2"] J["Driving Force:
EB1 minus EB2"] --> A I --> K["Net Heat Flux q"] A --> K L["Nth Shield Block"] -.-> E M["Increasing N reduces q
by factor N+1"] -.-> K style A fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#990000,color:#000000 style I fill:#ccccff,stroke:#000099,color:#000000 style E fill:#ffffcc,stroke:#999900,color:#000000 style J fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#006600,color:#000000 style K fill:#ffccff,stroke:#660066,color:#000000 style M fill:#ffffff,stroke:#333333,color:#000000 style L fill:#eeeeee,stroke:#666666,color:#000000 ``` ## 4.2 Reading the Diagram - **Top row (A → I):** Sequential resistances through which heat must flow — the network analogy. - **EB1 and EB2:** The driving potentials ($\sigma T^4$). - **J3:** The intermediate radiosity node representing the shield's thermal state. - **Dashed lines (L, M):** Indicate that adding more shield blocks (L) further reduces net heat flux (K). - **Color code:** - 🔴 **Red:** Hot boundary - 🔵 **Blue:** Cold boundary - 🟡 **Yellow:** Shield (intermediate state) - 🟢 **Green:** Driving potential - 🟣 **Magenta:** Output (heat flux) ## 4.3 Physical Schematic Mapping | Diagram Block | Physical Reality | |---|---| | $E_{b1}$ | Hot plate radiating at $\sigma T_1^4$ | | $R_{s1}$ | Surface 1's emissivity loss $(1-\varepsilon_1)/\varepsilon_1$ | | $R_{sp}$ | Vacuum/air gap — no resistance other than geometry | | $R_{s3a}, R_{s3b}$ | Both faces of the polished aluminum shield | | $J_3$ | Actual radiative flux emitted by the shield | | $E_{b2}$ | Cold plate radiating back at $\sigma T_2^4$ | --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, As per 2024 Scheme Pattern]** ### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Model]` **Q: Define radiation shield. Why is polished aluminum preferred as a radiation shield material?** **Model Answer:** A radiation shield is a thin, low-emissivity metallic surface interposed between two radiating surfaces to reduce the net radiative heat transfer without altering the boundary temperatures. It works by adding series surface resistances in the radiation network. Polished aluminum is preferred because: - It has a **very low hemispherical emissivity** ($\varepsilon \approx 0.03$ – $0.05$). - It has a **high reflectivity** in the infrared spectrum. - It is **lightweight, inexpensive**, and easily fabricated into thin foils. - Its low $\varepsilon$ minimizes the surface resistance $(1-\varepsilon)/\varepsilon$ contribution from each face of the shield, *but paradoxically* this large surface resistance is precisely what *reduces* the net heat flux — since heat flow = driving force / total resistance. > [!NOTE] > Word count target: 80–100 words. Marks awarded for: definition (1) + material property explanation (1.5) + working mechanism (0.5). --- ### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model]` **Q: Two large parallel plates at temperatures 800 K and 300 K exchange radiation. If a single radiation shield of emissivity 0.05 is placed between them, by what factor is the heat transfer reduced? Assume all surfaces have equal emissivity $\varepsilon = 0.8$.** **Model Answer:** Without shield, denominator = $2/\varepsilon - 1 = 2/0.8 - 1 = 2.5 - 1 = 1.5$. With one shield (all $\varepsilon$ equal = 0.8), denominator = $(N+1)(2/\varepsilon - 1) = 2 \times 1.5 = 3.0$. Reduction factor: $$\frac{q_0}{q_1} = \frac{3.0}{1.5} = 2$$ So heat transfer is reduced by a factor of **2**. Since $N+1 = 2$, this confirms the formula $\frac{q_0}{q_N} = N+1$. --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) > **[KTU University Exam — As per 2024 Scheme ESE Pattern]** --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` #### **Part (a) — 7 Marks** **Derive the expression for the rate of heat transfer between two large parallel plates (a) without a radiation shield and (b) with a single radiation shield. State all assumptions.** **Model Solution:** *(Full derivation as shown in Section 3.1 and 3.2 of these notes.)* **Assumptions to be stated:** 1. Surfaces are large and parallel (so $F_{12} = 1$). 2. Surfaces are gray, diffuse, and opaque. 3. The intervening medium is non-participating (no absorption, emission, or scattering). 4. Steady-state conditions; no convection/conduction across the gap. 5. Shield is thin (negligible conduction along its thickness); both faces are at the same temperature. **Final formulas (to be boxed in answer):** $$\text{(a)}\quad q = \frac{\sigma(T_1^4 - T_2^4)}{\dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_1} + \dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_2} - 1}$$ $$\text{(b)}\quad q = \frac{\sigma(T_1^4 - T_2^4)}{\dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_1} + \dfrac{2}{\varepsilon_3} + \dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_2} - 2}$$ #### **Part (b) — 7 Marks** **Two large parallel plates at $T_1 = 1000\ K$ and $T_2 = 500\ K$ have emissivities $\varepsilon_1 = 0.8$ and $\varepsilon_2 = 0.6$ respectively. A polished aluminum radiation shield ($\varepsilon_3 = 0.05$) is placed between them. Calculate: (i) the heat transfer rate per unit area without the shield, (ii) the heat transfer rate per unit area with the shield, and (iii) the temperature of the shield.** **Model Solution:** **Given:** - $T_1 = 1000\ K$, $T_2 = 500\ K$ - $\varepsilon_1 = 0.8$, $\varepsilon_2 = 0.6$, $\varepsilon_3 = 0.05$ - $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}\ W/m^2 K^4$ **(i) Without shield:** $$\begin{aligned} q_0 &= \frac{\sigma(T_1^4 - T_2^4)}{1/\varepsilon_1 + 1/\varepsilon_2 - 1} \\ &= \frac{5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times (1000^4 - 500^4)}{1/0.8 + 1/0.6 - 1} \\ &= \frac{5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times (10^{12} - 6.25 \times 10^{10})}{1.25 + 1.667 - 1} \\ &= \frac{5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times 9.375 \times 10^{11}}{1.917} \\ &= \frac{53156.25}{1.917} \\ &= 27729\ W/m^2 \end{aligned}$$ $$\boxed{q_0 \approx 27.73\ kW/m^2}$$ **(ii) With shield:** $$\begin{aligned} q_{sh} &= \frac{\sigma(T_1^4 - T_2^4)}{1/\varepsilon_1 + 2/\varepsilon_3 + 1/\varepsilon_2 - 2} \\ &= \frac{5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times 9.375 \times 10^{11}}{1/0.8 + 2/0.05 + 1/0.6 - 2} \\ &= \frac{53156.25}{1.25 + 40 + 1.667 - 2} \\ &= \frac{53156.25}{40.917} \\ &= 1299\ W/m^2 \end{aligned}$$ $$\boxed{q_{sh} \approx 1.30\ kW/m^2}$$ **(iii) Temperature of the shield:** Use heat flux through gap 1 (between plate 1 and shield): $$\begin{aligned} T_3^4 &= T_1^4 - \frac{q_{sh}}{\sigma}\left(\frac{1}{\varepsilon_1} + \frac{1}{\varepsilon_3} - 1\right) \\ &= (1000)^4 - \frac{1299}{5.67 \times 10^{-8}} \times (1/0.8 + 1/0.05 - 1) \\ &= 10^{12} - (2.291 \times 10^{10}) \times (1.25 + 20 - 1) \\ &= 10^{12} - (2.291 \times 10^{10}) \times 20.25 \\ &= 10^{12} - 4.639 \times 10^{11} \\ &= 5.361 \times 10^{11} \\ T_3 &= (5.361 \times 10^{11})^{1/4} \approx 856.5\ K \end{aligned}$$ $$\boxed{T_3 \approx 856.5\ K \approx 583.5^\circ C}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > Notice $T_3 = 856.5\ K$ is *not* the arithmetic mean (750 K). It is biased toward $T_1$ because $T^4$ scales radiation. This matches the midpoint-in-$T^4$ rule when emissivities are equal. **Heat transfer reduction:** $$\frac{q_0}{q_{sh}} = \frac{27729}{1299} \approx 21.35$$ This is *much greater* than the theoretical 2× (equal emissivity case) because the shield here is dramatically lower in emissivity than the plates — a key engineering insight. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model]` #### **Part (a) — 7 Marks** **Derive the expression for radiative heat transfer between two concentric cylinders with $N$ radiation shields. State the assumptions clearly.** **Model Solution:** *(Derivation follows the pattern of Section 3.4 extended to N shields.)* For a single shield between inner cylinder (1) and outer cylinder (3): $$q = \frac{A_1 \sigma(T_1^4 - T_3^4)}{\dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_1} + \dfrac{A_1}{A_2}\left(\dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_2} - 1\right)}$$ where $A_2$ is the shield surface area and $\varepsilon_2$ is its emissivity. For $N$ concentric shields with areas $A_3, A_4, \ldots, A_{N+2}$ and emissivities $\varepsilon_3, \varepsilon_4, \ldots, \varepsilon_{N+2}$, summing the series resistances: $$\boxed{q_N = \frac{A_1 \sigma(T_1^4 - T_{N+2}^4)}{\left[\dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_1} + \dfrac{A_1}{A_{N+2}}\left(\dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_{N+2}} - 1\right)\right] + \sum_{i=3}^{N+2} \dfrac{A_1}{A_i}\left(\dfrac{2}{\varepsilon_i}\right) - N}$$ **Assumptions:** gray, diffuse, opaque surfaces; concentric geometry giving $F_{12}=1$; steady state; negligible shield thickness; non-participating medium. #### **Part (b) — 7 Marks** **A thermocouple with bare-bulb emissivity $\varepsilon = 0.6$ measures the temperature inside a furnace. The bare thermocouple reads $200^\circ C$. When shielded with a polished radiation shield of emissivity $\varepsilon_s = 0.05$, it reads $250^\circ C$. The convective heat transfer coefficient between the gas and thermocouple is $h = 50\ W/m^2K$. Estimate the true gas temperature.** **Model Solution:** **Setup:** Under steady state, convective heat transfer *to* the thermocouple = net radiative heat transfer *from* the thermocouple. For the **bare** thermocouple at temperature $T_{tc,1}$ (in K): $$h(T_{gas} - T_{tc,1}) = \varepsilon_1 \sigma (T_{tc,1}^4 - T_{wall}^4)$$ For the **shielded** thermocouple at temperature $T_{tc,2}$: $$h(T_{gas} - T_{tc,2}) = \frac{\sigma(T_{tc,2}^4 - T_{wall}^4)}{1/\varepsilon_{tc} + 2/\varepsilon_s - 1}$$ **Simplifying assumption:** Assume furnace walls are at $T_{wall} \approx T_{tc,1} = 473\ K$ (very large furnace, walls cool quickly — or use iterative solution). For simplicity, take the furnace walls much cooler than the gas. **Bare reading (473 K):** $$50(T_{gas} - 473) = 0.6 \times 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times 473^4$$ $$50(T_{gas} - 473) = 0.6 \times 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times 5.01 \times 10^{10}$$ $$50(T_{gas} - 473) = 1704.7\ W/m^2$$ $$T_{gas} - 473 = 34.1$$ $$T_{gas} \approx 507.1\ K$$ **Shielded reading (523 K):** $$50(T_{gas} - 523) = \frac{5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times 523^4}{1/0.6 + 2/0.05 - 1}$$ $$\begin{aligned} \text{Denominator} &= 1.667 + 40 - 1 = 40.667 \\ 50(T_{gas} - 523) &= \frac{5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times 7.478 \times 10^{10}}{40.667} \\ 50(T_{gas} - 523) &= \frac{4240}{40.667} \\ 50(T_{gas} - 523) &= 104.3 \\ T_{gas} &= 523 + 2.09 = 525.1\ K \end{aligned}$$ **Average the two estimates:** $$\boxed{T_{gas} \approx 516\ K \approx 243^\circ C}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Real Engineering Note:** In practice, multiple shielded readings are taken with shields of varying emissivity, and the data is extrapolated to $\varepsilon = 0$ (which corresponds to the "true" gas temperature, since at $\varepsilon = 0$ no radiation error exists). This is the basis of the **radiation correction method** for high-temperature gas measurements. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Pitfalls Where KTU Students Lose Marks:** > > 1. **Forgetting the "-1" or "-(N+1)"** in the denominator: students write $\frac{1}{\varepsilon_1} + \frac{1}{\varepsilon_2}$ *without* subtracting 1. This is **wrong** — the "-1" comes from cancellation of space-resistance terms and is critical. **−2 marks per occurrence.** > > 2. **Mixing up shield emissivity:** The shield contributes **TWO surface resistances** (one for each face), so it's $\frac{2}{\varepsilon_3}$ — *not* $\frac{1}{\varepsilon_3}$. With $N$ shields, it's $\frac{2N}{\varepsilon_s}$. > > 3. **Using $F_{12} \neq 1$ for parallel plates:** Many students confuse this with the concentric-geometry case. For *infinite* parallel plates, $F_{12} = 1$ exactly. > > 4. **Arithmetic mean for shield temperature:** The correct formula is the **midpoint-in-$T^4$** rule, not $(T_1 + T_2)/2$. Board examiners explicitly test this. > > 5. **Unit inconsistency:** Always use **Kelvin** (not °C) inside $T^4$. Convert at the start. > > 6. **Showing $A_1$ vs per-unit area:** Decide early whether your answer is "per unit area" or "total." Board questions usually specify — read twice. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist — Radiation Shields (KTU PEPET754 Module 3)** ### Core Concepts - ☐ Radiation shields are *low-emissivity* interposed surfaces that *reduce* $q$ without changing $T_1$ or $T_2$. - ☐ Polished aluminum ($\varepsilon \approx 0.03$–$0.05$) is the standard shield material. - ☐ Each shield **doubles the surface resistance** in the radiation network. ### Master Formulas (must memorize) - ☐ No shield: $q = \sigma(T_1^4 - T_2^4)/[1/\varepsilon_1 + 1/\varepsilon_2 - 1]$ - ☐ With $N$ shields (equal $\varepsilon$): $q_N = \sigma(T_1^4 - T_2^4)/[(N+1)(2/\varepsilon - 1)]$ - ☐ Reduction ratio: $q_0/q_N = N + 1$ (only when all $\varepsilon$ equal) - ☐ Single-shield temperature: $T_3^4 = (T_1^4 + T_2^4)/2$ (equal $\varepsilon$) - ☐ Concentric cylinders: $A_1/A_2$ ratio appears in surface resistance of outer cylinder. ### Engineering Numbers - ☐ $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}\ W/m^2 K^4$ - ☐ $\varepsilon_{Al,polished} \approx 0.05$ - ☐ $\varepsilon_{Al,oxidized} \approx 0.30$ (avoid!) - ☐ MLI blankets in spacecraft: 10–30 layers ### Exam Strategy - ☐ Always convert °C → K before substituting into $T^4$. - ☐ Draw a small resistance-network sketch in your answer sheet — it earns partial credit even if the algebra fails. - ☐ For "find temperature of shield" questions, equate $q$ through both gaps — never assume $T_3 = (T_1+T_2)/2$. - ☐ State assumptions explicitly: gray, diffuse, opaque, steady state. ### Common Cross-Connections - ☐ Radiation shields + **thermocouple error correction** (radiation shields correct probe readings in high-temperature gas measurement). - ☐ Radiation shields + **cryogenic storage** (reduces boil-off in LNG tanks). - ☐ Radiation shields + **building insulation** (reflective foil barriers). - ☐ Multi-shield formula generalizes to **combustion chamber insulation** and **furnace lining** design.

topics.title: Boiling and Condensation: Filmwise and Dropwise Condensation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET754 - HEAT AND MASS TRANSFER
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Boiling and Condensation: Filmwise and Dropwise Condensation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🔥 MODULE 3: BOILING AND CONDENSATION — Filmwise & Dropwise Condensation

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is Phase Change Heat Transfer?

Phase change heat transfer is the science of heat transfer that occurs when a substance changes its physical state (liquid → vapor during **boiling**, or vapor → liquid during **condensation**). Unlike single-phase convection, phase change processes are accompanied by the **absorption** or **release of latent heat** at essentially constant temperature.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (2024 Scheme):**
> Boiling is a liquid-to-vapor phase change occurring *within* a bulk liquid (it happens everywhere in the fluid). Condensation is a vapor-to-liquid phase change occurring *on* a cooled surface (it is inherently a surface-driven phenomenon). Both involve **latent heat transfer** as the dominant mechanism.

---

### 1.2 Boiling — The Big Picture

**Boiling** is the convective heat transfer process in which a liquid changes to vapor by *bubbling at a heated surface*. It involves:
- **Bubble nucleation** at cavities/pits on the surface
- **Bubble growth** and detachment
- **Liquid motion induced by buoyancy** of bubbles

**Pool Boiling** = boiling on a heated surface submerged in a *stagnant (unforced)* bulk of liquid.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy:** Think of boiling water in an open vessel on a stove. As the bottom of the pan gets hot, tiny vapor bubbles form at microscopic pits/crevices on the metal surface (these are "nucleation sites"), grow, detach, and rise — like balloons released underwater. The bubbles carry the absorbed latent heat from the surface into the bulk liquid.

---

### 1.3 Condensation — The Big Picture

**Condensation** is the process by which a vapor changes to a liquid when it comes into contact with a surface whose temperature is **below the saturation temperature** of the vapor.

Two fundamental modes exist:

| Mode | Description | Surface Behavior |
|---|---|---|
| **Filmwise Condensation** | Vapor condenses as a continuous liquid *film* that covers the entire surface | Surface completely wetted |
| **Dropwise Condensation** | Vapor condenses as *discrete droplets* on the surface | Surface not wetted (non-wetting) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Definition — Filmwise Condensation:**
> A mode of condensation in which the condensate forms a *continuous liquid film* on the cooled surface, and further condensation occurs at the **liquid-vapor interface** of the film, not at the wall. Heat must conduct through this growing film — so it offers higher thermal resistance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Definition — Dropwise Condensation:**
> A mode of condensation in which the condensate forms *discrete droplets* on the cooled surface. Droplets grow by direct condensation on them and by coalescence, then roll/slide off under gravity — exposing fresh surface for further condensation. Heat transfer is much higher than filmwise.

---

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics Used

| Property | Symbol | Units (SI) | Typical Order (Steam) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Liquid density | $\rho_l$ | kg/m³ | **958 kg/m³** (100 °C water) |
| Vapor density | $\rho_v$ | kg/m³ | **0.6 kg/m³** (steam) |
| Latent heat of vaporization | $\lambda$ or $h_{fg}$ | J/kg | **2.257 × 10⁶ J/kg** |
| Thermal conductivity of condensate | $k_l$ | W/m·K | **0.679 W/m·K** (water at 100 °C) |
| Dynamic viscosity of condensate | $\mu_l$ | Pa·s | **2.82 × 10⁻⁴ Pa·s** |
| Acceleration due to gravity | $g$ | m/s² | **9.81 m/s²** |
| Surface tension | $\sigma$ | N/m | **0.0589 N/m** (water–air at 100 °C) |
| Contact angle | $\theta$ | degrees | < 90° → wetting (filmwise); > 90° → non-wetting (dropwise) |

---

### 1.5 Why This Topic is a Board-Examiner Favorite

Condensation heat transfer coefficients can range from **h ≈ 100 W/m²·K (filmwise)** to **h ≈ 10⁵ W/m²·K (dropwise)** — a difference of **three orders of magnitude**. This is why Nusselt's analytical treatment (1916) is one of the most classical derivations in heat transfer, and KTU examiners reward clean, complete derivations handsomely.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Pool Boiling Curve (Furnace–Berenson, 1961)

The **boiling curve** plots heat flux $q''$ vs. excess temperature $\Delta T_e = T_s - T_{sat}$ on a log-log scale. It reveals **distinct regimes** that any KTU answer on boiling must identify.

```
q″ (W/m²)
   ▲
   │         ★ Critical Heat Flux (CHF)
   │        ╱  ╲
   │       ╱    ╲   ← Transition Boiling (unstable)
   │      ╱      ╲
   │     ╱        ● ─── ★ Leidenfrost Point
   │    ╱        ╱  ← Stable Film Boiling
   │   ╱        ╱
   │  ╱        ╱
   │ ╱   ●●●●●    ← Nucleate Boiling (peak h!)
   │╱  ●●●
   ├──────────────●─────●─────────●─────────●──────► ΔTₑ (°C)
   │   Natural    Nucleate   Transition   Film
   │   Conv.      Boiling    Boiling      Boiling
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Five Regimes Explained:**
>
> 1. **Natural Convection (ΔTₑ < 4 °C):** Liquid is superheated slightly but no bubbles form. Heat transfer governed by single-phase natural convection correlations.
>
> 2. **Nucleate Boiling (4 °C < ΔTₑ < 30 °C):** Bubbles nucleate at active sites, grow, detach. This regime has the **highest heat transfer coefficient** of all boiling regimes — because bubble agitation violently mixes the liquid near the surface.
>
> 3. **Transition Boiling (30 °C < ΔTₑ < 120 °C):** Unstable regime. Vapor patches and liquid patches coexist. Heat flux *decreases* with increasing ΔTₑ (negative slope) — this is the **boiling crisis** in the making.
>
> 4. **Stable Film Boiling (ΔTₑ > 120 °C):** Surface is blanketed by a continuous vapor layer. Heat is transferred by conduction/radiation through this vapor film and convection in the vapor.
>
> 5. **Leidenfrost Point:** The minimum point in the transition curve where the heat flux reaches a local minimum. Above this point, stable film boiling begins.

---

## 2.2 Condensation — Physical Mechanism Comparison

### 2.2.1 Filmwise Condensation (Wetting Fluids)

For fluids that **wet the surface** (e.g., pure steam on clean copper, organic vapors), the condensate forms a continuous film. As the film flows downward under gravity, its thickness grows. The thermal resistance is dominated by conduction through this ever-thicker liquid film.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Nusselt's Simplifying Assumptions (1916) — must be memorized for KTU:**
>
> 1. **Laminar, steady, incompressible** flow of condensate film.
> 2. The vapor is **stationary** and exerts **no shear** on the free surface of the film (i.e., vapor velocity is low — pure vapor at atmospheric pressure).
> 3. Heat transfer across the film is by **pure conduction** (linear temperature profile).
> 4. Fluid properties ($\rho_l, \mu_l, k_l$) are evaluated at the **mean film temperature** $T_f = (T_s + T_{sat})/2$.
> 5. The sensible heat of the cooling liquid film is **negligible** compared to the latent heat released.
> 6. The free surface of the film is at $T_{sat}$ (i.e., vapor and liquid interface is in thermal equilibrium).
> 7. Body force is gravity; surface tension and viscous dissipation are neglected.

### 2.2.2 Dropwise Condensation (Non-Wetting Fluids)

When the surface is **not wetted** by the liquid (e.g., steam on a surface coated with a hydrophobic promoter like oleic acid, Teflon, or a noble metal like gold), the condensate forms **discrete droplets** rather than a continuous film.

- Tiny droplets form at nucleation sites.
- Droplets grow by **direct condensation** on them and by **coalescence** with neighboring droplets.
- Once droplets reach a critical size, gravity pulls them down — they **roll/slide** off, sweeping smaller droplets (a "self-cleaning" effect).
- Fresh surface is continuously **re-exposed**, maintaining very high $h$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Dropwise condensation is *notoriously difficult* to maintain in industrial systems. Surface contamination, aging of the promoter coating, and non-condensable gases quickly degrade dropwise into filmwise condensation. Hence, almost all industrial condensers are designed assuming **filmwise condensation** as a conservative (worst-case) estimate.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Geometry / Situation | Heat Transfer Coefficient $h$ | Reynolds Number for Film |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Vertical Plate** | $h = 0.943 \left[ \dfrac{\rho_l (\rho_l - \rho_v) g \, \lambda \, k_l^3}{\mu_l \, \Delta T_e \, L} \right]^{1/4}$ | $\text{Re}_\delta = \dfrac{4 \Gamma}{\mu_l}$ |
| 2 | **Vertical Tube** | Same as vertical plate with $L$ = tube length | Same |
| 3 | **Vertical Plate — N Tubes Stacked** | $h_N = h_1 \cdot N^{-1/4}$ | $\text{Re} = \dfrac{4 \dot{m}}{\pi D \mu_l N}$ |
| 4 | **Single Horizontal Tube** (Nusselt) | $h = 0.725 \left[ \dfrac{\rho_l (\rho_l - \rho_v) g \, \lambda \, k_l^3}{\mu_l \, \Delta T_e \, D} \right]^{1/4}$ | $\text{Re} = \dfrac{4 \Gamma}{\mu_l}$ |
| 5 | **Horizontal Tube — N Tubes Vertically Stacked** | $h_N = h_1 \cdot N^{-1/4}$ | $\text{Re} = \dfrac{4 \dot{m}}{\pi D \mu_l N}$ |
| 6 | **Mean Condensate Film Thickness** (vertical) | $\delta(x) = \left[ \dfrac{4 \mu_l k_l \, \Delta T_e \, x}{\rho_l (\rho_l - \rho_v) g \, \lambda} \right]^{1/4}$ | — |
| 7 | **Heat Transfer Rate** | $\dot{Q} = h A \Delta T_e$ | — |
| 8 | **Dropwise Condensation** (empirical) | $h \approx 10 \times h_{\text{filmwise}}$ (engineering rule) | — |

**Symbols Key:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\Gamma &= \text{condensate mass flow rate per unit width} \; [\text{kg/s·m}] \\
\dot{m} &= \text{total condensate mass flow rate} \; [\text{kg/s}] \\
\Delta T_e &= T_{sat} - T_s = \text{excess temperature difference} \\
L &= \text{vertical length of plate / tube} \\
D &= \text{outer diameter of horizontal tube} \\
N &= \text{number of tubes in a vertical tier} \\
\lambda &= h_{fg} = \text{latent heat of vaporization}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** Nusselt-type equations are the backbone of design for **steam condensers in power plants**, **refrigeration and air-conditioning condensers**, **distillation column overhead condensers**, and **cryogenic liquefaction systems**. The horizontal-tube Nusselt equation is more commonly used in industry because most condenser tubes are arranged horizontally in a shell-and-tube heat exchanger.

---

## 2.4 Why These Formulas Matter (Engineering Context)

A steam condenser in a 500 MW thermal power plant must reject **thousands of MW** of latent heat from steam to the cooling water. A 10% error in predicting $h$ translates to **wrong sizing** of the heat exchanger — either over-design (capital waste) or under-design (plant trip / efficiency loss). This is precisely why Nusselt's classical result has stood the test of time for over a century.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examination Directive:** The Nusselt derivation for **vertical plate filmwise condensation** is the *single most expected long-answer derivation* in this module. You must write it from memory — every step counts. Below is the exhaustive version.

---

## 3.1 Nusselt's Derivation — Filmwise Condensation on a Vertical Plate

### Setup

Consider a vertical plate of height $L$ and width $W$ maintained at a uniform temperature $T_s$ in a quiescent atmosphere of pure vapor at $T_{sat}$, where $T_s < T_{sat}$. Define $\Delta T_e = T_{sat} - T_s$.

Coordinate system: $x$ is measured downward from the top of the plate; $y$ is the distance normal to the plate measured into the film.

### Step 1 — Boundary Layer Momentum Equation (Film Equation)

The film is thin, so a boundary-layer approximation is valid. The steady-state momentum equation in the $x$-direction, with only gravity as a body force, reduces to:

$$\begin{aligned}
\rho_l \left( u \frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + v \frac{\partial u}{\partial y} \right) &= \mu_l \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial y^2} + \rho_l g
\end{aligned}$$

*Explanation of terms:* LHS is the inertial (convective) term; RHS first term is the viscous shear; second term is gravitational body force per unit volume.

### Step 2 — Integral Momentum Approach

Because the film is thin, we integrate the momentum equation across the film thickness from $y = 0$ (wall) to $y = \delta(x)$ (free surface).

Define the **mass flow rate per unit width** $\Gamma$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\Gamma = \int_0^{\delta(x)} \rho_l u \, dy \quad \text{(kg/s·m)}
\end{aligned}$$

After integration and application of the following boundary conditions:

| Boundary Condition | Mathematical Form |
|---|---|
| No-slip at wall | $u(0) = 0$ |
| Zero shear at free surface (vapor exerts no drag) | $\left. \dfrac{\partial u}{\partial y} \right\rvert_{y = \delta} = 0$ |
| Inertia term negligible (lubrication approximation for thin film) | $u \partial u/\partial x \approx 0$ |

…the momentum integral reduces to:

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{d}{dx}\left(\int_0^\delta u^2 \, dy\right) = -\nu_l \left.\frac{\partial u}{\partial y}\right|_{y=0} + g \delta - \frac{\rho_v}{\rho_l} g \delta
\end{aligned}$$

For a thin laminar film, the velocity profile is assumed **parabolic** (a classical lubrication-theory result):

$$\begin{aligned}
u(y) = \frac{\rho_l (\rho_l - \rho_v) g}{\mu_l} \left( \delta y - \frac{y^2}{2} \right)
\end{aligned}$$

*Explanation:* This parabolic profile satisfies $u(0) = 0$ at the wall and zero shear $\partial u / \partial y = 0$ at $y = \delta$.

### Step 3 — Mass Balance (Condensate Accumulation)

By mass conservation, the increase in $\Gamma$ along $x$ equals the mass of vapor condensed per unit length of plate:

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{d\Gamma}{dx} = \frac{q''(x)}{\lambda}
\end{aligned}$$

where $q''(x)$ is the local heat flux at position $x$ (W/m²).

### Step 4 — Energy Equation (Conduction Across Film)

Since sensible heat change of the film is neglected, all heat conducted across the film at position $x$ comes from condensation at the free surface. Assuming a **linear temperature profile** across the film (pure conduction, since velocity field is parallel to the surface — no normal velocity):

$$\begin{aligned}
q''(x) = k_l \frac{\Delta T_e}{\delta(x)} = \frac{k_l (T_{sat} - T_s)}{\delta(x)}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 5 — Combining the Three Equations

From Step 4, $\delta(x) = k_l \Delta T_e / q''(x)$. Substitute into the momentum solution. After algebraic manipulation:

$$\begin{aligned}
\Gamma = \frac{\rho_l (\rho_l - \rho_v) g \delta^3}{3 \mu_l}
\end{aligned}$$

Combining with $\delta = k_l \Delta T_e / q''$ and $d\Gamma/dx = q''/\lambda$ gives an **ordinary differential equation** in $\delta(x)$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{d}{dx}\left[\frac{\rho_l^2 (\rho_l - \rho_v) g \delta^3}{3 \mu_l}\right] = \frac{k_l \Delta T_e}{\lambda \delta}
\end{aligned}$$

Integrating once from $x = 0$ (where $\delta = 0$) to $x$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\delta(x) = \left[ \frac{4 \mu_l k_l \Delta T_e \, x}{\rho_l (\rho_l - \rho_v) g \, \lambda} \right]^{1/4}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is Equation (1) — the local film thickness profile.** Note the **1/4 power dependence on $x$** — the film grows slowly as we go down the plate.

### Step 6 — Local Heat Transfer Coefficient

From $q''(x) = k_l \Delta T_e / \delta(x)$:

$$\begin{aligned}
q''(x) &= k_l \Delta T_e \left[ \frac{\rho_l (\rho_l - \rho_v) g \, \lambda}{4 \mu_l k_l \Delta T_e \, x} \right]^{1/4} \\
q''(x) &= \left[ \frac{\rho_l (\rho_l - \rho_v) g \, \lambda \, k_l^3 \, \Delta T_e^3}{4 \mu_l \, x} \right]^{1/4}
\end{aligned}$$

The **local** heat transfer coefficient is $h_x = q''(x) / \Delta T_e$:

$$\begin{aligned}
h_x = \left[ \frac{\rho_l (\rho_l - \rho_v) g \, \lambda \, k_l^3}{4 \mu_l \, x \, \Delta T_e} \right]^{1/4}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 7 — Average Heat Transfer Coefficient over Length L

The average $h_L$ over the entire plate is obtained by integrating the local heat flux:

$$\begin{aligned}
h_L = \frac{1}{L} \int_0^L h_x \, dx = \frac{4}{3} h_x \big|_{x = L}
\end{aligned}$$

Substituting and using $4/3 \times (1/4)^{1/4} = 0.943$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\boxed{\,h_L = 0.943 \left[ \frac{\rho_l (\rho_l - \rho_v) g \, \lambda \, k_l^3}{\mu_l \, L \, \Delta T_e} \right]^{1/4}\,}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Nusselt's Final Result (Vertical Plate).** This is THE equation for vertical condensation in filmwise mode. Memorize the coefficient **0.943** and the **1/4 power**.

---

## 3.2 Nusselt's Derivation — Single Horizontal Tube

For a horizontal tube of outer diameter $D$, the condensate film drains as an axisymmetric sheet. Following an analogous analysis where the gravity-driven film Reynolds number defines the flow:

$$\begin{aligned}
\Gamma = \frac{\dot{m}}{\pi D}
\end{aligned}$$

Equating the gravity force on the film to the viscous drag at the wall, and applying the conduction-across-film thermal resistance, Nusselt obtained:

$$\begin{aligned}
\boxed{\,h_D = 0.725 \left[ \frac{\rho_l (\rho_l - \rho_v) g \, \lambda \, k_l^3}{\mu_l \, D \, \Delta T_e} \right]^{1/4}\,}
\end{aligned}$$

---

## 3.3 Effect of Multiple Horizontal Tubes (Vertical Tier)

For $N$ horizontal tubes stacked vertically, the condensate from the upper tube falls onto the lower one, increasing the load. The average $h$ for the entire tier is:

$$\begin{aligned}
\boxed{\,h_N = h_1 \cdot N^{-1/4}\,}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Rule of Thumb:** Doubling the number of tubes in a vertical tier reduces the average heat transfer coefficient by a factor of $2^{1/4} \approx 1.19$ (≈ 19% drop). This is why condensers in power plants often use a *single* horizontal pass — multi-tube vertical tiers are penalized.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem (14-Mark Type):** Saturated steam at atmospheric pressure ($T_{sat} = 100$ °C) condenses on a vertical surface of height $L = 0.5$ m maintained at $T_s = 90$ °C. Estimate:
(a) The average heat transfer coefficient.
(b) The condensate mass flow rate.
(c) The local film thickness at $x = 0.5$ m.

**Given fluid properties (evaluated at film mean temperature $T_f = 95$ °C):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\rho_l &= 961.9 \; \text{kg/m³}, \quad \rho_v = 0.5 \; \text{kg/m³} \quad (\rho_l - \rho_v \approx 961.4) \\
k_l &= 0.681 \; \text{W/m·K}, \quad \mu_l = 2.96 \times 10^{-4} \; \text{Pa·s} \\
\lambda &= h_{fg} &= 2.259 \times 10^6 \; \text{J/kg} \\
g &= 9.81 \; \text{m/s²}, \quad \Delta T_e = 100 - 90 = 10 \; \text{K}
\end{aligned}$$

### (a) Average heat transfer coefficient

$$\begin{aligned}
h_L &= 0.943 \left[ \frac{(961.9)(961.4)(9.81)(2.259 \times 10^6)(0.681)^3}{(2.96 \times 10^{-4})(0.5)(10)} \right]^{1/4}
\end{aligned}$$

Compute numerator step by step:

$$\begin{aligned}
\rho_l (\rho_l - \rho_v) &= 961.9 \times 961.4 \approx 9.246 \times 10^{5} \; \text{kg}^2/\text{m}^6 \\
\times g &= 9.246 \times 10^{5} \times 9.81 \approx 9.07 \times 10^{6} \\
\times \lambda &= 9.07 \times 10^{6} \times 2.259 \times 10^6 \approx 2.049 \times 10^{13} \\
\times k_l^3 &= 2.049 \times 10^{13} \times (0.681)^3 \approx 2.049 \times 10^{13} \times 0.316 \approx 6.47 \times 10^{12}
\end{aligned}$$

Denominator:

$$\begin{aligned}
\mu_l L \Delta T_e = 2.96 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.5 \times 10 = 1.48 \times 10^{-3}
\end{aligned}$$

So the bracketed term:

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{6.47 \times 10^{12}}{1.48 \times 10^{-3}} = 4.37 \times 10^{15}
\end{aligned}$$

Taking the 1/4 power:

$$\begin{aligned}
(4.37 \times 10^{15})^{1/4} = (4.37)^{1/4} \times (10^{15})^{1/4} = 1.446 \times 10^{3.75} = 1.446 \times 5623 \approx 8133
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\boxed{h_L \approx 0.943 \times 8133 \approx 7670 \; \text{W/m}^2 \cdot \text{K}}
\end{aligned}$$

### (b) Mass flow rate per unit width

$$\begin{aligned}
\Gamma &= \frac{h_L \cdot \Delta T_e \cdot L}{\lambda} = \frac{7670 \times 10 \times 0.5}{2.259 \times 10^6} \\
\Gamma &= \frac{38350}{2.259 \times 10^6} \approx 0.01698 \; \text{kg/s·m}
\end{aligned}$$

### (c) Local film thickness at $x = L = 0.5$ m

$$\begin{aligned}
\delta(L) &= \frac{k_l \Delta T_e}{q''(L)} = \frac{k_l \Delta T_e}{h_L \Delta T_e} = \frac{k_l}{h_L} \\
\delta(L) &= \frac{0.681}{7670} \approx 8.88 \times 10^{-5} \; \text{m} \approx 88.8 \; \mu\text{m}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\boxed{\delta(L) \approx 89 \; \mu\text{m}}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity Check:** Film thickness on the order of 10⁻⁵ to 10⁻⁴ m is typical for steam condensation — the **lubrication-theory approximation** (thin film) is justified.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block — Pool Boiling Regimes Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Pool Boiling Process]:::topNode
    A --> B[Stage 1: Natural Convection]
    A --> C[Stage 2: Nucleate Boiling]
    A --> D[Stage 3: Transition Boiling]
    A --> E[Stage 4: Stable Film Boiling]

    B --> B1[Delta T less than 4 C]
    B1 --> B2[No bubble formation]
    B2 --> B3[Single phase natural convection governs]

    C --> C1[Delta T between 4 and 30 C]
    C1 --> C2[Active nucleation sites]
    C2 --> C3[Bubble growth, detachment, agitation]
    C3 --> C4[Highest heat transfer coefficient region]
    C4 --> C5[Approaches Critical Heat Flux CHF]

    D --> D1[Unstable regime]
    D1 --> D2[Vapor patches and liquid patches coexist]
    D2 --> D3[Heat flux decreases as Delta T increases]
    D3 --> D4[Reaches Leidenfrost Point minimum]

    E --> E1[Delta T greater than 120 C]
    E1 --> E2[Continuous vapor blanket on surface]
    E2 --> E3[Conduction through vapor plus radiation]
    E3 --> E4[Heat transfer slowly recovers]

```

    S -- Contact Angle greater than 90 deg<br/>Hydrophobic Surface --> DM[Dropwise Condensation Mode]:::dropNode

    FM --> F1[Continuous liquid film covers surface]

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Differentiate between filmwise and dropwise condensation. Which gives a higher heat transfer coefficient and why?**

> **Model Answer (3 Marks):**
>
> | Feature | Filmwise Condensation | Dropwise Condensation |
> |---|---|---|
> | Surface wettability | Wetting fluid (contact angle < 90°) | Non-wetting fluid (contact angle > 90°) |
> | Condensate form | Continuous liquid film | Discrete droplets |
> | Thermal resistance | Conduction through growing film (increases with $x$) | Mostly through thin liquid–vapor interface |
> | $h$ value | 100 – 10,000 W/m²·K | 10,000 – 1,00,000 W/m²·K |
> | Stability | Stable, predictable | Unstable (degrades with surface aging) |
>
> **Dropwise condensation gives a higher heat transfer coefficient** because droplets coalesce and roll/slide off, exposing fresh surface to vapor continuously. The thermal resistance remains low because the surface is never blanketed by a thick film. **(3 marks)**

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**State the assumptions made by Nusselt in his analysis of filmwise condensation on a vertical plate.**

> **Model Answer (3 Marks):**
>
> 1. Laminar, steady, incompressible flow of condensate film. **(1 mark)**
> 2. Vapor is stationary and exerts no shear on the free surface of the film. **(0.5 mark)**
> 3. Heat transfer across the film is by pure conduction (linear temperature profile). **(0.5 mark)**
> 4. Fluid properties are constant, evaluated at the mean film temperature. **(0.5 mark)**
> 5. Sensible heat change of condensate is negligible compared to latent heat. **(0.5 mark)**
