## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Choose **ONE** of the following:

---

### **Question A [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

**(a)** Derive an expression for the average heat transfer coefficient for filmwise condensation of a pure saturated vapor on a vertical plate using Nusselt's theory. **(10 Marks)**

**(b)** Saturated steam at 1 atm condenses on a vertical tube of length 1 m and outer diameter 25 mm. The tube surface temperature is 95 °C. Estimate the heat transfer coefficient and the rate of condensate flow per tube. **(4 Marks)**

> **Property data at mean film temperature $T_f \approx 97.5$ °C:**
> $\rho_l = 960$ kg/m³, $\rho_v = 0.6$ kg/m³, $k_l = 0.68$ W/m·K, $\mu_l = 2.9 \times 10^{-4}$ Pa·s, $\lambda = 2.26 \times 10^6$ J/kg, $T_{sat} = 100$ °C, $\Delta T_e = 5$ K.

#### **Model Solution for (a) — Full Derivation (10 Marks)**

For complete derivation, **see Section 3.1, Steps 1 through 7** of these notes. The examiner's marking distribution is typically:

| Step / Result | Marks Allocated |
|---|---|
| Setting up momentum integral equation with BCs | 2 |
| Parabolic velocity profile derivation | 2 |
| Combining with mass balance & energy equation | 2 |
| Integrating to get $\delta(x)$ profile | 2 |
| Final $h_L = 0.943 \left[ \dfrac{\rho_l (\rho_l - \rho_v) g \lambda k_l^3}{\mu_l L \Delta T_e} \right]^{1/4}$ | 2 |

#### **Model Solution for (b) — Numerical (4 Marks)**

Treating the vertical tube as a vertical plate (since $L/D \gg 1$, i.e., $1/0.025 = 40$):

$$\begin{aligned}
h_L &= 0.943 \left[ \frac{(960)(959.4)(9.81)(2.26 \times 10^6)(0.68)^3}{(2.9 \times 10^{-4})(1)(5)} \right]^{1/4}
\end{aligned}$$

Numerator:

$$\begin{aligned}
960 \times 959.4 &\approx 9.21 \times 10^5 \\
\times 9.81 &\approx 9.04 \times 10^6 \\
\times 2.26 \times 10^6 &\approx 2.043 \times 10^{13} \\
\times (0.68)^3 = 0.3144 &\approx 6.42 \times 10^{12}
\end{aligned}$$

Denominator: $2.9 \times 10^{-4} \times 1 \times 5 = 1.45 \times 10^{-3}$

Bracketed term: $\dfrac{6.42 \times 10^{12}}{1.45 \times 10^{-3}} = 4.43 \times 10^{15}$

$$\begin{aligned}
(4.43 \times 10^{15})^{1/4} &= (4.43)^{1/4} \times 10^{3.75} = 1.452 \times 5623 \approx 8163 \\
h_L &= 0.943 \times 8163 \approx \mathbf{7700 \; W/m^2 \cdot K}
\end{aligned}$$

Condensate rate per tube (perimeter $= \pi D = \pi \times 0.025 = 0.0785$ m):

$$\begin{aligned}
\dot{m} &= h_L \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_e / \lambda \\
A &= \pi D L = 0.0785 \times 1 = 0.0785 \; \text{m}^2 \\
\dot{m} &= \frac{7700 \times 0.0785 \times 5}{2.26 \times 10^6} = \frac{3022.25}{2.26 \times 10^6} \\
\dot{m} &\approx \mathbf{1.34 \times 10^{-3} \; kg/s \;\; per \; tube}
\end{aligned}$$

---

### **Question B [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**(a)** Explain the various regimes of pool boiling with the help of a neat sketch of the boiling curve. State the significance of the Critical Heat Flux (CHF) and Leidenfrost Point. **(8 Marks)**

**(b)** A horizontal tube of 50 mm diameter has saturated steam condensing on its outer surface at atmospheric pressure. The tube wall temperature is 95 °C. Estimate the average heat transfer coefficient and the condensate mass flow rate per unit length. **(6 Marks)**

> **Property data:** $\rho_l = 960$ kg/m³, $\rho_v = 0.6$ kg/m³, $k_l = 0.68$ W/m·K, $\mu_l = 2.95 \times 10^{-4}$ Pa·s, $\lambda = 2.26 \times 10^6$ J/kg, $T_{sat} = 100$ °C.

#### **Model Solution for (a) — Pool Boiling Regimes (8 Marks)**

Refer to **Section 2.1** for the boiling curve sketch and regime descriptions. The marking distribution:

| Item | Marks |
|---|---|
| Neat sketch of boiling curve (log $q''$ vs $\Delta T_e$) | 2 |
| Description of 5 regimes (natural conv., nucleate, transition, film, post-Leidenfrost) | 4 |
| Significance of CHF and Leidenfrost Point | 2 |

> **Key Statements for full marks:**
>
> **Critical Heat Flux (CHF):** The maximum heat flux attainable in nucleate boiling. Beyond CHF, the heater surface can no longer sustain the rate of vapor generation, leading to vapor blanketing and a sharp temperature rise. In nuclear reactors, CHF is a **safety limit** — operating beyond it can cause fuel rod burnout.
>
> **Leidenfrost Point:** The minimum heat flux point in the transition boiling regime. It marks the lower limit of stable film boiling. Below this point, a liquid can touch the surface; above it, the vapor blanket is stable.

#### **Model Solution for (b) — Horizontal Tube (6 Marks)**

Nusselt's horizontal-tube equation:

$$\begin{aligned}
h_D &= 0.725 \left[ \frac{(960)(959.4)(9.81)(2.26 \times 10^6)(0.68)^3}{(2.95 \times 10^{-4})(0.050)(5)} \right]^{1/4}
\end{aligned}$$

Numerator (same as vertical plate): $\approx 6.42 \times 10^{12}$

Denominator: $2.95 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.05 \times 5 = 7.375 \times 10^{-5}$

Bracketed term: $\dfrac{6.42 \times 10^{12}}{7.375 \times 10^{-5}} = 8.70 \times 10^{16}$

$$\begin{aligned}
(8.70 \times 10^{16})^{1/4} &= (8.70)^{1/4} \times 10^4 = 1.717 \times 10000 \approx 17170 \\
h_D &= 0.725 \times 17170 \approx \mathbf{12,450 \; W/m^2 \cdot K}
\end{aligned}$$

Heat transfer rate per unit length:

$$\begin{aligned}
\dot{Q}/L &= h_D \cdot (\pi D) \cdot \Delta T_e \\
\dot{Q}/L &= 12450 \times \pi \times 0.05 \times 5 \\
\dot{Q}/L &= 12450 \times 0.1571 \times 5 \\
\dot{Q}/L &\approx 9779 \; \text{W/m}
\end{aligned}$$

Condensate rate per unit length:

$$\begin{aligned}
\dot{m}/L &= \dot{Q}/(L \lambda) = 9779 / (2.26 \times 10^6) \\
&\approx \mathbf{4.33 \times 10^{-3} \; kg/s \cdot m}
\end{aligned}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to convert units** — $\mu_l$ must be in Pa·s (not centipoise). **1 cp = 10⁻³ Pa·s.** Common error.
> 2. **Evaluating properties at the WRONG temperature** — always use mean film temperature $T_f = (T_{sat} + T_s)/2$, NOT at $T_{sat}$. **(2 marks lost if missed)**
> 3. **Using $\rho_l$ instead of $(\rho_l - \rho_v)$ in the buoyancy term** — for low-pressure steam, $\rho_v$ is negligible, but for high-pressure systems this matters.
> 4. **Forgetting to include the buoyancy correction** $(\rho_l - \rho_v)$ — this is a frequent slip.
> 5. **Confusing the vertical and horizontal tube formulas** — the coefficients differ (**0.943 vs 0.725**) and so does the characteristic length ($L$ vs $D$).
> 6. **Dropwise condensation problems** — students often quote the empirical "$h \approx 10 \times h_{\text{film}}$" rule without specifying the surface treatment; always mention the **promoter agent** (e.g., oleic acid, Teflon, gold).
> 7. **Not drawing the free-body diagram of the condensate film** in the derivation — examiners allocate 2 marks just for this.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Boiling** = liquid → vapor *inside bulk fluid*; **Condensation** = vapor → liquid *at surface*.
- [x] Pool boiling has **5 regimes**: Natural Convection → Nucleate Boiling → Transition Boiling → Stable Film Boiling (post-Leidenfrost).
- [x] **Nucleate boiling** gives the **highest $h$**; **CHF** is the safe upper limit; **Leidenfrost Point** is the lower limit of stable film boiling.
- [x] **Filmwise condensation** = continuous film, lower $h$ (≈ 10³–10⁴ W/m²·K); **Dropwise condensation** = discrete droplets, much higher $h$ (≈ 10⁴–10⁵ W/m²·K).
- [x] **Nusselt's assumptions** must be quoted verbatim in derivations.
- [x] **Vertical plate formula:** $h_L = 0.943 \left[ \dfrac{\rho_l (\rho_l - \rho_v) g \lambda k_l^3}{\mu_l L \Delta T_e} \right]^{1/4}$
- [x] **Horizontal tube formula:** $h_D = 0.725 \left[ \dfrac{\rho_l (\rho_l - \rho_v) g \lambda k_l^3}{\mu_l D \Delta T_e} \right]^{1/4}$
- [x] **Vertical N-tube stack:** $h_N = h_1 \cdot N^{-1/4}$
- [x] **Horizontal N-tube tier:** $h_N = h_1 \cdot N^{-1/4}$ (same form, applied to $\dot{m}/(\pi D N)$).
- [x] **Film thickness profile:** $\delta(x) \propto x^{1/4}$ — grows slowly downward.
- [x] Properties evaluated at **mean film temperature** $T_f = (T_{sat} + T_s)/2$.
- [x] Dropwise condensation **rare in industry** — design conservatively using filmwise.
- [x] Non-condensable gases (air) drastically reduce condensation $h$ — they accumulate at the liquid–vapor interface and block condensation. **Always purge air in steam condensers.**
- [x] **Contact angle** is the geometric signature: $\theta < 90°$ → filmwise; $\theta > 90°$ → dropwise.
- [x] For the KTU exam: **state assumptions → derive with integration → substitute numbers → box the answer**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Diffusion Mass Transfer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET754 - HEAT AND MASS TRANSFER
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Diffusion Mass Transfer

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Diffusion Mass Transfer** is the transport mechanism by which chemical species (atoms, molecules, or ions) spontaneously move from a region of higher concentration to a region of lower concentration within a single phase (gas, liquid, or solid), driven purely by the concentration gradient and random molecular motion — without the requirement of bulk fluid motion.

In the context of the KTU 2024 Scheme, diffusion is mathematically formalized by **Fick's First Law of Diffusion (1855)**, which is the mass-transfer analogue of Fourier's Law of heat conduction. The governing transport equation in one dimension is:

$$J_A^* = -D_{AB} \frac{\partial C_A}{\partial x}$$

where:
- $J_A^*$ = **molar flux of species A relative to the molar average velocity** (kmol/m²·s)
- $D_{AB}$ = **binary diffusion coefficient (mass diffusivity)** of A through B (m²/s)
- $\frac{\partial C_A}{\partial x}$ = concentration gradient of species A in the x-direction (kmol/m⁴)
- The **negative sign** signifies that flux occurs *down* the concentration gradient (from high to low concentration)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** The KTU 2024 Scheme emphasizes three core mass transfer configurations: (1) Steady-state equimolar counter-diffusion, (2) Steady-state diffusion of A through stagnant B (Stefan Diffusion), and (3) Non-steady (transient) diffusion governed by Fick's Second Law. Mastery of all three is mandatory for the University Examination.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Aromatic Coffee" Intuition

Imagine you are sitting in a closed room with a freshly brewed cup of hot coffee. Even though the air in the room is perfectly still (no fan, no breeze), after a few minutes, the **aroma of coffee** reaches every corner of the room. How is this possible?

Each coffee aroma molecule, being in constant chaotic motion (translational kinetic energy), randomly collides billions of times per second with nitrogen, oxygen, and other air molecules. With each collision, the molecule is deflected in a random direction. Statistically, however, molecules near the coffee cup experience *more* neighbors pushing them away from the cup (where concentration is highest) than toward it. This **net statistical bias** of random motion — averaged over trillions of molecules and trillions of collisions — produces a *macroscopic* flux of aroma from cup to your nose. **This is diffusion.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Distinction from Convection:** Diffusion is a *molecular-level* phenomenon requiring no bulk flow. Convection, in contrast, requires bulk fluid motion (forced or natural). In a moving fluid, both occur simultaneously — this is called **convective mass transfer**, and the total flux is the sum of both contributions.

## 1.3 Modes of Mass Transfer — The Triad

Mass transfer occurs by **two fundamental mechanisms** which combine in practice:

| Mode | Driver | Analogy |
|------|--------|---------|
| **Molecular Diffusion** | Concentration gradient + random molecular motion | Heat conduction (Fourier's Law analogue) |
| **Convective Mass Transfer** | Bulk fluid motion (forced or natural) | Heat convection (Newton's Law of Cooling analogue) |

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Mnemonic — "DCM" Triangle:** **D**iffusion (molecular) + **C**onvection (bulk) = **M**ass Transfer. Any real mass transfer problem involves BOTH simultaneously; diffusion dominates in quiescent fluids, convection dominates in flowing fluids.

## 1.4 Concentration Nomenclature — Know Your Variables

Different KTU textbook problems use different concentration definitions. **Do not mix them up in exams:**

| Symbol | Name | Units | Definition |
|--------|------|-------|------------|
| $\rho_A$ | Mass concentration of A | kg/m³ | Mass of A per unit volume of mixture |
| $C_A$ | Molar concentration of A | kmol/m³ | Moles of A per unit volume of mixture |
| $w_A$ | Mass fraction of A | dimensionless | $\rho_A / \rho$ |
| $x_A$ | Mole fraction of A | dimensionless | $C_A / C$ |
| $\rho$ | Total mass concentration | kg/m³ | $\sum \rho_i$ |
| $C$ | Total molar concentration | kmol/m³ | $\sum C_i$ |

The relationship $\rho_A = C_A \cdot M_A$ where $M_A$ is the molecular weight of A in kg/kmol.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Theoretical Foundation — Why Diffusion Happens

From the kinetic theory of gases, the **flux of species A relative to a stationary plane** can be derived by considering the number of molecules crossing a unit area per unit time from a distance equal to the **mean free path** $\lambda$. The resulting expression is:

$$D_{AB} = \frac{1}{3} \lambda \bar{v}$$

where $\bar{v} = \sqrt{8RT/(\pi M_A)}$ is the mean molecular speed. This shows that **diffusivity increases with temperature** (as $\sqrt{T}$) and decreases with molecular size and molecular weight.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "−" sign in Fick's Law?** Flux is defined as the *amount crossing per unit area per unit time in the positive x-direction*. Since molecules flow from high to low concentration, the gradient $\partial C_A/\partial x$ is *negative* when concentration decreases with x. The negative sign makes the flux come out *positive* (in the direction of decreasing concentration). This sign convention is critical in KTU problems.

## 2.2 Fick's First Law — The Two Reference Velocities

A subtle but examination-critical distinction exists between fluxes measured relative to **different reference frames**. There are three reference velocities:

1. **Molar average velocity** $\vec{v^*}$ (used in Fick's First Law for gas mixtures)
2. **Mass average velocity** $\vec{v}$ (used in convective mass transfer problems)
3. **Stationary (fixed) coordinate** $\vec{v} = 0$ (used in solids, stagnant films)

The **fundamental Fick's First Law** in terms of *molar flux relative to molar average velocity* is:

$$\vec{N_A^*} = -D_{AB} \nabla C_A$$

For a binary mixture A + B, the **absolute molar flux** (relative to fixed coordinates) of species A is:

$$\vec{N_A} = -D_{AB} \nabla C_A + x_A (\vec{N_A} + \vec{N_B})$$

The first term is **diffusive flux**, the second is **bulk convective flux**.

## 2.3 Three Canonical KTU Problem Configurations

### Configuration 1: Equimolar Counter-Diffusion (EMCD)

When two species A and B diffuse in **opposite directions at equal molar rates** ($\vec{N_A} = -\vec{N_B}$), bulk flow contribution vanishes. This occurs in binary distillation where latent heats are equal.

### Configuration 2: Diffusion of A Through Stagnant B (Stefan Diffusion)

When B is **non-diffusing** ($\vec{N_B} = 0$), e.g., water vapor diffusing through stagnant air. A bulk flow of A in the direction of diffusion is induced.

### Configuration 3: Non-Steady (Transient) Diffusion — Fick's Second Law

When concentration changes with time, governed by:

$$\frac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} = D_{AB} \frac{\partial^2 C_A}{\partial x^2}$$

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Equations

| # | Equation | Form | Application / Condition |
|---|----------|------|--------------------------|
| 1 | $N_A = -D_{AB} \dfrac{dC_A}{dx}$ | Fick's First Law (1-D) | Stationary medium, no bulk flow |
| 2 | $N_A = -D_{AB} \dfrac{dC_A}{dx} + \dfrac{C_A}{C} N$ | Absolute flux with bulk flow | Binary mixture with total flux N |
| 3 | $N_A = \dfrac{D_{AB} \cdot C}{L} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{1-x_{A2}}{1-x_{A1}}\right)$ | A through stagnant B (Stefan) | $\vec{N_B} = 0$ |
| 4 | $N_A = \dfrac{D_{AB}}{L}(C_{A1} - C_{A2})$ | Equimolar counter-diffusion | $\vec{N_A} = -\vec{N_B}$ |
| 5 | $N_A = \dfrac{D_{AB} \cdot P}{R T L} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{P - P_{A2}}{P - P_{A1}}\right)$ | Stefan diffusion in gases | Ideal gas, total P constant |
| 6 | $N_A = \dfrac{D_{AB} \cdot P}{R T L (1-x_A)_{lm}} (P_{A1} - P_{A2})$ | Linearized Stefan flux | Log-mean driving force |
| 7 | $\dfrac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} = D_{AB} \dfrac{\partial^2 C_A}{\partial x^2}$ | Fick's Second Law | Non-steady, 1-D |
| 8 | $\dfrac{C_A - C_{A0}}{C_{AS} - C_{A0}} = \text{erf}\!\left(\dfrac{x}{2\sqrt{D_{AB} t}}\right)$ | Semi-infinite solid solution | Transient diffusion into solid |
| 9 | $D_{AB} = \dfrac{0.001858 \, T^{3/2}}{P \, \sigma_{AB}^2 \, \Omega_D} \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{M_A} + \dfrac{1}{M_B}}$ | Chapman-Enskog equation | Binary gas diffusivity (SI: m²/s) |
| 10 | $Sh = \dfrac{k_c L}{D_{AB}}$ | Sherwood number | Convective mass transfer analog of Nusselt |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Log-Mean Convention:** Throughout KTU mass transfer problems, whenever a driving force varies non-linearly with position (as in Stefan diffusion), use the **log-mean** of the terms in the bracket, not the arithmetic mean. The arithmetic mean gives wrong answers by 5–15% in typical exam problems.

## 2.5 Engineering Applications

Diffusion mass transfer is the cornerstone of:
- **Distillation columns** — mass transfer between vapor and liquid phases
- **Gas absorption** (e.g., $\text{CO}_2$ removal using amine solutions)
- **Drying operations** — moisture removal from solids
- **Cement and ceramic processing** — solid-state sintering
- **Semiconductor doping** — diffusion of dopants into silicon wafers
- **Lung oxygen transport** — diffusion across alveolar membranes
- **Polymer membrane separations**
- **Food preservation** — salt/sugar diffusion into foods

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Utility:** The Chapman-Enskog equation (Eq. 9) allows chemical engineers to **estimate unknown diffusion coefficients** for novel gas pairs without conducting experiments — saving enormous time and cost during process design. The collision integral $\Omega_D$ and characteristic length $\sigma_{AB}$ are tabulated for most common species.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Molar Flux for A Diffusing Through Stagnant B (Stefan Diffusion)

This is the **most important derivation in KTU Module 4** and appears in nearly every past paper.

### Step 1: Start with the Absolute Flux Equation

For a binary mixture of A and B, the absolute molar flux of A is:

$$N_A = -D_{AB} \frac{dC_A}{dx} + \frac{C_A}{C} (N_A + N_B)$$

**Logic:** The first term is the diffusive contribution (Fick). The second term is the bulk convective contribution, where the molar average velocity times the concentration of A gives the moles of A carried by bulk flow. The factor $(N_A + N_B)$ is the *total* molar flux.

### Step 2: Apply the Stagnant-B Condition

For A diffusing through stagnant B, the flux of B is **zero** everywhere:

$$N_B = 0$$

**Logic:** Physically, B might be air (non-reactive) that does not move on its own. It is only "carried" by the bulk flow induced by A's diffusion. By setting $N_B = 0$ throughout, we constrain the bulk flow to be such that it just cancels the diffusive flux of B.

### Step 3: Substitute and Simplify

Substituting $N_B = 0$ into the flux equation:

$$N_A = -D_{AB} \frac{dC_A}{dx} + \frac{C_A}{C} N_A$$

Rearranging to isolate the diffusive term:

$$N_A \left(1 - \frac{C_A}{C}\right) = -D_{AB} \frac{dC_A}{dx}$$

Recognize that $(1 - C_A/C) = C_B/C$, so:

$$N_A \cdot \frac{C_B}{C} = -D_{AB} \frac{dC_A}{dx}$$

### Step 4: Introduce the Assumption of Constant Total Concentration

For **ideal gases at constant T and P**, the total molar concentration $C = P/(RT)$ is constant. Also, in a binary system $C_B = C - C_A$, so $dC_B = -dC_A$. Substituting:

$$N_A = -\frac{D_{AB} \cdot C}{C - C_A} \frac{dC_A}{dx}$$

### Step 5: Separate Variables and Integrate

Separating variables:

$$N_A \, dx = -D_{AB} \cdot C \frac{dC_A}{C - C_A}$$

Integrating from $x = 0$ (where $C_A = C_{A1}$) to $x = L$ (where $C_A = C_{A2}$):

$$\int_0^L N_A \, dx = -D_{AB} \cdot C \int_{C_{A1}}^{C_{A2}} \frac{dC_A}{C - C_A}$$

Since $N_A$ is constant (steady state), the left side gives $N_A \cdot L$:

$$N_A \cdot L = -D_{AB} \cdot C \left[ -\ln(C - C_A) \right]_{C_{A1}}^{C_{A2}}$$

$$N_A \cdot L = D_{AB} \cdot C \ln\!\left(\frac{C - C_{A1}}{C - C_{A2}}\right)$$

### Step 6: Final Result — Stefan Flux Equation

$$\boxed{N_A = \frac{D_{AB} \cdot C}{L} \ln\!\left(\frac{C - C_{A1}}{C - C_{A2}}\right) = \frac{D_{AB} \cdot C}{L} \ln\!\left(\frac{C_{B2}}{C_{B1}}\right)}$$

For ideal gases, $C = P/(RT)$, $C_A = P_A/(RT)$, $C_B = P_B/(RT)$, so:

$$N_A = \frac{D_{AB} \cdot P}{R T L} \ln\!\left(\frac{P - P_{A1}}{P - P_{A2}}\right) = \frac{D_{AB} \cdot P}{R T L} \ln\!\left(\frac{P_{B2}}{P_{B1}}\right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Physical Insight:** The "1" in $\ln(C_B)$ accounts for the *bulk flow* enhancement. If we naively used simple Fick's Law without the bulk-flow correction, we would underestimate $N_A$ by a factor that grows with concentration. This is why equimolar counter-diffusion and stagnant-B diffusion give **different** numerical answers for the same concentration gradient.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Fick's Second Law from a Mass Balance

### Step 1: Set Up a Differential Control Volume

Consider a thin slab of thickness $dx$ in a binary mixture. The total molar flux entering the slab at $x$ is $N_A \vert_x$ and leaving at $x + dx$ is $N_A \vert_{x+dx}$.

### Step 2: Apply Conservation of Mass

$$\text{Rate of accumulation} = \text{In} - \text{Out}$$

$$\frac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} \cdot A \cdot dx = N_A \vert_x \cdot A - N_A \vert_{x+dx} \cdot A$$

### Step 3: Expand Using Taylor Series

$$N_A \vert_{x+dx} = N_A \vert_x + \frac{\partial N_A}{\partial x} dx + \mathcal{O}(dx^2)$$

Substituting:

$$\frac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} = -\frac{\partial N_A}{\partial x}$$

### Step 4: Substitute Fick's First Law (No Bulk Flow)

Assuming negligible bulk flow and $D_{AB}$ constant:

$$N_A = -D_{AB} \frac{\partial C_A}{\partial x}$$

Therefore:

$$\frac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} = -\frac{\partial}{\partial x}\left(-D_{AB} \frac{\partial C_A}{\partial x}\right) = D_{AB} \frac{\partial^2 C_A}{\partial x^2}$$

### Step 5: Final Result

$$\boxed{\frac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} = D_{AB} \frac{\partial^2 C_A}{\partial x^2}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mathematical Nature:** Fick's Second Law is a **parabolic partial differential equation**, identical in form to the heat equation. Solutions for various boundary conditions (semi-infinite solid, slab, cylinder, sphere) are obtained by analogous methods to those used in transient heat conduction. The famous error function solution for a semi-infinite medium appears extensively in KTU numerical problems.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Typical 14-Mark Style)

**Problem:** Ammonia (A) is diffusing through a stagnant film of nitrogen (B) of thickness **1.5 mm** at **298 K** and **1 atm** total pressure. The partial pressure of $\text{NH}_3$ at one boundary is **$P_{A1} = 0.20 \text{ atm}$** and at the other is **$P_{A2} = 0.05 \text{ atm}$**. The diffusion coefficient is **$D_{AB} = 2.30 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$**. Calculate the steady-state molar flux of $\text{NH}_3$.

### Solution:

**Step 1 — Identify the configuration.** This is *A through stagnant B* → use Stefan equation.

**Step 2 — Write the Stefan equation:**

$$N_A = \frac{D_{AB} \cdot P}{R T L} \ln\!\left(\frac{P - P_{A1}}{P - P_{A2}}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Substitute known values:**

- $P = 1 \text{ atm} = 101325 \text{ Pa}$
- $R = 8.314 \text{ J/(mol·K)}$
- $T = 298 \text{ K}$
- $L = 1.5 \text{ mm} = 1.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$
- $D_{AB} = 2.30 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$

**Step 4 — Compute the total molar concentration $C$:**

$$C = \frac{P}{RT} = \frac{101325}{8.314 \times 298} = 40.89 \text{ mol/m}^3$$

**Step 5 — Compute the logarithm argument:**

$$\frac{P - P_{A1}}{P - P_{A2}} = \frac{1 - 0.20}{1 - 0.05} = \frac{0.80}{0.95} = 0.8421$$

$$\ln(0.8421) = -0.1719$$

**Step 6 — Compute the flux:**

$$N_A = \frac{2.30 \times 10^{-5} \times 40.89}{1.5 \times 10^{-3}} \times (-0.1719)$$

$$N_A = (6.270 \times 10^{-4}) \times (-0.1719)$$

$$\boxed{N_A = -1.078 \times 10^{-4} \text{ mol/(m}^2\cdot\text{s)}}$$

The **negative sign** indicates flux is in the negative x-direction (from $P_{A1}$ to $P_{A2}$). The **magnitude** is:

$$|N_A| = 1.08 \times 10^{-4} \text{ mol/(m}^2\cdot\text{s)}$$

**Step 7 — Verification (using log-mean form).** The log-mean of $(P - P_A)$ is:

$$(P - P_A)_{lm} = \frac{(P - P_{A1}) - (P - P_{A2})}{\ln[(P - P_{A1})/(P - P_{A2})]} = \frac{0.80 - 0.95}{-0.1719} = 0.8731 \text{ atm}$$

$$N_A = \frac{D_{AB} \cdot P}{R T L} \cdot \frac{P_{A1} - P_{A2}}{(P - P_A)_{lm}} = \frac{2.30 \times 10^{-5} \times 40.89}{1.5 \times 10^{-3}} \cdot \frac{0.15}{0.8731}$$

$$N_A = 6.270 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.1718 = 1.077 \times 10^{-4} \text{ mol/(m}^2\cdot\text{s)} \quad \checkmark$$

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Insight:** The two forms (log inside vs. log-mean outside) must give *identical* numerical answers. If they don't match, you've made a sign or arithmetic error. Always cross-verify using the alternate form in exams to catch mistakes.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mass Transfer Mechanism — Conceptual Flow Architecture

The block diagram below maps the conceptual architecture of diffusion mass transfer, mirroring the structure of a heat transfer problem but with mass transfer-specific components.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["DRIVING FORCE: Concentration Gradient dCA/dx"] --> nodeB["MOLECULAR RANDOM MOTION Brownian Kinetic Theory"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["MOLECULAR DIFFUSION Fick First Law: NA = -DAB dCA/dx"]
    nodeC --> nodeD{"REFERENCE FRAME SELECTION"}
    nodeD -->|Relative to Molar Avg Velocity| nodeE["FUNDAMENTAL FICK LAW: NA* = -DAB grad CA"]
    nodeD -->|Absolute Fixed Coordinates| nodeF["ABSOLUTE FLUX: NA = -DAB dCA/dx + CA/C times N"]
    nodeD -->|Stagnant Medium Gas Liquid Solid| nodeG["SIMPLE FICK LAW: NA = -DAB dCA/dx"]
    nodeE --> nodeH["PROBLEM CLASSIFICATION 3 CANONICAL TYPES"]
    nodeF --> nodeH
    nodeG --> nodeH
    nodeH -->|Type 1: NA = -NB| nodeI["EQUIMOLAR COUNTER DIFFUSION EMCD: NA = DAB CA1-CA2 over L"]
    nodeH -->|Type 2: NB = 0| nodeJ["STEFAN DIFFUSION A THROUGH STAGNANT B: NA = DAB C over L times ln CB2 over CB1"]
    nodeH -->|Type 3: dCA/dt not zero| nodeK["TRANSIENT DIFFUSION FICK SECOND LAW: dCA/dt = DAB d2CA/dx2"]
    nodeI --> nodeL["OUTPUT: MOLAR FLUX NA in kmol per m2 s"]
    nodeJ --> nodeL
    nodeK --> nodeM["OUTPUT: CONCENTRATION PROFILE CA x t"]
    nodeM --> nodeL
    nodeL --> nodeN["ENGINEERING APPLICATION Distillation, Absorption, Drying, Doping"]
```

## 4.2 Stefan Diffusion — Physical Setup Diagram

The schematic below represents the physical setup of A diffusing through stagnant B in a tube — a classic KTU problem configuration.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1["POINT 1: x = 0, CA1 HIGH concentration of A, CB1 LOW Partial pressure PA1"] -->|Bulk flow direction| A2["STAGNANT GAS FILM B Thickness L = 1.5 mm"]
    A2 -->|Bulk flow direction| A3["POINT 2: x = L, CA2 LOW concentration of A, CB2 HIGH Partial pressure PA2"]
    A1 -.->|"Diffusive flux of A: -DAB dCA/dx (negative x direction)"| A3
    A3 -.->|"Diffusive flux of B: -DAB dCB/dx (positive x direction)"| A1
    A2 -.->|"NB equals ZERO: B is non-diffusing on average"| A1
    A2 -.->|"NA is negative: net flux in negative x"| A3
    A1 --> R1["RESULT: Bulk flow carries A in negative x direction, exactly cancelling diffusive flux of B"]
    R1 --> R2["GOVERNING EQUATION: NA = DAB C over L times ln CB2 over CB1"]
```

## 4.3 Diffusivity Determination — Process Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start["DETERMINE BINARY DIFFUSIVITY DAB"] --> phase{"PHASE OF MIXTURE"}
    phase -->|GASEOUS| gas["CHAPMAN ENSKOG EQUATION DAB = 0.001858 T to 3/2 over P sigma AB squared Omega D times sqrt 1 over MA + 1 over MB"]
    phase -->|LIQUID| liq["WILKE CHANG EQUATION DAB = 7.4e-8 T over mu times sqrt psiB MB over LAMBDA A VA to 0.6"]
    phase -->|SOLID| sol["ARRHENIUS TYPE D = D0 exp -Q over RT"]
    gas -->|Inputs| g1["Temperature T, Pressure P, Molecular weights MA MB"]
    gas -->|Inputs| g2["Collision diameter sigma AB from Lennard Jones potential"]
    gas -->|Inputs| g3["Collision integral Omega D tabulated vs T over epsilon AB"]
    g1 --> gout["OUTPUT: DAB in m squared per s, typical 1e-5 to 1e-4 for gases"]
    g2 --> gout
    g3 --> gout
```

## 4.4 Concentration Boundary Layer — Comparison with Heat Transfer

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    h1["HEAT TRANSFER ANALOGUE"] --> h2["Thermal Boundary Layer thickness delta T"]
    h2 --> h3["Fourier Law: q double prime equals -k dT/dx"]
    h3 --> h4["Convective: q equals h times T_s minus T_infinity"]
    m1["MASS TRANSFER ANALOGUE"] --> m2["Concentration Boundary Layer thickness delta C"]
    m2 --> m3["Fick Law: NA equals -DAB dCA/dx"]
    m3 --> m4["Convective: NA equals kc times CA_s minus CA_infinity"]
    h1 <-.->|"Analogy: T replaced by CA"| m1
    h2 <-.->|"Analogy: delta T replaced by delta C"| m2
    h3 <-.->|"Analogy: k replaced by DAB"| m3
    h4 <-.->|"Analogy: h replaced by kc"| m4
    h4 --> rel1["Heat Mass Transfer Analogy: Nu equals Sh, Pr equals Sc"]
    m4 --> rel1
```

> **Reading the Diagrams:** The four flowcharts above form a complete mental map of the KTU Module 4 syllabus. The first captures the theoretical classification; the second illustrates the most-tested physical setup (Stefan diffusion); the third is the calculation tree for diffusivity; the fourth shows the heat-mass transfer analogy used in convective mass transfer analysis.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**State Fick's First Law of Diffusion. Explain the significance of the negative sign.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Fick's First Law of Diffusion states that the molar flux of a species A by molecular diffusion is proportional to the concentration gradient of that species, and occurs in the direction of decreasing concentration. Mathematically:

$$N_A = -D_{AB} \frac{dC_A}{dx}$$

where $N_A$ is the molar flux (kmol/m²·s), $D_{AB}$ is the diffusion coefficient (m²/s), and $dC_A/dx$ is the concentration gradient.

**Significance of the negative sign (1 Mark):** The negative sign ensures that the flux direction is *opposite* to the concentration gradient direction. Since molecules always diffuse from high to low concentration (a spontaneous process dictated by the Second Law of Thermodynamics), $dC_A/dx$ is negative when concentration decreases with x, and the negative sign makes $N_A$ come out positive (in the direction of diffusion). It is a *mathematical* statement of the *physical* irreversibility of the diffusion process.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — June 2023]
**Differentiate between equimolar counter-diffusion and diffusion of A through stagnant B.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Feature | Equimolar Counter-Diffusion (EMCD) | A through Stagnant B (Stefan Diffusion) |
|---------|-----------------------------------|------------------------------------------|
| Bulk flow | Absent (no net molar flow) | Present (induced by A's diffusion) |
| Condition | $N_A = -N_B$ | $N_B = 0$ |
| Flux equation | $N_A = \dfrac{D_{AB}}{L}(C_{A1} - C_{A2})$ | $N_A = \dfrac{D_{AB} \cdot C}{L} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{C_{B2}}{C_{B1}}\right)$ |
| Occurs in | Binary distillation at constant molal overflow | Liquid evaporation into stagnant air; gas absorption |
| Log-mean correction | Not required | Required (driving force is non-linear) |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (1 × 14 = 14 Marks)

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]

**(a)** Derive the expression for the steady-state molar flux of species A diffusing through a stagnant gas film of species B. State all assumptions clearly. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Hydrogen gas (A) is diffusing through a stagnant film of nitrogen gas (B) across a tube of length **2 mm** at **25 °C** and **1.2 atm** total pressure. The mole fraction of $\text{H}_2$ at one end is **0.80** and at the other end is **0.20**. The diffusion coefficient $D_{AB} = 7.65 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$. Calculate the molar flux of $\text{H}_2$. **(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution for Question A:

#### Part (a) — Derivation (7 Marks)

**Assumptions (2 Marks):**
1. Steady-state, one-dimensional diffusion
2. Isothermal, isobaric system
3. Ideal gas behavior ($C = P/RT = $ constant)
4. B is stagnant ($N_B = 0$)
5. $D_{AB}$ is constant over the concentration range
6. No chemical reaction

**Derivation (5 Marks):**

Starting from the absolute flux equation for a binary mixture:

$$N_A = -D_{AB} \frac{dC_A}{dx} + \frac{C_A}{C}(N_A + N_B)$$

Applying $N_B = 0$ (B stagnant):

$$N_A = -D_{AB} \frac{dC_A}{dx} + \frac{C_A}{C} N_A$$

Rearranging:

$$N_A\left(1 - \frac{C_A}{C}\right) = -D_{AB} \frac{dC_A}{dx} \implies N_A \cdot \frac{C_B}{C} = -D_{AB} \frac{dC_A}{dx}$$

For ideal gas: $C = $ const, and $C_B = C - C_A$, so $dC_B = -dC_A$:

$$N_A \, dx = -D_{AB} \cdot C \frac{dC_A}{C - C_A} = D_{AB} \cdot C \frac{dC_B}{C_B}$$

Integrating from $x=0$ ($C_{B1}$) to $x=L$ ($C_{B2}$):

$$N_A \cdot L = D_{AB} \cdot C \ln\!\left(\frac{C_{B2}}{C_{B1}}\right)$$

$$\boxed{N_A = \frac{D_{AB} \cdot C}{L} \ln\!\left(\frac{C_{B2}}{C_{B1}}\right) = \frac{D_{AB} \cdot P}{RTL} \ln\!\left(\frac{P_{B2}}{P_{B1}}\right)}$$

#### Part (b) — Numerical (7 Marks)

**Given Data:**
- $L = 2 \text{ mm} = 2 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$
- $T = 25 + 273 = 298 \text{ K}$
- $P = 1.2 \text{ atm} = 1.2 \times 101325 = 121590 \text{ Pa}$
- $x_{A1} = 0.80$, $x_{A2} = 0.20$
- $D_{AB} = 7.65 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$

**Step 1:** Calculate total molar concentration:

$$C = \frac{P}{RT} = \frac{121590}{8.314 \times 298} = 49.07 \text{ mol/m}^3$$

**Step 2:** Calculate mole fractions of B:

$$x_{B1} = 1 - 0.80 = 0.20, \quad x_{B2} = 1 - 0.20 = 0.80$$

**Step 3:** Apply Stefan equation:

$$N_A = \frac{D_{AB} \cdot C}{L} \ln\!\left(\frac{x_{B2}}{x_{B1}}\right) = \frac{7.65 \times 10^{-5} \times 49.07}{2 \times 10^{-3}} \ln\!\left(\frac{0.80}{0.20}\right)$$

**Step 4:** Numerical evaluation:

$$N_A = (1.877) \times \ln(4) = 1.877 \times 1.3863$$

$$\boxed{N_A = 2.602 \text{ mol/(m}^2\cdot\text{s)} \text{ in the negative x-direction}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Question A(b):** Common mistakes that cost 2-3 marks:
> 1. **Forgetting to convert mm to m** in the length — yields an answer 1000× too large
> 2. **Using the wrong ratio** in the log (students flip $x_{B1}$ and $x_{B2}$, getting negative flux when positive is expected)
> 3. **Using $x_A$ instead of $x_B$** inside the log — wrong by orders of magnitude
> 4. **Forgetting the negative sign** in the final answer — flux direction is part of the answer in KTU
> 5. **Using EMCD formula** ($N_A = D \Delta C/L$) instead of Stefan — underestimates by 11% in this problem
> 
> **Examiner's Tip:** Always write the governing equation symbolically *first*, then substitute. This earns method marks even if arithmetic goes wrong.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam — June 2022]

**(a)** Explain the physical significance of the diffusion coefficient $D_{AB}$. Discuss the factors affecting it and write the Chapman-Enskog equation for estimating gas-phase diffusivity. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A solid slab of thickness **5 mm** with initial uniform concentration of solute $C_0 = 0.10 \text{ kg/m}^3$ is suddenly exposed on both sides to a fluid that maintains the surface concentration at $C_s = 1.00 \text{ kg/m}^3$. The diffusion coefficient inside the solid is $D_{AB} = 4 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$. Using the appropriate transient diffusion solution, calculate the concentration at the mid-plane of the slab (x = 0) after **10 hours**. **(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution for Question B:

#### Part (a) — Theoretical Discussion (7 Marks)

**Physical Significance of $D_{AB}$ (2 Marks):**

The diffusion coefficient $D_{AB}$ is a **transport property** of the binary system A–B that quantifies the *ease* with which species A moves through species B under a unit concentration gradient. Its units (m²/s) are those of a *kinematic* transport coefficient, identical to thermal diffusivity $\alpha$ and kinematic viscosity $\nu$. A higher $D_{AB}$ means faster molecular mixing. Typical magnitudes:
- **Gases:** $10^{-5}$ to $10^{-4}$ m²/s
- **Liquids:** $10^{-9}$ to $10^{-10}$ m²/s (10000× slower than gases)
- **Solids:** $10^{-12}$ to $10^{-14}$ m²/s (extremely slow, requires years)

**Factors Affecting $D_{AB}$ (2 Marks):**

| Factor | Effect on $D_{AB}$ | Reason |
|--------|---------------------|--------|
| Temperature (T) | Increases as $T^{3/2}$ | Higher kinetic energy → faster molecular motion |
| Pressure (P) | Decreases as $1/P$ | Higher P → smaller mean free path |
| Molecular size (σ) | Decreases as $1/\sigma^2$ | Larger molecules collide more often |
| Molecular weight | Higher M → lower D | Slower mean molecular speed |

**Chapman-Enskog Equation (3 Marks):**

$$D_{AB} = \frac{0.001858 \, T^{3/2}}{P \, \sigma_{AB}^2 \, \Omega_D} \sqrt{\frac{1}{M_A} + \frac{1}{M_B}}$$

where:
- $T$ = absolute temperature (K)
- $P$ = pressure (atm, for this constant)
- $\sigma_{AB} = (\sigma_A + \sigma_B)/2$ = characteristic length (Å)
- $\Omega_D$ = dimensionless collision integral (function of $kT/\epsilon_{AB}$)
- $M_A, M_B$ = molecular weights (g/mol)
- $D_{AB}$ is in cm²/s (convert to m²/s by multiplying by $10^{-4}$)

#### Part (b) — Numerical (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- Slab thickness $2L = 5 \text{ mm} \Rightarrow L = 2.5 \text{ mm} = 2.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$ (half-thickness)
- $C_0 = 0.10 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $C_s = 1.00 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- $D_{AB} = 4 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$, $t = 10 \text{ h} = 36000 \text{ s}$

**Step 1 — Recognize the configuration.** This is a transient diffusion problem in a slab of finite thickness with sudden surface concentration change. The exact series solution is:

$$\frac{C_A - C_0}{C_s - C_0} = 1 - \frac{4}{\pi}\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \frac{(-1)^n}{2n+1} \exp\!\left[-\frac{(2n+1)^2 \pi^2 D_{AB} t}{4 L^2}\right] \cos\!\left[\frac{(2n+1)\pi x}{2L}\right]$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate at mid-plane (x = 0):** At $x = 0$, $\cos(0) = 1$:

$$\frac{C_A - C_0}{C_s - C_0} = 1 - \frac{4}{\pi}\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \frac{(-1)^n}{2n+1} \exp\!\left[-\frac{(2n+1)^2 \pi^2 D_{AB} t}{4 L^2}\right]$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Fourier number $Fo$:**

$$Fo = \frac{D_{AB} t}{L^2} = \frac{4 \times 10^{-9} \times 36000}{(2.5 \times 10^{-3})^2} = \frac{1.44 \times 10^{-4}}{6.25 \times 10^{-6}} = 23.04$$

**Step 4 — Truncate series to first term ($n=0$):**

$$\frac{C_A - C_0}{C_s - C_0} \approx 1 - \frac{4}{\pi} \exp\!\left(-\frac{\pi^2}{4} \times 23.04\right) = 1 - 1.2732 \times e^{-56.83}$$

Since $e^{-56.83} \approx 2.7 \times 10^{-25} \approx 0$:

$$\frac{C_A - C_0}{C_s - C_0} \approx 1.000$$

**Step 5 — Calculate $C_A$:**

$$C_A = C_0 + 1.000 \times (C_s - C_0) = 0.10 + 1.00 \times (1.00 - 0.10)$$

$$\boxed{C_A(x=0, t=10\text{h}) = 1.00 \text{ kg/m}^3}$$

**Physical interpretation:** Because $Fo = 23.04 \gg 1$, the slab has reached *steady state* (or extremely close to it). The concentration at the center equals the surface concentration. The transient period is effectively over.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Question B(b):**
> 1. **Slab half-thickness confusion:** The "thickness" given is the *full* slab; the formula uses $L$ as the *half-thickness*. Mixing these up is the #1 reason students get this wrong.
> 2. **Series truncation error:** Some students sum only $n=0$; this is acceptable when $Fo > 0.2$ at the mid-plane. Always state your truncation assumption.
> 3. **Unit conversion:** $Fo$ must be dimensionless. Forgetting to convert mm to m in $L^2$ gives answers off by a factor of $10^6$.
> 4. **Logistics of the answer:** A "well-mixed at center" answer is correct when $Fo$ is large. Don't second-guess your own result.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!TIP]
> **Print this section and keep it as your one-page cheat sheet for Module 4.**

- ☐ **Fick's First Law:** $N_A = -D_{AB} \, dC_A/dx$ — *always* include the negative sign
- ☐ **Fick's Second Law:** $\partial C_A/\partial t = D_{AB} \, \partial^2 C_A/\partial x^2$ — for *transient* problems
- ☐ **EMCD condition:** $N_A = -N_B$ → bulk flow vanishes → use linear driving force $\Delta C$
- ☐ **Stefan condition:** $N_B = 0$ → bulk flow present → use **log-mean** driving force with $\ln(C_{B2}/C_{B1})$
- ☐ **For gases:** convert $C \to P/(RT)$ and $C_B \to (P - P_A)/(RT)$ in all flux equations
- ☐ **Chapman-Enskog:** $D_{AB}$ increases with $T^{3/2}$, decreases with $P$ and $\sigma^2$
- ☐ **Three typical magnitudes:** Gases $\sim 10^{-5}$, Liquids $\sim 10^{-9}$, Solids $\sim 10^{-13}$ m²/s
- ☐ **For transient slab problems:** use *half-thickness* $L$ in the Fourier number, *full thickness* $2L$ in the description
- ☐ **Heat-Mass analogy:** Replace $T \to C_A$, $k \to D_{AB}$, $\alpha \to D_{AB}$, $h \to k_c$, $Nu \to Sh$, $Pr \to Sc$
- ☐ **Sherwood number** $Sh = k_c L / D_{AB}$ is the convective mass transfer analogue of Nusselt number
- ☐ **Always verify** the steady-state assumption by checking if $Fo \gg 1$ (then series truncates to unity)
- ☐ **Common pitfall:** Using arithmetic mean instead of log-mean in Stefan diffusion → errors of 5–15%
- ☐ **Common pitfall:** Forgetting to convert mm to m, atm to Pa, or hours to seconds → catastrophic arithmetic errors
- ☐ **Common pitfall:** Sign convention — state the *direction* of flux explicitly in your answer

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU Exam Strategy:** The KTU Module 4 paper typically contains 1 numerical on Stefan diffusion (7 marks), 1 derivation of Fick's Law or Stefan equation (4–5 marks), 1 conceptual question on diffusivity estimation or heat-mass analogy (3–4 marks), and short notes on Fick's Second Law applications (2–3 marks). Practicing the three worked examples in these notes covers ~80% of the expected paper pattern.

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 MODULE 4: FICK'S LAW OF DIFFUSION

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fick's Law of Diffusion** is the fundamental governing equation of molecular mass transfer, directly analogous to Fourier's Law of heat conduction and Newton's Law of viscosity. It quantifies the rate at which a chemical species migrates through a medium under the influence of a **concentration gradient**.

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!NOTE]
> **Fick's First Law of Diffusion (1855, Adolf Fick):** *The molar flux of a species A by molecular diffusion relative to a fixed coordinate system is proportional to the gradient of its molar concentration, with the proportionality constant being the binary diffusion coefficient.*

Mathematically, for a binary mixture of species **A** and **B**:

$$\vec{J}_A = -D_{AB} \, \nabla C_A$$

Where the negative sign indicates that mass transport occurs from regions of **higher** concentration to regions of **lower** concentration (down the gradient, following the Second Law of Thermodynamics).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Engineering Intuition

Imagine you drop a **single crystal of potassium permanganate (KMnO₄)** into a **stagnant, undisturbed beaker of clear water** at room temperature. There is no stirring, no convection — only the random thermal motion (Brownian motion) of water and dye molecules.

- At **t = 0 seconds:** The purple color is highly localized near the crystal. There is an **enormous concentration gradient**.
- At **t = 10 minutes:** Purple streaks slowly begin to invade the clear water. Mass has moved *down* the concentration gradient.
- At **t = 24 hours:** The beaker is uniformly purple. The gradient is now **zero**, and macroscopic mass transfer ceases.

> [!TIP]
> 🔬 **Engineering Analogy Box:**
> - **Heat Transfer ↔ Mass Transfer:** Fourier's Law ($q'' = -k \, dT/dx$) is structurally identical to Fick's Law. The driving forces (temperature vs. concentration) and transport properties (thermal conductivity $k$ vs. diffusivity $D_{AB}$) play analogous roles.
> - **Real-world example:** Oxygen diffusing through the alveolar membrane into blood capillaries in your lungs, CO₂ escaping from a carbonated drink, moisture migrating through a porous building wall, and semiconductor doping via gaseous diffusion all obey Fick's Law at the molecular scale.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Symbol | Quantity | SI Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $D_{AB}$ | Binary Mass Diffusivity | **m²/s** |
| $C_A$ | Molar Concentration of A | **kmol/m³** |
| $J_A$ | Molar Diffusive Flux (relative to $V_{avg}$) | **kmol/(m²·s)** |
| $N_A$ | Absolute Molar Flux (relative to fixed coordinates) | **kmol/(m²·s)** |
| $R$ | Universal Gas Constant | **8314 J/(kmol·K)** |
| $P$ | Total Absolute Pressure | **Pa (N/m²)** |
| $T$ | Absolute Temperature | **K** |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fick's First Law — Unidirectional Steady Diffusion

For one-dimensional steady-state diffusion along the **x-direction**, Fick's First Law reduces to a scalar ordinary differential equation:

$$J_{A,x} = -D_{AB} \, \frac{dC_A}{dx}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction (Frequently Tested in KTU):**
> - $J_A$ = **Molar flux relative to the molar-average velocity** ($V^*$) of the mixture. It represents *pure diffusion* with no bulk flow.
> - $N_A$ = **Absolute molar flux** relative to stationary coordinates. It includes the **convective (bulk-flow) contribution**.
> - Relation: $N_A = J_A + C_A \cdot V^* = J_A + x_A \cdot N$ (where $N = N_A + N_B$).

## 2.2 The Two Canonical Boundary Scenarios

### Scenario A: Equimolar Counter-Diffusion (EMCD)
This occurs in **equimolar binary systems** such as ideal gas mixtures where species A and B diffuse in **opposite directions at equal molar rates** ($N_A = -N_B$, hence $N = 0$, and bulk flow vanishes).

$$N_A = \frac{D_{AB} \, (C_{A,1} - C_{A,2})}{L}$$

### Scenario B: Diffusion of A Through a Stagnant Film of B
This is the **Zeroth-order stagnant film approximation** ($N_B = 0$, only A moves). It applies to cases like liquid evaporation into still air or gas absorption where B is essentially non-diffusing.

$$N_A = \frac{D_{AB} \, P}{R \, T \, L} \, \ln\!\left(\frac{P - p_{A,2}}{P - p_{A,1}}\right)$$

> [!WARNING]
> In the **stagnant film** case, the driving force is **non-linear** in concentration due to the bulk-flow term. The flux cannot be expressed as a constant $\times$ (linear $\Delta C$). This is a high-frequency trap in KTU numerical problems.

## 2.3 Fick's Second Law — Unsteady (Transient) Diffusion

When the concentration field $C_A(x, y, z, t)$ is **time-dependent**, the governing PDE is:

$$\frac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} = D_{AB} \, \nabla^2 C_A$$

In one-dimensional Cartesian form:

$$\frac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} = D_{AB} \, \frac{\partial^2 C_A}{\partial x^2}$$

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

> [!TIP]
> ⭐ **Master these equations — they account for ~80% of Module 4 marks.**

| # | Governing Equation | Driving Force | Typical Application |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $J_A = -D_{AB} \, dC_A/dx$ | Concentration gradient | General molecular diffusion |
| 2 | $N_A = \frac{D_{AB}}{L} \, (C_{A,1} - C_{A,2})$ | $\Delta C$ (linear) | EMCD in gases |
| 3 | $N_A = \frac{D_{AB}}{L} \, \frac{P}{RT} \, (p_{A,1} - p_{A,2})$ | $\Delta p$ (linear, ideal gas) | EMCD in ideal gases |
| 4 | $N_A = \frac{D_{AB} \, P}{R \, T \, L} \, \ln\!\left(\frac{P - p_{A,2}}{P - p_{A,1}}\right)$ | $\ln$ of partial pressure ratio | Stagnant film diffusion |
| 5 | $\frac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} = D_{AB} \, \frac{\partial^2 C_A}{\partial x^2}$ | Transient gradient | Unsteady diffusion |
| 6 | $N_A = k_c \, (C_{A,1} - C_{A,2})$ | Bulk mass transfer coeff. | Convective mass transfer |
| 7 | $Sh = \frac{k_c \, L}{D_{AB}}$ | Dimensionless | Sherwood number analogy |

**Key Engineering Connections:**

- **Process Engineering:** Design of gas absorption towers, distillation columns, membrane separators, and extractors.
- **Production Engineering:** Controlled-atmosphere heat treatment furnaces (carburizing, nitriding).
- **Refrigeration & HVAC:** Dehumidification of air, refrigerant migration.
- **Biomedical:** Oxygen transport in tissues, drug release from polymer matrices.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation #1 — Equimolar Counter-Diffusion (EMCD) Flux

### Assumptions:
1. Steady state ($\partial C_A/\partial t = 0$).
2. One-dimensional diffusion along the $x$-axis.
3. Constant total molar concentration $C_{total} = C_A + C_B$ = constant (isothermal, isobaric ideal gas or dilute liquid).
4. Constant diffusivity $D_{AB}$ (independent of position).
5. No net bulk flow: $N = N_A + N_B = 0 \Rightarrow N_B = -N_A$.

### Mathematical Derivation:

Starting from Fick's First Law with the bulk-flow correction:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_A &= -D_{AB} \, \frac{dC_A}{dx} + x_A \, (N_A + N_B) \\
N_A &= -D_{AB} \, \frac{dC_A}{dx} + C_A \, \frac{N_A + N_B}{C} \\
N_A - C_A \, \frac{(N_A + N_B)}{C} &= -D_{AB} \, \frac{dC_A}{dx} \\
N_A \left(1 - \frac{C_A}{C}\right) &= -D_{AB} \, \frac{dC_A}{dx} \\
N_A \left(\frac{C - C_A}{C}\right) &= -D_{AB} \, \frac{dC_A}{dx} \\
N_A \left(\frac{C_B}{C}\right) &= -D_{AB} \, \frac{dC_A}{dx}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic Note:** We applied the definition of molar-average velocity $V^* = (N_A + N_B)/C$ to convert $J_A$ (relative flux) to $N_A$ (absolute flux). The factor $C_B/C$ is the **mole fraction of B**, capturing the bulk-flow drag.

For **EMCD**, $N_A + N_B = 0$, so $N_A = -D_{AB} \, dC_A/dx$.

Separating variables and integrating across the slab of thickness $L$ with BCs $C_A = C_{A,1}$ at $x = 0$ and $C_A = C_{A,2}$ at $x = L$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_A \int_0^L dx &= -D_{AB} \int_{C_{A,1}}^{C_{A,2}} dC_A \\
N_A \cdot L &= -D_{AB} \left(C_{A,2} - C_{A,1}\right) \\
N_A \cdot L &= D_{AB} \left(C_{A,1} - C_{A,2}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{N_A = \frac{D_{AB}}{L} \left(C_{A,1} - C_{A,2}\right)}
$$

### Result in terms of Partial Pressures (Ideal Gas Law $C_A = p_A / RT$):

$$
N_A = \frac{D_{AB} \, P}{R \, T \, L} \left(p_{A,1} - p_{A,2}\right)
$$

## 3.2 Derivation #2 — Stagnant Film Diffusion of A Through B

### Assumptions:
1. Steady state, one-dimensional.
2. B is **stationary** ($N_B = 0$). Only A moves.
3. Constant total pressure $P$ and temperature $T$ (ideal gas).
4. Constant $D_{AB}$.

### Derivation:

Since $N_B = 0$, the governing equation becomes:

$$
N_A \left(\frac{C_B}{C}\right) = -D_{AB} \, \frac{dC_A}{dx}
$$

With $C = P/RT$ constant and $C_B = C - C_A$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_A \left(1 - \frac{C_A}{C}\right) &= -D_{AB} \, \frac{dC_A}{dx} \\
N_A \left(\frac{P - p_A}{P}\right) &= -\frac{D_{AB}}{RT} \, \frac{dp_A}{dx}
\end{aligned}
$$

Separating variables:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_A \, dx &= -\frac{D_{AB} \, P}{RT} \, \frac{dp_A}{P - p_A} \\
N_A \int_0^L dx &= -\frac{D_{AB} \, P}{RT} \int_{p_{A,1}}^{p_{A,2}} \frac{dp_A}{P - p_A}
\end{aligned}
$$

The right integral yields a logarithm:

$$
N_A \cdot L = \frac{D_{AB} \, P}{RT} \left[ \ln(P - p_{A,1}) - \ln(P - p_{A,2}) \right]
$$

$$
\boxed{N_A = \frac{D_{AB} \, P}{R \, T \, L} \, \ln\!\left(\frac{P - p_{A,2}}{P - p_{A,1}}\right)}
$$

> **Logic Note:** The minus sign flipped the bounds, producing the positive logarithm. This equation is the **workhorse** of gas absorption, humidification, and liquid evaporation problems.

## 3.3 Derivation #3 — Fick's Second Law from First Principles

### Conservation of Mass in a Control Volume (Reynolds Transport Theorem):

For species A in a fixed control volume with no chemical reaction:

$$
\frac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} = -\nabla \cdot \vec{N}_A
$$

Substituting the absolute flux expression (assuming constant total flux for now, $N = $ const.):

$$
\vec{N}_A = -D_{AB} \, \nabla C_A + C_A \, \vec{V}^*
$$

Assuming the convective contribution is negligible (pure diffusion, $\vec{V}^* = 0$):

$$
\frac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} = -\nabla \cdot \left(-D_{AB} \, \nabla C_A\right) = \nabla \cdot \left(D_{AB} \, \nabla C_A\right)
$$

If $D_{AB}$ is **constant** (does not vary with position or composition):

$$
\boxed{\frac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} = D_{AB} \, \nabla^2 C_A = D_{AB} \left(\frac{\partial^2 C_A}{\partial x^2} + \frac{\partial^2 C_A}{\partial y^2} + \frac{\partial^2 C_A}{\partial z^2}\right)}
$$

In 1-D:

$$
\frac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} = D_{AB} \, \frac{\partial^2 C_A}{\partial x^2}
$$

### Worked Example: Steady Diffusion Across a Stagnant Gas Film

**Problem:** Ammonia (A) is absorbed from a gas mixture into water. The gas, at total pressure 101.325 kPa and 25 °C, contains 20 mol% NH₃ at the gas-bulk side ($x = 0$). At the gas-liquid interface ($x = 2$ mm), the partial pressure of NH₃ is 5 kPa (equilibrium with the very dilute aqueous solution). Air (B) is insoluble in water and remains stagnant. Given $D_{NH_3-air} = 2.30 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s, calculate the molar flux of NH₃.

**Solution:**

Given data:
- $P = 101.325$ kPa, $T = 298.15$ K, $L = 0.002$ m
- $p_{A,1} = 0.20 \times 101.325 = 20.265$ kPa
- $p_{A,2} = 5.0$ kPa
- $D_{AB} = 2.30 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s

Stagnant-film equation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_A &= \frac{(2.30 \times 10^{-5}) \times (101325)}{8.314 \times 298.15 \times 0.002} \ln\!\left(\frac{101325 - 5000}{101325 - 20265}\right) \\
N_A &= \frac{2.3305}{4.9574} \ln\!\left(\frac{96325}{81060}\right) \\
N_A &= 0.4701 \times \ln(1.1884) \\
N_A &= 0.4701 \times 0.1727 \\
N_A &= 0.0812 \, \text{kmol/(m}^2\text{·s)}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{N_A \approx 8.12 \times 10^{-2} \, \text{kmol/(m}^2\text{·s)}}
$$

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note:** Fick's Law concerns **molecular-scale** physical processes. Since detailed molecular trajectory drawings are not feasible in Mermaid, the following **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** maps the conceptual and computational workflow of diffusion analysis, mirroring the Fick's Law decision tree used in KTU numerical problems.

## 4.1 Master Flowchart — Diffusion Problem Solver

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Start: Diffusion Problem Statement]) --> Identify{Identify Geometry and BCs}
    Identify --> OneD[1D Planar Slab x direction]
    Identify --> Radial[Radial Cylindrical]
    Identify --> Sphere[Spherical Geometry]
    OneD --> CheckSteady{Steady State or Transient}
    Radial --> CheckSteady
    Sphere --> CheckSteady
    CheckSteady --> Steady[Steady State dC dt equals 0]
    CheckSteady --> Transient[Unsteady Use Ficks Second Law]
    Steady --> CheckFlow{Is Net Bulk Flow Zero}
    CheckFlow --> ZeroFlow[EMCD N A plus N B equals 0]
    CheckFlow --> NonZeroFlow[Bulk Flow Exists]
    ZeroFlow --> EMCD[Apply N A equals DAB by L times C A1 minus C A2]
    NonZeroFlow --> Stagnant{Is N B exactly zero}
    Stagnant --> Yes[Stagnant Film B]
    Stagnant --> No[General Convective Case]
    Yes --> StagEq[Apply Logarithmic Stagnant Film Equation]
    No --> ConvEq[Apply kc times Delta C Formulation]
    EMCD --> ComputeFlux[Compute Molar Flux N A]
    StagEq --> ComputeFlux
    ConvEq --> ComputeFlux
    ComputeFlux --> Units[Check Units kmol per m2 per s]
    Units --> Done([Answer Validated])
    Transient --> Ficks2nd[Apply Ficks Second Law PDE]
    Ficks2nd --> BCApply[Apply IC and BCs]
    BCApply --> SolvePDE[Solve via Separation of Variables]
    SolvePDE --> Series[Obtain Infinite Series Solution]
    Series --> TimeEval[Evaluate at Specified Time t]
    TimeEval --> Done
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 📝 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]** State Fick's First Law of Diffusion. What is the physical significance of the negative sign in the equation?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> [!NOTE]
> **Fick's First Law** states that the molar diffusive flux of a species A in a binary mixture is directly proportional to the concentration gradient and the binary diffusion coefficient: $J_A = -D_{AB} \, (dC_A/dx)$.
>
> **Physical Significance of the negative sign:** It indicates that mass transfer occurs from a region of **higher** concentration to a region of **lower** concentration. In other words, the flux vector points in the direction of **decreasing** concentration, which is consistent with the natural tendency of systems to move toward equilibrium (Second Law of Thermodynamics). (1 mark — definition; 1 mark — equation; 1 mark — physical significance.)

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]** Distinguish between equimolar counter-diffusion and diffusion of A through stagnant B.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> [!NOTE]
> - **EMCD:** $N_A = -N_B$, hence the total molar flux $N = 0$, bulk flow is absent, and the flux is linearly proportional to the concentration difference: $N_A = (D_{AB}/L)(C_{A,1} - C_{A,2})$. (1.5 marks)
> - **Stagnant B:** $N_B = 0$, but $N_A \neq 0$. Bulk flow is present (in the same direction as A's diffusion) and **augments** the flux. The flux is proportional to the **logarithm** of the partial pressure ratio: $N_A = (D_{AB}P/RTL)\ln[(P-p_{A,2})/(P-p_{A,1})]$. (1.5 marks)

---

## 📝 Part B — Long Answer Questions (Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 Adapted]

**(a)** Starting from Fick's First Law, derive the steady-state molar flux equation for **diffusion of A through stagnant B** in a gas mixture. State all assumptions clearly. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A mixture of O₂ and N₂ is contained in a tube of length 0.1 m at 1.013 bar and 25 °C. At one end, the mole fraction of O₂ is 0.4; at the other end, it is 0.1. If N₂ is stagnant, calculate the steady-state molar flux of O₂. Given: $D_{O_2-N_2} = 1.89 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Question A(a) — 7 Marks**

**Assumptions (1 mark):**
1. Steady state, 1-D diffusion along x.
2. Stagnant B → $N_B = 0$.
3. Ideal gas behavior, constant T and P.
4. Constant $D_{AB}$.

**Derivation (6 marks):** The detailed step-by-step derivation was provided in **Section 3.2** above. The final governing equation is:

$$
N_A = \frac{D_{AB} \, P}{R \, T \, L} \, \ln\!\left(\frac{P - p_{A,2}}{P - p_{A,1}}\right)
$$

---

#### **Solution to Question A(b) — 7 Marks**

**Given Data:**
- $P = 1.013 \times 10^5$ Pa, $T = 298.15$ K
- $L = 0.1$ m
- $x_{A,1} = 0.4 \Rightarrow p_{A,1} = 0.4 \times 101300 = 40520$ Pa
- $x_{A,2} = 0.1 \Rightarrow p_{A,2} = 0.1 \times 101300 = 10130$ Pa
- $D_{AB} = 1.89 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s

**Calculation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_A &= \frac{(1.89 \times 10^{-5})(1.013 \times 10^5)}{(8.314)(298.15)(0.1)} \ln\!\left(\frac{101300 - 10130}{101300 - 40520}\right) \\
N_A &= \frac{1.9146}{247.87} \ln\!\left(\frac{91170}{60780}\right) \\
N_A &= 7.724 \times 10^{-3} \times \ln(1.5000) \\
N_A &= 7.724 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.4055 \\
N_A &= 3.13 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{kmol/(m}^2\text{·s)}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{N_A \approx 3.13 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{kmol/(m}^2\text{·s)}}
$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam — June 2023 Adapted]

**(a)** Derive Fick's Second Law of Diffusion in one-dimensional Cartesian coordinates, clearly mentioning the continuity equation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A slab of solid polymer initially contains no penetrant A. At $t = 0$, the surface concentration is suddenly raised to $C_{A,s}$ and held constant. The slab is of thickness $L$ and is considered semi-infinite if $L$ is large. Obtain the concentration profile $C_A(x, t)$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Question B(a) — 7 Marks**

**Continuity Equation (species A, no reaction):**

$$
\frac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot \vec{N}_A = 0
$$

**Absolute flux for pure diffusion:** $\vec{N}_A = -D_{AB} \nabla C_A$ (bulk flow neglected).

**Substitution:** With constant $D_{AB}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} &= -\nabla \cdot (-D_{AB} \nabla C_A) \\
\frac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} &= D_{AB} \nabla^2 C_A
\end{aligned}
$$

**In 1-D Cartesian:**

$$
\boxed{\frac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} = D_{AB} \, \frac{\partial^2 C_A}{\partial x^2}}
$$

(Marks: 2 for continuity, 3 for substitution, 2 for final PDE.)

---

#### **Solution to Question B(b) — 7 Marks**

**Boundary and Initial Conditions:**
1. $C_A(x, 0) = 0$ for $x > 0$
2. $C_A(0, t) = C_{A,s}$ for $t \geq 0$
3. $C_A(\infty, t) = 0$ (semi-infinite)

**Solution via similarity variable** $\eta = x / (2\sqrt{D_{AB} \, t})$:

$$
\frac{C_A(x, t) - C_{A,s}}{0 - C_{A,s}} = \text{erf}\!\left(\frac{x}{2\sqrt{D_{AB} \, t}}\right)
$$

$$
\boxed{\frac{C_A(x, t)}{C_{A,s}} = 1 - \text{erf}\!\left(\frac{x}{2\sqrt{D_{AB} \, t}}\right) = \text{erfc}\!\left(\frac{x}{2\sqrt{D_{AB} \, t}}\right)}
$$

**Surface flux (Fick's First Law at x = 0):**

$$
N_A(0, t) = \frac{D_{AB} \, C_{A,s}}{\sqrt{\pi \, D_{AB} \, t}} = C_{A,s} \sqrt{\frac{D_{AB}}{\pi \, t}}
$$

(Marks: 1 for BCs, 4 for PDE solution, 2 for surface flux expression.)

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 3 Mark-Deduction Traps in Module 4:**
> 1. **Stagnant vs. EMCD confusion (–2 to –3 marks):** Students routinely apply the linear $\Delta C$ equation when the problem specifies stagnant B. Always check: *"Is bulk flow present?"* If yes, use the logarithmic form.
> 2. **Forgetting the bulk-flow correction (–1 to –2 marks):** Writing $N_A = -D_{AB} \, dC_A/dx$ instead of $N_A = -D_{AB} \, dC_A/dx + x_A N$ is incomplete. The "absolute" flux always contains the convective term.
> 3. **Unit inconsistency (–1 mark):** Mixing SI (kmol/m³) with CGS (gmol/L) within a single calculation. Stick to **kmol, m, s, K, Pa** throughout.
> 4. **Sign error in logarithm (–1 mark):** The argument of the ln must be **greater than 1**, i.e., $p_{A,1} > p_{A,2}$ at $x = 0$. If your ln argument is less than 1, the boundary labeling is reversed.

---

## 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Fick's Law of Diffusion**

- ✅ Fick's **First Law** (steady, isothermal): $J_A = -D_{AB} \, dC_A/dx$ — flux is proportional to concentration gradient.
- ✅ Fick's **Second Law** (unsteady): $\partial C_A/\partial t = D_{AB} \, \nabla^2 C_A$ — diffusion equation, parabolic PDE.
- ✅ Two boundary archetypes: **EMCD** (linear, $N=0$) and **Stagnant B** (logarithmic, $N_B=0$).
- ✅ Binary diffusivity $D_{AB}$ has units of **m²/s**; depends strongly on $T$ and $P$ (gas: $D_{AB} \propto T^{1.75}/P$).
- ✅ Analogies: $D_{AB} \leftrightarrow k$ (heat), $D_{AB} \leftrightarrow \nu$ (momentum) — basis of the **Reynolds, Prandtl, Schmidt** analogies.
- ✅ Mass transfer coefficient $k_c$ (m/s) bridges the gap between interface concentration and bulk concentration: $N_A = k_c (C_{A,s} - C_{A,\infty})$.
- ✅ Sherwood number: $Sh = k_c L / D_{AB}$ — the mass-transfer analog of the Nusselt number.
- ✅ Common assumptions: ideal gas, constant $D_{AB}$, isothermal, isobaric, no chemical reaction.
- ✅ Solution methods: (i) Direct integration for steady 1-D; (ii) Separation of variables + Fourier series for transient; (iii) Similarity variable + error function for semi-infinite media.
- ✅ KTU-favored topics: numerical flux calculations, analogy with heat transfer, transient slab problems, and the physical interpretation of diffusion coefficients.

---

topics.title: Equimolar Diffusion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET754 - HEAT AND MASS TRANSFER
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Equimolar Diffusion

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Equimolar Counter-Diffusion (EMD)** is a special case of molecular diffusion in which two ideal-gas species **A** and **B** diffuse through one another in *opposite directions at equal and opposite molar fluxes*, i.e.,

$$N_A = -N_B$$

The total molar concentration $C_T = C_A + C_B = \dfrac{P}{RT}$ remains spatially invariant along the diffusion path because the net molar flux $(N_A + N_B)$ is identically zero. This condition is rigorously satisfied in processes such as **rigorous McCabe–Thiele binary distillation** at total reflux and **equimolar gas-phase counter-flow** in a Stefan tube where the inert constraint is removed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mandate:** Equimolar diffusion is a *CO-2 / Apply level* topic under Module 4 (Mass Transfer). You must be able to (a) derive the working flux equation from Fick's Law, and (b) compute the steady-state molar flux for binary gas pairs given partial pressures and film thickness.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine two parallel conveyor belts in a warehouse, moving in **exactly opposite directions** at the **same speed**. Each box leaving on belt A is matched by one box arriving on belt B. From an observer standing at the midpoint, the *crowd density never changes* — the total number of people in the room is constant.

In the diffusion context:
- **Belt A** = molecules of species **A** diffusing upward (say, from liquid to gas in a reboiler).
- **Belt B** = molecules of species **B** diffusing downward (condensing vapour).
- **Constant crowd** = constant total molar concentration $C_T$.
- **Same speed, opposite direction** = $N_A = -N_B$.

The textbook scenario: in a binary distillation column operating at **total reflux**, every mole of the more-volatile component vaporising at the reboiler is matched by a mole of the less-volatile component condensing at the condenser. There is no net molar transfer through any cross-section of the column — only **inter-diffusion**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Distinguish from "Stagnant Film" Diffusion:**
> In the *stagnant B* case (e.g., absorption of A into a liquid with non-diffusing B), $N_B = 0$ and the flux equation acquires an extra bulk-flow term $\dfrac{P}{P - P_A}$.
> In *equimolar counter-diffusion*, that bulk-flow term **vanishes**, giving the simplest possible form of Fick's Law.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Symbol | Quantity | SI Units |
|:------:|:--------|:--------|
| $D_{AB}$ | Binary diffusivity of A in B | **m²/s** |
| $P$ | Total absolute pressure | **Pa** |
| $R$ | Universal gas constant | **8.314 J/(mol·K)** |
| $T$ | Absolute temperature | **K** |
| $C_T$ | Total molar concentration | **kmol/m³** |
| $N_A$ | Molar flux of A | **kmol/(m²·s)** |
| $z$ | Diffusion path length (film thickness) | **m** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Logical Breakdown — From Fick's First Law to Equimolar Form

**Step 1 — Fick's First Law for a binary system (general form).**
For a binary mixture of **A** and **B**, the molar flux of A combines a molecular-diffusion term and a bulk-convection (bulk-flow) term:

$$N_A = -D_{AB}\dfrac{dC_A}{dz} + x_A(N_A + N_B)$$

where $x_A = C_A / C_T$ is the mole fraction of A. This is the **fundamental working equation** of mass transfer.

**Step 2 — Why the second term exists.**
Whenever there is a *net* molar flux $(N_A + N_B) \neq 0$, the entire fluid drifts in the z-direction, dragging molecules with it. This bulk motion augments pure molecular diffusion.

**Step 3 — Apply the equimolar constraint.**
The defining condition of equimolar counter-diffusion is:

$$N_A = -N_B \quad \Rightarrow \quad N_A + N_B = 0$$

Substituting into the general equation:

$$N_A = -D_{AB}\dfrac{dC_A}{dz} + x_A(0) \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{N_A = -D_{AB}\dfrac{dC_A}{dz}}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to mole-fraction form for ideal gases.**
For an ideal gas at constant T and P: $C_A = \dfrac{P_A}{RT} = \dfrac{y_A P}{RT}$ and $C_T = \dfrac{P}{RT}$. Therefore:

$$\dfrac{dC_A}{dz} = \dfrac{P}{RT}\dfrac{dy_A}{dz} = C_T \dfrac{dy_A}{dz}$$

Hence:

$$N_A = -D_{AB}\,C_T\,\dfrac{dy_A}{dz}$$

**Step 5 — Integrate across a film of thickness $z$** with boundary conditions $y_A = y_{A1}$ at $z=0$ and $y_A = y_{A2}$ at $z=z$:

$$N_A = \dfrac{D_{AB}\,P}{RT\,z}\,(y_{A1} - y_{A2}) \;=\; \dfrac{D_{AB}}{RT\,z}\,(P_{A1} - P_{A2})$$

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Every Step

- **Why $C_T$ is constant:** because $C_A + C_B = C_T$ (mass conservation) and the molar fluxes are exactly equal and opposite.
- **Why no bulk-flow correction:** in equimolar diffusion, bulk flow cancels out by symmetry.
- **Why it's called *counter*-diffusion:** species A moves in +z while species B moves in −z.
- **Why distillation at total reflux is the canonical example:** the molting of low boiler at reboiler = condensation of high boiler at condenser → $N_A = -N_B$ rigorously.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Memorize the four boxed relations below — they are the KTU board-exam workhorses for this topic.**

| # | Equation | Applicable Form | Comments |
|:-:|:---------|:----------------|:---------|
| 1 | $N_A = -D_{AB}\dfrac{dC_A}{dz}$ | Concentration form | Simplest Fick's law form |
| 2 | $N_A = -D_{AB}\,C_T\,\dfrac{dy_A}{dz}$ | Mole-fraction form (ideal gas) | Use when $P,T$ given |
| 3 | $N_A = \dfrac{D_{AB}\,P}{RT\,z}(y_{A1} - y_{A2})$ | Integrated molar flux (gas) | Steady-state, constant $P,T$ |
| 4 | $N_A = \dfrac{D_{AB}}{RT\,z}(P_{A1} - P_{A2})$ | Partial-pressure form | Direct from $P_A = y_A P$ |
| 5 | $N_A = -N_B$ | Definition | Equimolar constraint |
| 6 | $C_T = \dfrac{P}{RT}$ | Ideal-gas relation | Constant under isothermal, isobaric conditions |
| 7 | $x_A + x_B = 1$ | Mole-fraction closure | Always true for binary |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Condition Convention (KTU-Standard):** Subscript "1" denotes the *bulk-gas / high-concentration* side (typically $z=0$); subscript "2" denotes the *interface / low-concentration* side ($z=z$). The flux is positive (i.e., A moves from 1 → 2) **iff** $y_{A1} > y_{A2}$.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Binary Distillation Columns** (at total reflux): rigorous McCabe–Thiele analysis assumes equimolar counter-diffusion between vapor and liquid phases on each tray.
- **Counter-current Gas Exchangers:** design of membrane separators where the membrane is permeable to A but not B (often modeled as the limit of equimolar diffusion on the permeate side).
- **Catalytic Surface Reactions** with stoichiometric A → B: at steady state, every mole of A consumed generates a mole of B, enforcing the equimolar constraint in the boundary layer.
- **Reservoir Engineering / Petroleum:** modeling of gas-oil counter-diffusion in retrograde condensate reservoirs and lean-gas injection EOR processes.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Integrated Flux Equation

**Starting Point — Fick's First Law for Binary Mixture (General Form):**

$$N_A = -D_{AB}\,\dfrac{dC_A}{dz} + x_A(N_A + N_B) \quad \text{...(i)}$$

**Step 1 — Apply the equimolar counter-diffusion condition.**

By definition of equimolar counter-diffusion:

$$N_A = -N_B \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; N_A + N_B = 0$$

**Step 2 — Substitute into (i).**

$$N_A = -D_{AB}\,\dfrac{dC_A}{dz} + x_A \cdot (0)$$

$$\therefore \quad N_A = -D_{AB}\,\dfrac{dC_A}{dz} \quad \text{...(ii)}$$

**Step 3 — Express concentration in terms of partial pressure (ideal gas).**

For an ideal-gas mixture at temperature $T$:

$$C_A = \dfrac{P_A}{RT} = \dfrac{y_A P}{RT}$$

Differentiating with respect to $z$ (with $P$ and $T$ constant):

$$\dfrac{dC_A}{dz} = \dfrac{1}{RT}\dfrac{dP_A}{dz} = \dfrac{P}{RT}\dfrac{dy_A}{dz} \quad \text{...(iii)}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute (iii) into (ii).**

$$N_A = -D_{AB}\left(\dfrac{P}{RT}\right)\dfrac{dy_A}{dz} \quad \text{...(iv)}$$

**Step 5 — Recognize that $N_A$ is constant at steady state.**

At steady-state, the molar flux $N_A$ is *independent of $z*$. Therefore we may separate variables and integrate from $z = 0$ (where $y_A = y_{A1}$) to $z = z$ (where $y_A = y_{A2}$):

$$\int_{0}^{z} N_A\,dz = -\dfrac{D_{AB}\,P}{RT}\int_{y_{A1}}^{y_{A2}} dy_A$$

**Step 6 — Evaluate both integrals.**

$$N_A \cdot z = -\dfrac{D_{AB}\,P}{RT}\left[y_A\right]_{y_{A1}}^{y_{A2}}$$

$$N_A \cdot z = -\dfrac{D_{AB}\,P}{RT}\left(y_{A2} - y_{A1}\right)$$

**Step 7 — Rearrange for $N_A$.**

$$\boxed{N_A = \dfrac{D_{AB}\,P}{RT\,z}\left(y_{A1} - y_{A2}\right)} \quad \text{...(v)}$$

Equivalently, using $P_{A} = y_A P$:

$$\boxed{N_A = \dfrac{D_{AB}}{RT\,z}\left(P_{A1} - P_{A2}\right)} \quad \text{...(vi)}$$

Equivalently, in concentration units:

$$\boxed{N_A = \dfrac{D_{AB}}{z}\left(C_{A1} - C_{A2}\right)} \quad \text{...(vii)}$$

**Step 8 — By symmetry, the flux of B.**

$$\boxed{N_B = \dfrac{D_{AB}\,P}{RT\,z}\left(y_{B1} - y_{B2}\right) = -N_A}$$

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Board Style

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Pattern]**
> Ammonia (A) is being absorbed from a **non-diffusing inert gas mixture** into water in a wetted-wall column, while simultaneously nitrogen (B) is being stripped from the liquid. The system operates under equimolar counter-diffusion at steady state. Data:
> - $P = 1.013 \times 10^5$ Pa ;  $T = 298$ K
> - $D_{AB} = 2.30 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s
> - Film thickness $z = 0.002$ m
> - $y_{A1} = 0.20$ (bulk gas side); $y_{A2} = 0.05$ (interface side)
>
> **Find:** (a) Molar flux $N_A$ of ammonia; (b) Confirm $N_A = -N_B$.

**Solution.**

**Part (a) — Compute $N_A$.**

Using Eq. (v):

$$N_A = \dfrac{D_{AB}\,P}{RT\,z}\left(y_{A1} - y_{A2}\right)$$

Plug in values step-by-step:

$$N_A = \dfrac{(2.30 \times 10^{-5})(1.013 \times 10^5)}{(8.314)(298)(0.002)}\,(0.20 - 0.05)$$

**Numerator of coefficient:**

$$(2.30 \times 10^{-5})(1.013 \times 10^5) = 2.30 \times 1.013 = 2.3299$$

**Denominator of coefficient:**

$$(8.314)(298)(0.002) = 8.314 \times 0.596 = 4.9551$$

**Driving force:**

$$(0.20 - 0.05) = 0.15$$

**Combine:**

$$N_A = \dfrac{2.3299}{4.9551} \times 0.15$$

$$N_A = 0.4702 \times 0.15$$

$$\boxed{N_A = 7.053 \times 10^{-2} \;\text{kmol/(m}^2\cdot\text{s)}}$$

**Part (b) — Compute $N_B$ and verify sign.**

Mole fractions of B at the two boundaries (binary closure $y_A + y_B = 1$):

$$y_{B1} = 1 - 0.20 = 0.80 \quad ; \quad y_{B2} = 1 - 0.05 = 0.95$$

$$N_B = \dfrac{D_{AB}\,P}{RT\,z}\left(y_{B1} - y_{B2}\right)$$

$$N_B = \dfrac{(2.30 \times 10^{-5})(1.013 \times 10^5)}{(8.314)(298)(0.002)}\,(0.80 - 0.95)$$

$$N_B = 0.4702 \times (-0.15)$$

$$\boxed{N_B = -7.053 \times 10^{-2} \;\text{kmol/(m}^2\cdot\text{s)}}$$

Indeed, $N_B = -N_A$, confirming equimolar counter-diffusion. **The negative sign on $N_B$ denotes flux in the −z direction (opposite to A).**

### 3.3 Comparison Matrix — Equimolar vs. Stagnant-Film Diffusion

| Property | Equimolar Counter-Diffusion | Stagnant-Film (B non-diffusing) |
|:---------|:---------------------------|:--------------------------------|
| Constraint | $N_A = -N_B$ | $N_B = 0$ |
| Bulk-flow term | Absent | Present: $\dfrac{P}{P - P_A}$ |
| Flux equation | $N_A = \dfrac{D_{AB} P}{RTz}(y_{A1} - y_{A2})$ | $N_A = \dfrac{D_{AB} P}{RTz}\dfrac{(P_{A1}-P_{A2})}{P_{BM}}$ |
| Total concentration | $C_T$ constant | $C_T$ increases toward the interface |
| Canonical example | Distillation at total reflux | Gas absorption with inert carrier |
| KTU difficulty | Low–Moderate | Moderate–High |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Diffusion Geometry & Boundary Conditions

```mermaid
graph LR

    B1[STAGNANT GAS FILM - thickness z] -->|y_A2, P_A2| A2[INTERFACE - z=z]
    A2 -->|Condensing B / Vaporizing A| C1[BULK LIQUID SIDE]
    A3[SPECIES A diffuses +z direction]:::aArrow --> B1
    A4[SPECIES B diffuses -z direction]:::bArrow --> B1
    A5[N_A = -N_B, oppmolar]:::constraint --> B1

    classDef aArrow fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,color:#000
    classDef bArrow fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24,color:#000

```

```mermaid
graph TD
    P1[Concentration Profile in Film Thickness z] --> P2["At z=0: C_A = C_A1 (high), C_B = C_B1 (low)"]
    P1 --> P3[Linear Variation Across Film]
    P1 --> P4["At z=z: C_A = C_A2 (low), C_B = C_B2 (high)"]
    P2 --> P5[C_A decreases linearly with z]
    P4 --> P6[C_B increases linearly with z]

    P6 --> P8["Slope = -(N_B) / D_AB = (N_A) / D_AB"]

    class P4 lo
```

### 4.3 Process-Level Block Diagram (Distillation Analogy)

```mermaid
graph LR
    REBOILER[REBOILER: Vaporises A] -->|Vapor A + Liquid B| TRAY1[Tray 1]
    TRAY1 -->|Counter-diffusion z direction| TRAY2[Tray 2]
    TRAY2 --> TRAYn[Tray n]
    TRAYn -->|Vapor condenses A| CONDENSER[CONDENSER: Condenses A]
    CONDENSER -->|Reflux A back| TRAYn
    REBOILER -.->|N_A upward| TRAY1
    CONDENSER -.->|N_B downward| TRAYn
    NOTE[Total Reflux ⇒ N_A = -N_B ⇒ Equimolar Counter-diffusion]:::note -.-> TRAY1
```

### 4.4 Flux-Generation Logic Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    S1[Fick First Law General N_A = -D_AB dC_A/dz + x_A N_A+N_B] --> S2{Apply constraint}
    S2 -->|N_A = -N_B| S3[N_A = -D_AB dC_A/dz]
    S2 -->|N_B = 0| S4[N_A = -D_AB C_T dy_A dz / 1-y_A]
    S3 --> S5[Substitute C_A = P_A/RT]
    S5 --> S6[Integrate over film z]
    S6 --> S7["Final N_A = D_AB P / RTz (y_A1 - y_A2)"]
    S7 --> S8[Numerically evaluate]
```
